nodes.label: Aging
nodes.description: Age-related decline in cellular repair mechanisms
nodes.mechanism: Reduces ESCRT repair efficiency, impairs proteostasis, increases oxidative stress, decreases glymphatic function ~40%

nodes.label: APOE4
nodes.description: APOE ε4 allele - strongest genetic risk factor for late-onset AD
nodes.mechanism: Impairs lipid transport, lysosomal function, cholesterol homeostasis, and pericyte survival

nodes.label: TREM2 Variants
nodes.description: Loss-of-function TREM2 variants (R47H, R62H) increase AD risk 2-4x
nodes.mechanism: TREM2 is a microglial lipid sensor. Variants impair microglial phagocytosis and response to damage signals.

nodes.label: Sleep Disruption
nodes.description: Chronic sleep deprivation or poor sleep quality
nodes.mechanism: Glymphatic clearance increases 60% during sleep (Xie 2013). Sleep disruption reduces Aβ clearance and causes AQP4 depolarization.

nodes.label: TBI
nodes.description: Traumatic brain injury as AD risk factor
nodes.mechanism: Causes acute BBB disruption, pericyte loss, iron deposition from microhemorrhages, and chronic neuroinflammation

nodes.label: Metabolic Syndrome
nodes.description: Insulin resistance, obesity, T2DM as AD risk factors
nodes.mechanism: Insulin resistance impairs IDE availability for Aβ clearance. Hyperglycemia damages pericytes. Obesity drives chronic inflammation.

nodes.label: Lysosome
nodes.description: Central degradation organelle responsible for autophagy and clearance
nodes.mechanism: Maintains pH ~4.5-5 via V-ATPase. Contains hydrolases for degradation. Hub of the PLIG framework.

nodes.label: Lysosomal pH
nodes.description: Acidic pH (~4.5-5) required for hydrolase enzyme function
nodes.mechanism: V-ATPase pumps protons to maintain acidity. pH elevation inactivates cathepsins, GBA, and other enzymes. Master switch for multiple downstream pathways.

nodes.label: V-ATPase
nodes.description: Vacuolar ATPase proton pump that acidifies lysosomes
nodes.mechanism: Multi-subunit enzyme complex. Estrogen upregulates expression. ORF3a disrupts function. Target of C381.

nodes.label: Membrane Integrity
nodes.description: Intact lysosomal membrane containing hydrolases and iron
nodes.mechanism: Membrane keeps cathepsins, iron, and other contents contained. Breach causes lysosomal membrane permeabilization (LMP).

nodes.label: LMP
nodes.description: Lysosomal membrane permeabilization - contents leak into cytosol
nodes.mechanism: Releases cathepsins B/D/L, iron, and ROS into cytosol. Can be partial (repairable via ESCRT) or full (cell death).

nodes.label: ESCRT Repair
nodes.description: ESCRT-III membrane repair machinery that seals lysosomal damage
nodes.mechanism: ESCRT-III, ALIX, TSG101 recruited to damaged membranes. Efficiency declines with age - key reason animal models mislead.

nodes.label: Cathepsin B (Cytosolic)
nodes.description: Cytosolic cathepsin B danger signal; NLRP3 activator after lysosomal membrane breach
nodes.mechanism: Cathepsins B, D, L activate apoptosis and inflammasome when in cytosol. Triggers NLRP3 activation.

nodes.label: Cathepsin Activity
nodes.description: Cathepsin B and D enzymatic activity within functional lysosomes
nodes.mechanism: pH-dependent proteases that degrade Aβ, tau, and other substrates. Cathepsin B cleaves Aβ. Active only at pH < 5.

nodes.label: Lysosomal Biogenesis
nodes.description: TFEB-mediated creation of new lysosomes
nodes.mechanism: TFEB translocates to nucleus and activates CLEAR gene network. Creates NEW functional lysosomes rather than repairing damaged ones.

nodes.label: Lysosomal Dysfunction
nodes.description: REVERSIBLE impairment: pH elevation, enzyme deficiency
nodes.mechanism: Can be repaired by V-ATPase restoration (C381) or new lysosome creation (sulforaphane). What animal models typically show.
nodes.notes: REVERSIBLE - key distinction from damage

nodes.label: Lysosomal Damage
nodes.description: IRREVERSIBLE structural damage: LMP, membrane rupture
nodes.mechanism: Membrane integrity lost. ESCRT repair fails with aging. Requires clearance via lysophagy, not repair.
nodes.notes: IRREVERSIBLE - what human AD brains show

nodes.label: Lipofuscin
nodes.description: Indigestible lysosomal waste that accumulates with age
nodes.mechanism: Cross-linked lipid-protein aggregates that trap iron and resist degradation. Occupies lysosomal volume, reducing capacity. Generates ROS via Fenton chemistry.

nodes.label: Autophagosome
nodes.description: Double-membrane vesicle that engulfs cargo for degradation
nodes.mechanism: Forms around cargo, fuses with lysosome. Accumulates when lysosomes are damaged or dysfunctional.

nodes.label: Autophagy Flux
nodes.description: Rate of cargo delivery and degradation through autophagy
nodes.mechanism: Complete process rate. Enhanced flux is HARMFUL when lysosomes are damaged (cargo piles up with nowhere to go).

nodes.label: Cargo Accumulation
nodes.description: Buildup of undegraded material due to lysosomal failure
nodes.mechanism: Autophagosomes deliver cargo to damaged lysosomes that cannot degrade it. The autophagy paradox: more autophagy = more damage when lysosomes are broken.
nodes.notes: Why autophagy enhancers fail in human AD

nodes.label: mTORC1 Hyperactive
nodes.description: Blocks TFEB nuclear translocation; inhibits autophagy initiation; hyperactive in AD
nodes.mechanism: When active: inhibits autophagy initiation and sequesters TFEB in cytosol.

nodes.label: TFEB Phosphorylated
nodes.description: Master lysosomal transcription factor; sequestered in cytosol when phosphorylated by mTORC1
nodes.mechanism: Regulates CLEAR gene network. Nuclear translocation activates lysosome production. Can be activated mTOR-independently by sulforaphane.

nodes.label: Lysophagy
nodes.description: Selective autophagy of damaged lysosomes
nodes.mechanism: Ubiquitin-dependent recognition and clearance of damaged lysosomes. Requires functional lysosomes to degrade the damaged ones - a catch-22 in late disease.

nodes.label: Pericyte Count
nodes.description: Mural cells that wrap brain capillaries and maintain BBB integrity
nodes.mechanism: Regulate BBB permeability, capillary diameter, and neurovascular coupling. Lost early in AD. APOE4 accelerates pericyte death.
nodes.notes: INTEGRITY NOTE: Key pericyte-AD mouse studies from Zlokovic lab retracted (Sagare 2013 Nat Commun, Montagne 2018 Nat Med) due to image manipulation. Pericyte biology supported independently by Armulik 2010 (Nature), Daneman 2010 (Nature), and human imaging studies (Montagne 2015 Nat Med, NOT retracted). Claims about pericyte loss in AD remain plausible but mouse model evidence is weakened.

nodes.label: BBB Integrity
nodes.description: Tight-junction barrier controlling brain entry/exit of molecules
nodes.mechanism: Formed by endothelial cells, pericytes, and astrocyte endfeet. Pericyte loss opens BBB, allowing peripheral iron and immune cells into brain.
nodes.notes: BBB breakdown in AD supported by independent human MRI studies (Montagne 2015 Nat Med, Nation 2019 Nat Med) and is not solely dependent on retracted Zlokovic mouse data.

nodes.label: Astrocyte Endfoot Integrity
nodes.description: Structural basis for AQP4 + Fpn/Cp localization; FELINE keystone node
nodes.mechanism: Express AQP4 water channels for glymphatic flow. Polarization (AQP4 concentrated at endfeet) is required for efficient clearance. Lost in AD.

nodes.label: Endfoot AQP4 Polarization
nodes.description: AQP4 water channel polarized to perivascular endfeet; drives glymphatic flow
nodes.mechanism: Mediates perivascular CSF-ISF exchange (glymphatic flow). Polarization to endfeet required. Depolarization reduces clearance.

nodes.label: Glymphatic Flow Rate
nodes.description: AQP4-dependent perivascular bulk flow; primary iron exit route via CSF; increases 60% during sleep
nodes.mechanism: CSF flows along periarterial spaces, exchanges with ISF, clears solutes along perivenous routes. Increases 60% during sleep. Declines ~40% with age.

nodes.label: LRP1
nodes.description: Low-density lipoprotein receptor-related protein 1 - primary Aβ efflux receptor at BBB
nodes.mechanism: Two trafficking routes: PACSIN2-mediated tubular transcytosis (rapid Aβ efflux, physiological) vs PICALM-clathrin-Rab5 pathway (leads to LRP1 lysosomal degradation). In AD, trafficking shifts pathologically to Rab5 route.

nodes.label: Endothelial Iron Import
nodes.description: TfR1 on luminal side; DMT1 transcytosis; Fpn on abluminal side
nodes.mechanism: BBB breakdown allows peripheral iron, transferrin, and ferritin into brain parenchyma. Contributes to iron maldistribution.

nodes.label: Labile Iron Pool
nodes.description: Free redox-active iron in cytosol (Fe²⁺) available for Fenton chemistry
nodes.mechanism: Released from damaged lysosomes (LMP), dying cells, and degraded ferritin. Catalyzes hydroxyl radical production. The dangerous form of iron.

nodes.label: Functional Iron Deficiency
nodes.description: Iron bound in enzymes, mitochondria needed for life; depleted by maldistribution even as total brain iron rises
nodes.mechanism: Necessary for mitochondrial electron transport, DNA synthesis, neurotransmitter production. Iron maldistribution depletes functional pools while toxic pools grow.

nodes.label: Lysosomal Iron
nodes.description: Iron sequestered in dysfunctional lysosomes; requires acid pH to release via DMT1/TRPML1
nodes.mechanism: Alkalinized lysosomes cannot reduce Fe³⁺ to Fe²⁺ for export via DMT1. Iron accumulates, undergoes Fenton chemistry within the lysosome itself, damaging membranes.

nodes.label: Iron Trapped in Aggregates
nodes.description: Iron bound within Aβ plaques, α-synuclein aggregates, and lipofuscin
nodes.mechanism: Protein aggregates chelate iron. This iron is not bioavailable but can generate localized ROS. Contributes to aggregate toxicity.

nodes.label: Fenton Reaction
nodes.description: Fe²⁺ + H₂O₂ → Fe³⁺ + OH• + OH⁻ - produces hydroxyl radicals
nodes.mechanism: Most destructive ROS in biology. Occurs wherever labile iron meets hydrogen peroxide. Damages lipids, proteins, DNA, and lysosomal membranes.

nodes.label: Lipid Peroxides
nodes.description: PUFA oxidation products; ferroptosis executioner when GPX4 overwhelmed
nodes.mechanism: Iron-catalyzed oxidation of PUFAs. Damages cell membranes and lysosomal membranes. Produces 4-HNE and MDA. Leads to ferroptosis.

nodes.label: Ferroptosis
nodes.description: Iron-dependent regulated cell death via lipid peroxidation
nodes.mechanism: Defined by iron dependence, lipid peroxide accumulation, and GPX4 inactivation. Releases intracellular iron, propagating damage to neighbors.

nodes.label: GPX4 Activity
nodes.description: Glutathione peroxidase 4 - master ferroptosis brake; reduces lipid peroxides to alcohols
nodes.mechanism: Reduces lipid peroxides to alcohols. Selenium-dependent. Inactivation triggers ferroptosis.

nodes.label: Ferroportin
nodes.description: Only known cellular iron exporter (SLC40A1)
nodes.mechanism: Exports Fe²⁺ from cells. Expressed on neurons, microglia, endothelial cells, and enterocytes. Degraded by hepcidin binding. Key to iron redistribution strategy.
nodes.notes: The only way cells can export iron. Central to redistribution vs chelation debate.

nodes.label: Cell Death
nodes.description: Neuronal death through ferroptosis, lysosomal cell death, or apoptosis
nodes.mechanism: All cell death pathways release intracellular iron stores, propagating iron toxicity to neighboring cells. Creates a feed-forward loop.

nodes.label: Iron Release
nodes.description: Iron released from dying cells into extracellular space
nodes.mechanism: Cell death releases stored iron from ferritin and iron-containing proteins. Propagates damage to neighboring cells via labile iron.

nodes.label: Aβ Production
nodes.description: Normal amyloid-beta production rate via APP processing
nodes.mechanism: SILK study (Mawuenyega 2010): Aβ production rates are NORMAL in sporadic AD. Late-onset AD is a clearance disease, not overproduction.
nodes.notes: CRITICAL: production is normal, clearance is impaired

nodes.label: Aβ Clearance
nodes.description: Combined clearance of Aβ via BBB transport, glymphatic flow, enzymatic degradation, and microglial phagocytosis
nodes.mechanism: Multiple redundant systems: LRP1 BBB efflux, glymphatic drainage, neprilysin/IDE/cathepsin degradation, microglial phagocytosis. Plaques form only when MULTIPLE systems fail.

nodes.label: Aβ Aggregation
nodes.description: Amyloid-beta plaque formation due to clearance failure
nodes.mechanism: Aβ accumulates when clearance is 30% reduced (Mawuenyega 2010). Oligomers form first, then fibrils and plaques. Plaques trap iron and activate complement.

nodes.label: Tau Aggregation
nodes.description: Hyperphosphorylated tau neurofibrillary tangles
nodes.mechanism: Forms when autophagy-lysosome pathway fails. Better correlates with cognitive decline than Aβ.

nodes.label: α-Synuclein
nodes.description: Alpha-synuclein aggregation - hallmark of Parkinson's and Lewy body dementia
nodes.mechanism: GBA deficiency causes α-synuclein accumulation. Lysosomal dysfunction is common upstream cause across AD and PD.

nodes.label: Neprilysin / IDE
nodes.description: Extracellular Aβ-degrading enzymes: neprilysin and insulin-degrading enzyme
nodes.mechanism: Degrade soluble Aβ monomers and oligomers. IDE is competed away by insulin (link to metabolic syndrome). Cannot clear fibrillar plaques.

nodes.label: Microglial Phagocytosis
nodes.description: The ONLY mechanism capable of clearing deposited Aβ plaques
nodes.mechanism: Requires functional lysosomes for degradation and complement receptors (CR3, CR1) for recognition. Misdirected by chronic complement activation toward synapses instead of plaques.
nodes.notes: Only microglia can clear deposited plaques. Antibodies substitute for this function.

nodes.label: Homeostatic Microglia
nodes.description: Surveilling microglia in healthy state - phagocytic and neuroprotective
nodes.mechanism: Express P2RY12, TMEM119, CX3CR1. Perform synaptic pruning, debris clearance, and neurotrophic support. Transition to DAM or LDAM under stress.

nodes.label: DAM Microglia
nodes.description: Disease-associated microglia - TREM2-dependent activated state
nodes.mechanism: Upregulate TREM2, TYROBP, LPL, CST7. Enhanced phagocytosis but also produce inflammatory cytokines. TREM2 variants block this transition.

nodes.label: LDAM Microglia
nodes.description: Lipid-droplet-accumulating microglia - dysfunctional phagocytes
nodes.mechanism: Accumulate lipid droplets due to lysosomal failure. Cannot degrade phagocytosed material. Produce ROS and inflammatory cytokines. Age-associated.

nodes.label: Reactive Astrocyte (A1)
nodes.description: Neurotoxic reactive astrocyte phenotype induced by activated microglia
nodes.mechanism: Induced by IL-1α, TNFα, and C1q from microglia (Liddelow 2017). Lose normal functions and become neurotoxic. Kill neurons and oligodendrocytes.

nodes.label: Oligodendrocyte Count
nodes.description: Myelin-producing glial cells dependent on lysosomal function
nodes.mechanism: Require GBA for lipid recycling and myelin maintenance. GBA deficiency causes demyelination. Killed by A1 astrocytes.

nodes.label: Myelin Integrity
nodes.description: Myelin sheath integrity; shields axons from extracellular ROS; maintained by oligodendrocytes
nodes.mechanism: Caused by oligodendrocyte death (via A1 astrocytes) and GBA-dependent lipid recycling failure. Prominent in vascular dementia and MS.

nodes.label: NLRP3 Inflammasome
nodes.description: Inflammasome activated by cathepsin release from damaged lysosomes; lysosomal damage sensor first, glial effector second
nodes.mechanism: Activated by cathepsin release from damaged lysosomes, Aβ fibrils, and iron-mediated ROS. Amplifies neuroinflammation.

nodes.label: SARS-CoV-2
nodes.description: COVID-19 virus that targets pericytes via ACE2 and alkalinizes lysosomes via ORF3a
nodes.mechanism: Enters via ACE2 on pericytes. ORF3a protein inserts into lysosomal membrane and disrupts V-ATPase. Causes acute PLIG dysfunction.

nodes.label: ORF3a
nodes.description: SARS-CoV-2 viroporin that directly alkalinizes lysosomes
nodes.mechanism: Inserts into lysosomal membrane, forms ion channels that dissipate pH gradient. Blocks autophagosome-lysosome fusion. Persists in tissues post-acute infection.

nodes.label: Viral Persistence
nodes.description: Persistent viral proteins or RNA in tissues post-acute infection
nodes.mechanism: ORF3a and spike protein fragments detected in brain, gut, and lymph nodes months post-infection. Causes ongoing lysosomal alkalinization.

nodes.label: Herpes Viruses (HSV/HHV)
nodes.description: HSV-1 and HHV-6 associated with AD risk
nodes.mechanism: Reactivate under immune stress. Trigger neuroinflammation and lysosomal dysfunction. Epidemiological association with AD risk.

nodes.label: C1q
nodes.description: Complement initiator that tags synapses and debris for elimination
nodes.mechanism: Binds exposed phosphatidylserine on stressed synapses. INITIATES complement cascade. In AD, chronically elevated, causing excessive synapse elimination.
nodes.notes: Blocking C1q (the initiator) may redirect microglia from synapse destruction to Aβ clearance

nodes.label: C3 / C3a
nodes.description: Central complement component and its activation fragment
nodes.mechanism: C3 cleavage produces C3a (anaphylatoxin) and C3b (opsonin). C3b tags targets for phagocytosis. C3a recruits microglia. Amplification loop: C3b recruits more C3.

nodes.label: Complement Activation
nodes.description: Full complement cascade from C1q through membrane attack complex
nodes.mechanism: C1q → C4/C2 → C3 → C5 → C5b-9 (MAC). In AD, chronic activation diverts microglia from Aβ clearance to synapse destruction.

nodes.label: Complement-Mediated Pruning
nodes.description: C1q→C3→CR3→microglial phagocytosis; developmental pruning reactivated in neurodegeneration
nodes.mechanism: C1q tags stressed synapses, C3b opsonizes them, microglia phagocytose via CR3. Normal in development. Pathological in AD where it drives cognitive decline.
nodes.notes: Hong 2016: complement mediates early synapse loss in AD models

nodes.label: Cognitive Function
nodes.description: Clinical outcome; measured by MMSE, MoCA, CDR-SB; strongest histological correlate is synapse density
nodes.mechanism: Correlates more with synapse loss and tau pathology than with Aβ plaque burden. End result of multiple converging PLIG pathways.

nodes.label: Estrogen
nodes.description: Estradiol (E2) - neuroprotective hormone that supports lysosomal function
nodes.mechanism: Upregulates V-ATPase expression (maintains lysosomal pH). Supports pericyte survival. Anti-inflammatory. Loss at menopause accelerates PLIG decline.

nodes.label: Hepcidin
nodes.description: Master iron-regulatory hormone that controls ferroportin
nodes.mechanism: Binds ferroportin causing internalization and degradation. Elevated in inflammation (IL-6 drives hepcidin). Traps iron inside cells by removing the only export route.
nodes.notes: Hepcidin-ferroportin axis is central to iron maldistribution

nodes.label: Menopause
nodes.description: Estrogen decline at menopause accelerates PLIG dysfunction
nodes.mechanism: Loss of estrogen removes V-ATPase upregulation, pericyte protection, and anti-inflammatory effects. Explains 2:1 female:male AD ratio.

nodes.label: GBA (Glucocerebrosidase)
nodes.description: Lysosomal enzyme that cleaves glucosylceramide. pH-dependent.
nodes.mechanism: Requires lysosomal pH < 5.5 for activity. GBA mutations cause Gaucher disease and increase PD risk 5-10x. GBA deficiency impairs α-synuclein clearance and oligodendrocyte lipid recycling.

nodes.label: C381
nodes.description: V-ATPase enhancer that restores lysosomal acidification
nodes.mechanism: Benzyl urea derivative. Brain-penetrant, oral. Restores lysosomal pH in early dysfunction. Cannot help already-damaged lysosomes.
nodes.notes: PROMISING for early-stage. Addresses master switch (pH).

nodes.label: Sulforaphane
nodes.description: TFEB activator via mild ROS signaling
nodes.mechanism: Induces mild ROS that activates TFEB via mTOR-INDEPENDENT pathway. Creates new lysosomes rather than repairing damaged ones.
nodes.notes: PROMISING - bypasses mTOR, creates NEW lysosomes

nodes.label: Ambroxol
nodes.description: GBA chaperone that restores glucocerebrosidase activity
nodes.mechanism: pH-dependent chaperone: stabilizes GBA in ER at neutral pH, releases in acidic lysosome. Crosses BBB. In PD trials (phase 2).
nodes.notes: PROMISING for GBA-linked PD and AD

nodes.label: Fasudil
nodes.description: ROCK inhibitor enabling selective mitophagy/lysophagy
nodes.mechanism: Inhibits ROCK, de-represses PINK1-Parkin pathway. Enables selective clearance of damaged organelles (not bulk autophagy).
nodes.notes: PROMISING - targets damaged organelles selectively

nodes.label: ROCK
nodes.description: Rho-associated kinase that suppresses selective mitophagy
nodes.mechanism: Normally suppresses PINK1-Parkin pathway. Fasudil inhibition enables selective organelle clearance.

nodes.label: PINK1-Parkin
nodes.description: Selective mitophagy/lysophagy pathway
nodes.mechanism: PINK1 accumulates on damaged organelles, recruits Parkin for ubiquitination and selective degradation. Clears damaged mitochondria AND lysosomes.

nodes.label: Iron Redistribution
nodes.description: Therapeutic strategy: move iron from toxic compartments to functional pools, rather than removing it
nodes.mechanism: The goal is not less iron but correctly distributed iron. Variants: (1) ferroportin upregulation to enhance export, (2) lysosomal pH restoration to release trapped iron, (3) chelation (removes all iron - does not work). Redistribution requires functioning export machinery.
nodes.notes: Umbrella concept. Chelation is a failed variant. Redistribution via ferroportin and pH restoration are the viable approaches.

nodes.label: Iron Chelation (Deferiprone)
nodes.description: Systemic iron chelation - removes all iron non-selectively. Does NOT work.
nodes.mechanism: FAIRPARK-II trial (deferiprone in PD): WORSENED outcomes. Chelators remove functional iron that cells need while trapped iron in aggregates/lysosomes remains inaccessible to chelators.
nodes.notes: ANTI-TARGET: FAIRPARK-II showed deferiprone worsened PD outcomes. Chelation is the wrong approach - redistribution is needed.

nodes.label: Ferroportin Upregulation
nodes.description: Enhancing cellular iron export via ferroportin as redistribution strategy
nodes.mechanism: Increase ferroportin surface expression or block hepcidin-mediated degradation. Allows cells to export excess iron safely rather than accumulating it. Anti-hepcidin antibodies or mini-hepcidins under investigation.
nodes.notes: Viable iron redistribution approach: enhance export rather than chelate.

nodes.label: ANX005 (Anti-C1q)
nodes.description: Anti-C1q antibody that blocks complement initiation
nodes.mechanism: Blocks C1q, the INITIATING step of complement. May redirect microglia from synapse destruction to productive Aβ phagocytosis. Showed disease stabilization in Huntington's and promising NfL in ALS.
nodes.notes: PROMISING: blocks complement at initiation. Ravulizumab (C5 inhibitor) failed in ALS - must block upstream.

nodes.label: Ferroptosis Inhibitors
nodes.description: Lipid peroxidation inhibitors and GPX4 activators
nodes.mechanism: Liproxstatin-1, ferrostatin-1, vitamin E analogs. Interrupt iron-ROS-lipid peroxidation cascade. Symptomatic relief but do not address upstream iron maldistribution.

nodes.label: DGAT Inhibitors
nodes.description: Block lipid droplet formation in microglia to prevent LDAM phenotype
nodes.mechanism: Inhibit DGAT1/2 to prevent triglyceride synthesis and lipid droplet accumulation. May restore microglial phagocytic function.
nodes.notes: Experimental - targets LDAM microglia

nodes.label: Low-Dose Naltrexone
nodes.description: Low-dose naltrexone as microglial modulator
nodes.mechanism: At low doses (1-5mg), modulates TLR4 on microglia, reducing inflammatory activation without immunosuppression. Inexpensive, well-tolerated.
nodes.notes: Generic, inexpensive. Modulates rather than suppresses.

nodes.label: Rapamycin
nodes.description: mTOR inhibitor - context-dependent effects
nodes.mechanism: Inhibits mTOR, increasing autophagy flux. In EARLY disease (functional lysosomes): may help clear cargo. In LATE disease (damaged lysosomes): pushes cargo into broken lysosomes, worsening accumulation.
nodes.notes: NUANCED: Tier 2 per PLIG. Context-dependent - helpful early, potentially harmful late.

nodes.label: Sleep Optimization
nodes.description: Improving sleep quality to enhance glymphatic clearance
nodes.mechanism: Glymphatic clearance increases 60% during deep sleep. Melatonin restores AQP4 polarization. Sleep position (lateral) enhances drainage.
nodes.notes: Lifestyle intervention with strong mechanistic basis

nodes.label: Ferritin Iron
nodes.description: Safe storage pool; becomes trap when ferritinophagy overwhelms capacity

nodes.label: Transferrin Receptor
nodes.description: TfRC; iron import receptor upregulated when cytosol senses deficit

nodes.label: Iron-Sulfur Clusters
nodes.description: Fe-S clusters essential for mitochondrial ETC complexes I-III

nodes.label: Total Brain Iron
nodes.description: QSM-MRI visible but misleading; does not distinguish functional vs trapped pools

nodes.label: Glutathione
nodes.description: GPX4 cofactor; requires cysteine from system Xc-

nodes.label: System Xc-
nodes.description: Cystine/glutamate antiporter; imports cystine for GSH synthesis

nodes.label: FSP1 Activity
nodes.description: CoQ10-dependent ferroptosis backup system independent of GPX4

nodes.label: Senescent Cell Iron
nodes.description: Labile iron in senescent cells; high and targetable by senolytics

nodes.label: SASP Factors
nodes.description: IL-6, IL-1beta from senescent cells; drives inflammatory hepcidin

nodes.label: DMT1/Dexras1
nodes.description: NMDA→NO→Dexras1→DMT1 iron import; excitotoxicity-iron bridge

nodes.label: IRP1/IRP2
nodes.description: Iron-responsive element binding proteins; sense cytosolic iron and regulate TfR/ferroportin mRNA

nodes.label: NCOA4
nodes.description: Ferritinophagy receptor; delivers ferritin to lysosomes for iron release

nodes.label: cPLA2
nodes.description: Cleaves PUFAs from membranes; supplies ferroptosis substrate

nodes.label: Damaged Mitochondria
nodes.description: Accumulating damaged mitochondria from failed mitophagy

nodes.label: Escaped mtDNA
nodes.description: Escaped mtDNA from damaged lysosomes/mitochondria; cGAS-STING activator

nodes.label: v-ATPase Activity
nodes.description: Proton pump activity level; determines lysosomal pH; FELINE L-layer lynchpin

nodes.label: Lysosomal Iron Release
nodes.description: Fe2+ released from ferritin degradation when lysosomal pH is sufficient

nodes.label: Ferritinophagy Rate
nodes.description: NCOA4-mediated ferritin delivery to lysosomes; iron liberation rate

nodes.label: BMP Levels
nodes.description: Bis(monoacylglycero)phosphate; PGRN-dependent; lipid degradation cofactor

nodes.label: OPC Pool
nodes.description: Oligodendrocyte precursor cells; remyelination capacity reservoir

nodes.label: OPC Differentiation
nodes.description: Blocked by Wnt, complement C3a; required for remyelination repair

nodes.label: OL FTH1 Export
nodes.description: OLs export ferritin heavy chain to neurons via exosomes; intercellular iron insulation

nodes.label: OL Iron Content
nodes.description: Oligodendrocyte iron content; >= 3x neuronal iron (FELINE prediction)

nodes.label: Tau Iron Binding
nodes.description: Tau R1/R2 repeat domains bind Fe2+/Fe3+; compartmental insulation. Lost when tau hyperphosphorylates.

nodes.label: aSyn Ferrireductase
nodes.description: Alpha-synuclein ferrireductase activity (Fe3+ → Fe2+); context-dependent: health=uptake, disease=membrane ROS

nodes.label: PS Exposure (Ferroptotic)
nodes.description: Phosphatidylserine externalized on ferroptotic membranes; eat-me signal for C1q binding

nodes.label: LPC Generation
nodes.description: Lysophosphatidylcholine from lipid peroxidation; dual PS/C1q opsonization marker

nodes.label: Dendritic Retraction
nodes.description: Phase 1 sublethal ferroptosis → somatic shrinkage, dendritic/neuropil loss WITHOUT cell death

nodes.label: Wnt OPC Inhibition
nodes.description: Complement activation → Wnt → blocks OPC differentiation and remyelination

nodes.label: Pericyte PDGFRbeta
nodes.description: Pericyte health marker; sPDGFRbeta in CSF = pericyte damage marker

nodes.label: Endfoot Fpn/Cp
nodes.description: Iron distribution machinery on endfeet; ferroportin + ceruloplasmin co-localized; supplies neurons/OLs

nodes.label: alpha1-Syntrophin
nodes.description: Anchors AQP4 to endfoot membrane; Snta1-/- mice show AQP4 depolarization

nodes.label: Perivascular Space
nodes.description: Virchow-Robin spaces; glymphatic conduit for CSF-ISF exchange

nodes.label: Endothelial Hepcidin Response
nodes.description: Astrocyte-secreted hepcidin degrades endothelial ferroportin; restricts iron import

nodes.label: CSF Iron Clearance
nodes.description: Iron exits brain via CSF → subarachnoid reabsorption; one-way traffic (Morris 2011)

nodes.label: Astrocyte Iron Distribution
nodes.description: Iron distribution FROM astrocytes TO neurons/OLs (internal redistribution, not export)

nodes.label: Meningeal Lymphatic Drainage
nodes.description: Deep cervical lymph nodes; downstream of glymphatic; declines with age

nodes.label: Sleep-Glymphatic Coupling
nodes.description: Sleep increases glymphatic clearance ~60% (Xie 2013); primary modifiable factor

nodes.label: Systemic Hepcidin
nodes.description: Systemic iron regulation from liver; distinct from brain hepcidin

nodes.label: H2S Production
nodes.description: CSE/CBS → H2S; anti-ferroptotic; sulfhydration of proteins

nodes.label: CSE Enzyme
nodes.description: Cystathionine gamma-lyase; CSE-/- sufficient for AD phenotype

nodes.label: CBS Enzyme
nodes.description: Cystathionine beta-synthase; homocysteine metabolism

nodes.label: Synapses
nodes.description: Synapse density; strongest histological correlate of cognitive status (Terry 1991)

nodes.label: Functional Independence
nodes.description: ADL/IADL capacity; bridges cognitive decline to mortality via immobility, dysphagia, aspiration risk

nodes.label: Mortality
nodes.description: Death; ultimate endpoint; proximate causes are systemic complications of advanced dementia

nodes.label: Familial AD Mutations
nodes.description: APP, PSEN1, PSEN2 autosomal dominant mutations; deterministic EOAD

nodes.label: Biological Sex
nodes.description: Biological sex; affects iron metabolism, immune responses, hormone levels

nodes.label: Insulin Resistance
nodes.description: Brain insulin resistance; "Type 3 diabetes" concept. Drives mTORC1 hyperactivation via PI3K-AKT-TSC1/2 pathway

nodes.label: AMPK (inhibited)
nodes.description: pSer345 by mTORC1 prevents AMPK recruitment to lysosome; blocks autophagy initiation

nodes.label: ULK1 (inhibited)
nodes.description: pSer757 by mTORC1 blocks AMPK binding site, inhibits autophagy initiation

nodes.label: Mitophagy Reduced
nodes.description: AMPK/ULK1 pathway impaired; PINK1/Parkin still functional but cannot initiate

nodes.label: S6K1 Active
nodes.description: p70 S6 kinase; phosphorylated at Thr389 by mTORC1. Feeds back to phosphorylate IRS-1

nodes.label: IRS-1 Serine Phosphorylated
nodes.description: pS307, pS616, pS636/639 = inhibitory; 14-3-3 binding, degradation → insulin resistance

nodes.label: Mitophagosome
nodes.description: PINK1/Parkin-tagged mitochondrion in autophagosome; requires lysosomal fusion

nodes.label: Autolysosome
nodes.description: Mitophagosome fused with lysosome; if pH >5.5, degradation fails

nodes.label: Undegraded mtDNA
nodes.description: DNase II inactive at high pH; mtDNA persists in autolysosome → escapes via LMP

nodes.label: Mitochondrial ROS
nodes.description: Superoxide/H2O2 from dysfunctional ETC complex I/III

nodes.label: Oxidized mtDNA
nodes.description: 8-oxo-dG modified mtDNA within mitochondria; precursor to ox-mtDNA fragments

nodes.label: Mitochondrial Ca2+ Overload
nodes.description: Matrix Ca2+ overload from impaired MCU/NCLX handling; triggers mPTP opening

nodes.label: mPTP Open
nodes.description: Mitochondrial permeability transition pore open state; triggered by Ca2+ via CypD

nodes.label: VDAC Oligomers
nodes.description: VDAC1 oligomers forming pores in outer membrane; allow mtDNA fragment exit

nodes.label: Cytosolic Ox-mtDNA
nodes.description: 500-650 bp FEN1-cleaved oxidized mtDNA fragments in cytosol; specifically activates NLRP3

nodes.label: Cytosolic mtDNA
nodes.description: Non-oxidized mtDNA in cytosol; preferentially activates cGAS-STING pathway

nodes.label: cGAS-STING
nodes.description: Cytosolic DNA sensor → 2'3'-cGAMP → STING → TBK1 → IRF3 → Type I IFN; drives aging inflammation

nodes.label: Microglia Activated
nodes.description: Generic activated state; entry point from neuroinflammatory signals (IL-1beta, TNF-alpha, IL-6)

nodes.label: HIF-1alpha Stabilized
nodes.description: Stabilized by NF-kB under normoxic conditions (pseudohypoxia); drives metabolic reprogramming

nodes.label: PKM2 Upregulated
nodes.description: Dimeric form acts as transcription coactivator; feed-forward loop with HIF-1alpha

nodes.label: Glycolytic Switch
nodes.description: Warburg effect - aerobic glycolysis in activated microglia; driven by HIF-1alpha target genes

nodes.label: SREBP1 Activated
nodes.description: Master lipogenic transcription factor; activated by glycolytic metabolites and mTORC1

nodes.label: Lipid Droplets
nodes.description: TAG-rich storage organelles; defines LDAM phenotype. Localize near Abeta plaques

nodes.label: Phagocytosis Impaired
nodes.description: LDAM show ~50% reduced phagocytic capacity; DGAT2 degrader restores function

nodes.label: Persistent Cytokine Secretion
nodes.description: Chronic proinflammatory output (IL-1beta, TNF-alpha, IL-6) from LDAM even after stimulus removed

nodes.label: A1 Astrocytes
nodes.description: Neurotoxic astrocyte phenotype; induced by microglial IL-1alpha + TNF-alpha + C1q

nodes.label: BACE1 Upregulated
nodes.description: Beta-secretase; rate-limiting for Abeta production. Upregulated by NF-kB (inflammation → more Abeta)

nodes.label: Amyloidogenic APP Processing
nodes.description: BACE1 → C99 → gamma-secretase → Abeta40/Abeta42

nodes.label: Abeta Oligomers
nodes.description: Soluble oligomers; MOST TOXIC species. Inhibit LTP, bind PrPc/mGluR5/NMDAR

nodes.label: Abeta Plaques
nodes.description: Insoluble fibrillar deposits; may be less toxic than oligomers (sequestration function)

nodes.label: Plaque Compaction
nodes.description: TREM2/microglia-dependent protective compaction; prevents oligomer release

nodes.label: Synaptic Abeta Binding
nodes.description: Oligomer binding to PrPc, mGluR5, NMDAR → LTP inhibition → synapse dysfunction

nodes.label: GSK3beta Active
nodes.description: Major tau kinase; phosphorylates Ser199, Ser202, Thr231, Ser396. Activated by NLRP3/Wnt inhibition

nodes.label: p38 MAPK Active
nodes.description: IL-1beta-activated tau kinase; phosphorylates Ser199, Ser202, Thr205

nodes.label: PP2A Inhibited
nodes.description: Major tau phosphatase (>70% of dephosphorylation); activity down 20-40% in AD brain

nodes.label: Tau Hyperphosphorylated
nodes.description: pTau at AT8 (Ser202/Thr205), PHF-1 (Ser396/404) epitopes; detaches from microtubules

nodes.label: Tau PHF
nodes.description: Paired helical filaments; beta-sheet-rich aggregates from hyperphosphorylated tau

nodes.label: Neurofibrillary Tangles
nodes.description: Mature NFTs; burden correlates with cognitive decline better than Abeta plaques

nodes.label: Tau Exosomal Release
nodes.description: Exosome-mediated tau secretion by neurons and microglia; mechanism for Braak-stage spreading

nodes.label: Tau Seeding
nodes.description: Prion-like templated misfolding; exosomal tau seeds aggregation in recipient neurons

nodes.label: PHF Formation Capacity
nodes.description: Species-dependent: requires 3R/4R tau mixture (human) for PHF; 4R-only (mouse) does not aggregate same way

nodes.label: Homocysteine
nodes.description: From methionine metabolism; elevated in AD risk. CBS substrate

nodes.label: Cystathionine
nodes.description: Intermediate in reverse transsulfuration; CBS product, CSE substrate

nodes.label: Cysteine
nodes.description: Rate-limiting substrate for GSH synthesis; CSE product

nodes.label: Sulfhydration
nodes.description: S-sulfhydration/persulfidation; protective PTM by H2S on protein cysteine residues

nodes.label: GSK3beta Sulfhydrated
nodes.description: GSK3beta with Cys sulfhydrated → INACTIVE; counter-mechanism to NLRP3-driven tau phosphorylation

nodes.label: TREM2 Variants
nodes.description: Genetic variants; ~3x AD risk, hypomorphic function

nodes.label: Cognitive Score
nodes.description: MMSE (0-30), ADAS-Cog (0-70), CDR-SB (0-18), MoCA (0-30)

nodes.description: Neuron number

edges.keyInsight: ESCRT efficiency declines with age - key translational factor
Aging weakens lysosomal membranes
APOE4 impairs lysosomal lipid handling
APOE4 accelerates pericyte death, BBB dysfunction
edges.notes: INTEGRITY NOTE: Some APOE4-pericyte evidence from Zlokovic lab. Independent support from Halliday 2016 (Acta Neuropathol) and human imaging studies.

edges.keyInsight: Sleep disruption reduces glymphatic Aβ clearance by up to 60%
TBI causes acute BBB disruption and pericyte loss
TBI microhemorrhages deposit iron in brain tissue
Hyperglycemia damages pericytes (same mechanism as diabetic retinopathy)
edges.notes: Pericyte damage from hyperglycemia is well-established in retinal research (Hammes 2002) independent of Zlokovic lab.

edges.keyInsight: Insulin competes with Aβ for IDE binding
TREM2 variants block transition to disease-associated microglia
Glymphatic function declines ~40% with aging

edges.mechanismDescription: V-ATPase pumps protons to maintain acidic pH. More V-ATPase = lower (more acidic) pH.
edges.keyInsight: V-ATPase is the master switch for lysosomal function

edges.mechanismDescription: Elevated (alkaline) pH inactivates lysosomal enzymes
edges.keyInsight: Elevated pH = enzyme dysfunction (REVERSIBLE)

edges.mechanismDescription: Cathepsins require pH < 5 for activity. Alkalinization inactivates them.
edges.keyInsight: pH elevation → loss of Aβ/tau degradation capacity

edges.mechanismDescription: GBA requires pH < 5.5 for activity. Alkalinization inactivates it.
edges.keyInsight: pH elevation → GBA failure → α-synuclein and lipid accumulation

edges.mechanismDescription: Alkalinized lysosomes cannot reduce Fe³⁺ to Fe²⁺ for DMT1 export. Iron accumulates and undergoes Fenton chemistry.
edges.keyInsight: LYSOSOMAL FENTON LOOP: pH up → iron trapped → Fenton → LMP → more pH disruption

edges.mechanismDescription: Intact membrane prevents permeabilization
edges.keyInsight: Membrane health prevents LMP

edges.mechanismDescription: Membrane breach causes structural damage
edges.keyInsight: LMP = irreversible damage (key distinction from dysfunction)

edges.mechanismDescription: Damage triggers ESCRT recruitment attempt
edges.keyInsight: ESCRT recruited to repair but fails in aged humans

edges.mechanismDescription: Successful ESCRT repair seals membrane
edges.keyInsight: Successful in young animals, rare in aged humans

edges.mechanismDescription: Failed repair leads to cathepsin leakage into cytosol
edges.keyInsight: Cathepsin release = death cascade initiation

edges.mechanismDescription: Cytosolic cathepsins trigger apoptosis
edges.keyInsight: Lysosomal cell death pathway

edges.mechanismDescription: Cytosolic cathepsin B activates NLRP3
edges.keyInsight: Lysosomal damage → inflammasome → IL-1β amplification

edges.mechanismDescription: TFEB activates CLEAR gene network
edges.keyInsight: TFEB = master regulator of lysosome creation
Creates new functional lysosomes to replace damaged ones

edges.mechanismDescription: Lipofuscin traps iron and catalyzes ROS within lysosomes
edges.keyInsight: Lipofuscin is an iron sink that generates ongoing oxidative damage

edges.mechanismDescription: Lipofuscin occupies lysosomal volume, reducing degradation capacity
edges.keyInsight: Indigestible waste reduces lysosomal capacity with age
More flux = more autophagosomes formed

edges.mechanismDescription: Autophagosomes fuse with lysosomes for degradation
edges.keyInsight: Autolysosome formation requires functional lysosomes

edges.mechanismDescription: Damaged lysosomes cannot degrade delivered cargo
edges.keyInsight: CRITICAL: Why autophagy enhancers fail in human AD

edges.mechanismDescription: mTOR inhibits autophagy initiation
edges.keyInsight: mTOR = autophagy brake

edges.mechanismDescription: mTOR phosphorylates TFEB, sequestering it in cytosol
edges.keyInsight: Active mTOR = TFEB trapped, no new lysosomes
Dysfunctional lysosomes cannot clear Aβ - the core clearance failure

edges.mechanismDescription: Lysophagy clears damaged lysosomes
edges.keyInsight: Catch-22: lysophagy requires functional lysosomes to degrade the damaged ones

edges.mechanismDescription: Pericytes maintain tight junctions and regulate BBB permeability
edges.keyInsight: Pericyte loss → BBB breakdown → uncontrolled brain entry
edges.notes: INTEGRITY NOTE: Zlokovic lab mouse studies (Sagare 2013, Montagne 2018) retracted. L2 confidence retained based on independent evidence: Armulik 2010 (Nature), Daneman 2010 (Nature), Bell 2010 (Neuron). Pericyte-BBB relationship is well-established beyond Zlokovic lab.

edges.mechanismDescription: Intact BBB limits iron entry to regulated transferrin receptor pathway
edges.keyInsight: BBB breakdown = unregulated iron flooding into brain

edges.mechanismDescription: Unregulated iron crosses broken BBB
edges.keyInsight: BBB breach contributes to brain iron maldistribution

edges.mechanismDescription: Astrocyte endfeet express and polarize AQP4 channels
edges.keyInsight: AQP4 polarization at endfeet required for glymphatic flow

edges.mechanismDescription: AQP4 enables perivascular CSF-ISF exchange
edges.keyInsight: AQP4 depolarization (lost from endfeet) reduces clearance

edges.mechanismDescription: Glymphatic flow clears soluble Aβ from parenchyma to CSF
edges.keyInsight: One of multiple redundant Aβ clearance systems

edges.mechanismDescription: LRP1 mediates Aβ transcytosis across BBB (PACSIN2 route)
edges.keyInsight: Primary route for Aβ efflux. In AD, LRP1 trafficked to lysosomes and degraded.

edges.mechanismDescription: In AD, LRP1 trafficking shifts from PACSIN2 (efflux) to Rab5 (lysosomal degradation)
edges.keyInsight: Lysosomal dysfunction causes LRP1 to be degraded instead of recycled

edges.mechanismDescription: Pericytes support endothelial ferroportin expression for iron export across BBB
edges.keyInsight: Pericyte loss impairs brain iron export via ferroportin at BBB

edges.mechanismDescription: Fe²⁺ + H₂O₂ → Fe³⁺ + OH• + OH⁻
edges.keyInsight: Free iron + hydrogen peroxide = hydroxyl radicals (most destructive ROS)

edges.mechanismDescription: Hydroxyl radicals oxidize membrane PUFAs
edges.keyInsight: Fenton-generated radicals damage membranes
Accumulated lipid peroxides trigger ferroptotic cell death

edges.mechanismDescription: GPX4 reduces lipid peroxides to alcohols
edges.keyInsight: GPX4 = primary ferroptosis brake
Ferroptosis is a major cell death pathway in neurodegeneration
FEEDBACK: dying cells release stored iron
Released iron enters labile pool, propagating damage to neighbors

edges.mechanismDescription: Iron in alkalinized lysosomes undergoes Fenton chemistry, damaging lysosomal membrane from within
edges.keyInsight: LYSOSOMAL FENTON LOOP: internal Fenton → LMP → more iron release

edges.mechanismDescription: Hydroxyl radicals from Fenton reaction damage lysosomal membranes
edges.keyInsight: Iron-mediated damage to lysosomes creates a vicious cycle

edges.mechanismDescription: Aβ plaques bind and sequester iron
edges.keyInsight: Aggregates become iron sinks, contributing to functional iron depletion

edges.mechanismDescription: Ferroportin exports Fe²⁺ from cells, reducing intracellular labile iron
edges.keyInsight: Only known cellular iron exporter. Functional ferroportin = iron homeostasis.

edges.mechanismDescription: Peroxidized lipids weaken lysosomal membrane
edges.keyInsight: Iron damage feeds back to lysosomal damage

edges.mechanismDescription: 30% clearance reduction is sufficient for Aβ accumulation (SILK study)
edges.keyInsight: Clearance failure, NOT overproduction, drives sporadic AD
Undegraded tau forms neurofibrillary tangles
Undegraded intracellular Aβ seeds aggregation

edges.mechanismDescription: Cathepsin B cleaves Aβ within lysosomes
edges.keyInsight: Functional cathepsins degrade Aβ - requires acidic pH

edges.mechanismDescription: Extracellular enzymes degrade soluble Aβ monomers/oligomers
edges.keyInsight: Cannot clear fibrillar plaques - only soluble forms

edges.mechanismDescription: Only mechanism that clears deposited Aβ plaques
edges.keyInsight: Microglia are the ONLY plaque-clearing mechanism. Anti-Aβ antibodies substitute for this.
edges.notes: Key PLIG insight: restore microglial function instead of antibody substitution

edges.mechanismDescription: GBA cleaves glucosylceramide, preventing α-synuclein accumulation
edges.keyInsight: GBA deficiency → α-synuclein aggregation (PD link)

edges.mechanismDescription: Normal production + impaired clearance = accumulation
edges.keyInsight: Production is normal in sporadic AD. Only clearance is impaired.

edges.mechanismDescription: Lysosomal failure causes lipid droplet accumulation in microglia
edges.keyInsight: Dysfunctional lysosomes → LDAM phenotype → lost phagocytic capacity

edges.mechanismDescription: Lipid-laden microglia cannot phagocytose effectively
edges.keyInsight: LDAM microglia are dysfunctional phagocytes

edges.mechanismDescription: Healthy microglia perform efficient phagocytosis
edges.keyInsight: Homeostatic microglia maintain brain clearance

edges.mechanismDescription: DAM activation replaces homeostatic state
edges.keyInsight: Disease activation trades surveillance for inflammatory response

edges.mechanismDescription: NLRP3 produces IL-1β/IL-6; IL-6 drives hepcidin transcription → ferroportin degradation → iron trapping
edges.keyInsight: Specific mechanism: inflammasome → IL-6 → hepcidin → iron trapping (not generic inflammation)

edges.mechanismDescription: IL-1α, TNFα, C1q from activated microglia induce A1 astrocytes (Liddelow 2017)
edges.keyInsight: Activated microglia convert astrocytes to neurotoxic A1 phenotype

edges.mechanismDescription: A1 astrocytes kill oligodendrocytes
edges.keyInsight: Reactive astrocytes → oligodendrocyte death → demyelination

edges.mechanismDescription: GBA provides lipid recycling for myelin maintenance
edges.keyInsight: GBA deficiency → failed myelin maintenance → demyelination

edges.mechanismDescription: Healthy oligodendrocytes maintain myelin
edges.keyInsight: Oligodendrocyte loss leads to white matter damage

edges.mechanismDescription: Reactive transformation disrupts endfeet polarization and AQP4 localization
edges.keyInsight: A1 astrocytes lose endfeet function → glymphatic impairment

edges.mechanismDescription: Viral infection damages and kills pericytes
edges.keyInsight: COVID → pericyte death → BBB breakdown

edges.mechanismDescription: ORF3a viroporin inserts into lysosomal membrane, dissipates pH gradient
edges.keyInsight: ORF3a directly causes lysosomal alkalinization

edges.mechanismDescription: ORF3a ion channels dissipate proton gradient
edges.keyInsight: Viral alkalinization mimics age-related lysosomal dysfunction

edges.mechanismDescription: Persistent viral proteins maintain ORF3a in tissues
edges.keyInsight: Long COVID: ongoing lysosomal alkalinization from persistent ORF3a

edges.mechanismDescription: Herpesvirus reactivation triggers inflammasome via cell damage signals, not generic inflammation
edges.keyInsight: Viral reactivation → specific cell damage → NLRP3 activation (immune RESPONDS to damage)

edges.mechanismDescription: C1q initiates classical complement cascade
edges.keyInsight: C1q is the INITIATING step. Block here, not downstream.

edges.mechanismDescription: Cascade produces C3b (opsonin) and C3a (anaphylatoxin)
edges.keyInsight: C3b amplification loop: C3b recruits more C3 cleavage

edges.mechanismDescription: C1q tags stressed synapses for complement-mediated elimination by microglia
edges.keyInsight: C1q on synapses → C3b → microglial phagocytosis via CR3

edges.mechanismDescription: Chronic complement activation misdirects microglia toward synapses instead of plaques
edges.keyInsight: KEY PLIG INSIGHT: complement diverts microglia from Aβ clearance to synapse destruction
edges.notes: C1q inhibition may redirect microglial resources toward plaque clearance

edges.mechanismDescription: Aβ plaques activate complement by binding C1q
edges.keyInsight: Plaques drive complement activation → synapse loss feedback loop

edges.mechanismDescription: C1q is one of three signals (with IL-1α and TNFα) that induce neurotoxic A1 astrocytes
edges.keyInsight: C1q contributes to A1 astrocyte induction (Liddelow 2017)

edges.mechanismDescription: Estradiol upregulates V-ATPase subunit expression
edges.keyInsight: Estrogen maintains lysosomal acidification - loss at menopause accelerates pH elevation

edges.mechanismDescription: Estrogen supports pericyte survival and BBB integrity
edges.keyInsight: Post-menopausal BBB deterioration partly estrogen-dependent

edges.mechanismDescription: Ovarian estrogen production ceases at menopause
edges.keyInsight: Menopause removes protective estrogen effects on all PLIG pillars

edges.mechanismDescription: C381 enhances V-ATPase activity
edges.keyInsight: RESTORES existing lysosome function by fixing pH

edges.mechanismDescription: Ambroxol acts as pharmacological chaperone for GBA
edges.keyInsight: Stabilizes GBA in ER, releases in acidic lysosome. Crosses BBB.

edges.mechanismDescription: Fasudil directly inhibits ROCK kinase
edges.keyInsight: Direct kinase inhibition

edges.mechanismDescription: ROCK normally suppresses PINK1-Parkin pathway
edges.keyInsight: ROCK inhibition enables selective mitophagy/lysophagy

edges.mechanismDescription: PINK1-Parkin clears damaged organelles selectively
edges.keyInsight: Selective clearance of damaged lysosomes (lysophagy)

edges.mechanismDescription: Goal: move iron from toxic labile pools back to functional compartments or export it
edges.keyInsight: Redistribution (not removal) is the correct iron strategy
edges.notes: Umbrella concept: viable approaches are ferroportin upregulation and lysosomal pH restoration

edges.mechanismDescription: Chelators remove all iron non-selectively including functional iron
edges.keyInsight: FAILED: FAIRPARK-II deferiprone worsened PD outcomes
edges.notes: ANTI-TARGET: chelation removes functional iron while trapped iron remains inaccessible

edges.mechanismDescription: Chelators bind labile iron but also deplete functional pools
edges.keyInsight: Chelation reduces labile iron but at cost of functional iron depletion

edges.mechanismDescription: Increase ferroportin surface expression or block hepcidin-mediated degradation
edges.keyInsight: Enhancing export rather than chelating: preserves functional iron while reducing toxic pools

edges.mechanismDescription: Anti-C1q antibody blocks complement initiation
edges.keyInsight: Block at initiation (C1q), not downstream (C5). Ravulizumab C5-inhibitor failed in ALS.
edges.notes: Redirect microglia from synapse destruction to Aβ clearance

edges.mechanismDescription: Radical-trapping antioxidants block lipid peroxidation
edges.keyInsight: Symptomatic relief but does not address upstream iron maldistribution

edges.mechanismDescription: Block lipid droplet formation to prevent LDAM phenotype
edges.keyInsight: Experimental: may restore microglial phagocytic function

edges.mechanismDescription: Low-dose naltrexone modulates TLR4, reducing inflammatory activation
edges.keyInsight: Modulates rather than suppresses microglial inflammation. Generic, inexpensive.

edges.mechanismDescription: Direct mTOR inhibition increases autophagy flux
edges.keyInsight: Context-dependent: helpful early (functional lysosomes), harmful late (damaged lysosomes)

edges.mechanismDescription: Increased autophagy flux pushes cargo into damaged lysosomes
edges.keyInsight: PARADOX: more autophagy = more damage when lysosomes are broken
edges.notes: Nuanced: Tier 2 per PLIG. Timing matters.

edges.mechanismDescription: Deep sleep increases glymphatic flow 60%
edges.keyInsight: Lifestyle intervention: sleep is the primary glymphatic driver

edges.mechanismDescription: Restoring lysosomal pH enables Fe³⁺ → Fe²⁺ reduction and DMT1 export
edges.keyInsight: pH restoration = iron redistribution from lysosomes to functional pools
edges.notes: Lysosomal pH fix is itself an iron redistribution strategy

edges.mechanismDescription: Lysosomal iron release requires acid pH for DMT1/TRPML1. pH >5.5 → iron remains sequestered
edges.keyInsight: Core mechanism linking L-layer failure to Fe-layer functional deficiency

edges.mechanismDescription: LMP dumps lysosomal iron into cytosol → spike in labile iron pool → Fenton chemistry
edges.keyInsight: Catastrophic transition: from iron-trapped-in-lysosomes to iron-flooding-cytosol

edges.mechanismDescription: NCOA4 delivers ferritin to lysosomes; if pH impaired, iron trapped
edges.keyInsight: Autophagy enhancement can WORSEN iron-mediated damage by increasing ferritinophagy

edges.mechanismDescription: OLs contain >= 2x more iron than any other brain cell type. OL death releases entire iron payload
edges.keyInsight: Explains why demyelinating diseases show iron-rim lesions

edges.mechanismDescription: Tau R1/R2 repeat domains bind Fe2+/Fe3+, reducing labile iron. Hyperphosphorylation releases iron.
edges.keyInsight: Tau reframed from toxic aggregate to failed iron buffer

edges.mechanismDescription: aSyn reduces Fe3+ → Fe2+; in health facilitates uptake, in disease generates reactive Fe2+ near membranes
edges.keyInsight: Explains PD: aSyn aggregation at membranes + ferrireductase = Fe2+ at PUFA-rich membranes

edges.mechanismDescription: BBB breakdown → unregulated iron entry → OLs (highest uptake cells) preferentially loaded
edges.keyInsight: Connects N-layer damage to I-layer vulnerability

edges.mechanismDescription: Pericytes physically support astrocyte endfoot attachment. Loss → endfoot retraction → AQP4/Fpn depolarization.
edges.keyInsight: CRITICAL: connects pericyte biology to FELINE dual-failure hypothesis

edges.mechanismDescription: Glymphatic/CSF drainage is primary iron exit route from brain. Reduced flow → iron accumulates.
edges.keyInsight: LOAD-BEARING EDGE: if wrong, E-layer export thesis collapses

edges.mechanismDescription: Hepcidin binds ferroportin → internalization → ubiquitination → degradation
edges.keyInsight: Inflammation → iron trapping via specific molecular mechanism

edges.mechanismDescription: Sleep increases glymphatic clearance ~60% vs wakefulness
edges.keyInsight: Sleep disruption as AD risk factor through iron-specific mechanism

edges.mechanismDescription: OLs export FTH1 to neurons; OL death → loss of iron-buffering supply
edges.keyInsight: Neurons die after demyelination even when axonal damage minimal - they lose iron-buffering partner

edges.mechanismDescription: ORF3a alkalinizes lysosomes; VZV gM degrades SNAP29 blocking autophagosome-lysosome fusion
edges.keyInsight: VZV/SARS-CoV-2 → AD risk via specific L-layer mechanism, not generic inflammation

edges.mechanismDescription: JmjC demethylases are Fe2+-dependent; rising iron → epigenetic de-repression → reactivation
edges.keyInsight: Unifying mechanism for iron-driven reactivation of ALL herpesviruses simultaneously

edges.mechanismDescription: Sub-lethal ferroptotic stress → scramblase activation → PS externalized on synaptic membranes
edges.keyInsight: Biochemical bridge from Fe-layer to immune effector arm

edges.mechanismDescription: Iron-catalyzed lipid peroxidation generates LPC as direct degradation product
edges.keyInsight: LPC is the chemical fingerprint of iron-catalyzed membrane oxidation

edges.mechanismDescription: C1q globular heads bind exposed PS with Kd ~37-70 nM
edges.keyInsight: Complement reads oxidative damage state of synaptic membranes

edges.mechanismDescription: Microglia phagocytose ferroptotic synapses → internalize iron → feedforward loop
edges.keyInsight: Janitors become part of the problem: ferroptosis-complement amplification cycle

edges.mechanismDescription: Phase 1 ferroptotic stress (1.3-1.6x baseline iron) causes somatic atrophy and dendritic retraction without death
edges.keyInsight: Explains SCD/early MCI: neurons alive but shrunken, dendrites retracted

edges.mechanismDescription: Dendritic spine retraction eliminates postsynaptic contact sites
edges.keyInsight: Cell-autonomous geometric consequence of membrane damage; no immune involvement needed

edges.mechanismDescription: Complement cascade eliminates tagged synapses via microglial phagocytosis
edges.keyInsight: Triple knockout validation: C1q-/-, C3-/-, CR3-/- all preserve synapses

edges.mechanismDescription: Synapse density is the strongest predictor of cognitive status (r = 0.70-0.88)
edges.keyInsight: Final common pathway: every upstream edge converges here

edges.mechanismDescription: Progressive cognitive decline impairs IADLs before basic ADLs
edges.keyInsight: Intermediate node between cognitive decline and mortality

edges.mechanismDescription: Advanced dementia → immobility → dysphagia → aspiration pneumonia (60-70% of AD deaths)
edges.keyInsight: Completes throughline from iron atom to death certificate

edges.mechanismDescription: NCOA4 is the ferritinophagy receptor
edges.keyInsight: NCOA4 delivers ferritin to lysosomes

edges.mechanismDescription: System Xc- imports cystine for GSH synthesis
edges.keyInsight: GSH supply depends on system Xc-

edges.mechanismDescription: GSH is the essential cofactor for GPX4
edges.keyInsight: GSH depletion → GPX4 inactivation → ferroptosis

edges.mechanismDescription: cPLA2 cleaves PUFAs from membranes, supplying ferroptosis substrate
edges.keyInsight: PUFA supply for lipid peroxidation

edges.mechanismDescription: IL-6 from SASP drives hepcidin production
edges.keyInsight: Senescent cells → inflammatory hepcidin → iron trapping

edges.mechanismDescription: Low iron → IRP binding stabilizes TfR mRNA
edges.keyInsight: Iron sensing feedback loop

edges.mechanismDescription: IRPs regulate ferroportin mRNA translation
edges.keyInsight: Iron sensing controls both import and export

edges.mechanismDescription: FSP1 reduces CoQ10 which traps lipid radicals; GPX4-independent
edges.keyInsight: Backup ferroptosis defense system

edges.mechanismDescription: Ferritin safely sequesters iron from labile pool
edges.keyInsight: Ferritin is the primary iron safety buffer

edges.mechanismDescription: More OPCs available for differentiation into mature OLs
edges.keyInsight: OPC pool determines remyelination capacity

edges.mechanismDescription: OPC differentiation produces new mature oligodendrocytes
edges.keyInsight: OPC → OL differentiation required for myelin repair

edges.mechanismDescription: Wnt signaling blocks OPC differentiation into mature OLs
edges.keyInsight: Complement → Wnt → OPC block → failed remyelination

edges.mechanismDescription: More OLs = more FTH1 export capacity to neurons
edges.keyInsight: OL loss impairs iron buffering supply to neurons

edges.mechanismDescription: OLs accumulate iron for myelin production; most iron-rich brain cells
edges.keyInsight: OL iron content >=3x neuronal (FELINE prediction)

edges.mechanismDescription: Glymphatic bulk flow carries iron species to CSF for clearance
edges.keyInsight: Primary iron exit route from brain

edges.mechanismDescription: Meningeal lymphatics drain CSF to deep cervical lymph nodes
edges.keyInsight: Downstream of glymphatic; declines with age

edges.mechanismDescription: CSE catalyzes H2S production
edges.keyInsight: CSE-/- sufficient for AD phenotype

edges.mechanismDescription: CBS catalyzes H2S production via transsulfuration
edges.keyInsight: CBS: another H2S source

edges.mechanismDescription: H2S is anti-ferroptotic via sulfhydration and iron chelation
edges.keyInsight: H2S/CSE pathway as antioxidant defense

edges.mechanismDescription: alpha1-Syntrophin anchors AQP4 to endfoot membrane
edges.keyInsight: Snta1-/- mice show AQP4 depolarization

edges.mechanismDescription: Endfoot structural integrity required for Fpn/Cp localization
edges.keyInsight: FELINE founding dual-failure hypothesis: endfoot loss = simultaneous AQP4 + Fpn/Cp loss

edges.mechanismDescription: Endfoot Fpn/Cp enables iron distribution from astrocytes to neurons/OLs
edges.keyInsight: Iron distribution (not export) via endfoot machinery

edges.mechanismDescription: Virchow-Robin spaces are the glymphatic conduit
edges.keyInsight: PVS patency required for glymphatic flow

edges.mechanismDescription: Sulforaphane activates TFEB nuclear translocation via mTOR-independent pathway (mild ROS)
edges.keyInsight: Creates new lysosomes via mTOR-INDEPENDENT TFEB activation

edges.mechanismDescription: Functional iron depletion impairs Fe-S cluster assembly for mitochondrial ETC
edges.keyInsight: Iron maldistribution → mitochondrial dysfunction via Fe-S cluster deficit

edges.mechanismDescription: Iron-loaded senescent cells produce SASP factors (IL-6, IL-1beta)
edges.keyInsight: Senescent cell iron drives inflammatory cascade via SASP

edges.mechanismDescription: Senescent cells have high labile iron; targetable by senolytics
edges.keyInsight: Senescent cells as iron reservoirs

edges.mechanismDescription: NMDA→NO→Dexras1→DMT1 iron import pathway; excitotoxicity-iron bridge
edges.keyInsight: Glutamate excitotoxicity drives iron import via DMT1

edges.mechanismDescription: Damaged mitochondria accumulate when mitophagy fails
edges.keyInsight: Mitochondrial damage contributes to autophagic cargo buildup

edges.mechanismDescription: PINK1-Parkin pathway clears damaged mitochondria
edges.keyInsight: Selective mitophagy requires functional lysosomes

edges.mechanismDescription: LMP releases lysosomal contents including escaped mtDNA
edges.keyInsight: Lysosomal breach releases danger signals

edges.mechanismDescription: Cytosolic mtDNA activates cGAS-STING and NLRP3 inflammasome
edges.keyInsight: Escaped mtDNA is a potent inflammatory trigger

edges.mechanismDescription: V-ATPase protein level determines proton pump activity
edges.keyInsight: V-ATPase expression → pump activity → lysosomal pH

edges.mechanismDescription: Active V-ATPase pumps protons to acidify lysosome
edges.keyInsight: V-ATPase activity is the master switch for lysosomal pH

edges.mechanismDescription: Alkalinized pH prevents Fe3+ reduction needed for DMT1/TRPML1 export
edges.keyInsight: pH > 5.5 → iron cannot be released from lysosomes

edges.mechanismDescription: Lysosomal iron release supplies functional iron pools
edges.keyInsight: Blocked release → functional iron deficiency

edges.mechanismDescription: BMP (PGRN-dependent) supports lipid degradation in lysosomes
edges.keyInsight: BMP deficiency contributes to lysosomal dysfunction

edges.mechanismDescription: PDGFRbeta expressed on pericytes; sPDGFRbeta in CSF = damage marker
edges.keyInsight: Pericyte health marker: PDGFRbeta levels track pericyte count

edges.mechanismDescription: Astrocyte-secreted hepcidin degrades endothelial ferroportin
edges.keyInsight: Brain hepcidin controls iron entry at BBB

edges.mechanismDescription: Hepcidin-mediated Fpn degradation restricts iron transport
edges.keyInsight: Hepcidin response restricts iron import at BBB

edges.mechanismDescription: Systemic hepcidin regulates serum iron availability for brain uptake
edges.keyInsight: Systemic vs brain hepcidin: distinct regulatory axes

edges.mechanismDescription: Insulin resistance, obesity, T2DM drive brain insulin resistance
edges.keyInsight: Metabolic risk factors converge on brain insulin resistance

edges.mechanismDescription: Chronic hyperinsulinemia → sustained PI3K-AKT → TSC1/2 inhibited → Rheb-GTP → mTORC1 disinhibited
edges.keyInsight: Insulin resistance removes the brake on mTORC1

edges.mechanismDescription: mTORC1 phosphorylates ULK1 at Ser757, blocking AMPK binding site
edges.keyInsight: Direct biochemical inhibition of autophagy initiation

edges.mechanismDescription: mTORC1 phosphorylates AMPK at Ser345, preventing lysosomal recruitment
edges.keyInsight: mTORC1 suppresses the energy sensor that would oppose it

edges.mechanismDescription: ULK1 inhibition blocks autophagy initiation → mitophagy cannot begin
edges.keyInsight: Autophagy initiation is the bottleneck for mitophagy

edges.mechanismDescription: Inhibited AMPK cannot activate ULK1 or recruit to damaged mitochondria
edges.keyInsight: Dual inhibition (ULK1 + AMPK) ensures mitophagy block

edges.mechanismDescription: mTORC1 phosphorylates S6K1 at Thr389 (hydrophobic motif); canonical substrate
edges.keyInsight: S6K1-pThr389 is standard readout for mTORC1 activity

edges.mechanismDescription: S6K1 phosphorylates IRS-1 at S307, S616, S636/639 → 14-3-3 binding, degradation
edges.keyInsight: S6K1 feeds back to inhibit insulin signaling

edges.mechanismDescription: IRS-1 serine phosphorylation reduces tyrosine phosphorylation, promotes degradation → impaired PI3K-AKT
edges.keyInsight: Closes the vicious cycle: mTORC1 → S6K1 → IRS1↓ → more insulin resistance

edges.mechanismDescription: Failed mitophagy → damaged mitochondria accumulate → ROS source
edges.keyInsight: Mitophagy failure feeds both Route 1B and Route 2

edges.mechanismDescription: APP/PSEN1/PSEN2 mutations increase Abeta42/40 ratio or total Abeta; deterministic EOAD
edges.keyInsight: Familial AD = production increase (vs sporadic = clearance decrease)

edges.mechanismDescription: Female sex → higher estrogen (pre-menopause); affects iron metabolism and immune responses
edges.keyInsight: Sex differences in AD risk are partly mediated by hormone levels

edges.mechanismDescription: PINK1/Parkin tag damaged mitochondria → autophagosome engulfs → mitophagosome
edges.keyInsight: Mitophagy step 1: damaged mito → mitophagosome

edges.mechanismDescription: Mitophagosome fuses with lysosome → autolysosome for degradation
edges.keyInsight: Mitophagy step 2: fusion (requires functional lysosome)

edges.mechanismDescription: Dysfunctional lysosomes fuse but cannot degrade cargo (pH >5.5, low cathepsin activity)
edges.keyInsight: Autolysosome forms but degradation fails → cargo persists

edges.mechanismDescription: DNase II inactive at high pH → mtDNA persists undegraded in autolysosome
edges.keyInsight: pH-dependent DNase II failure → mtDNA accumulation

edges.mechanismDescription: Undegraded mtDNA escapes through compromised lysosomal membrane (LMP) → cytosolic DAMP
edges.keyInsight: Route 1B endpoint: lysosomal mtDNA escape → cGAS-STING/NLRP3

edges.mechanismDescription: Damaged ETC → electron leak at Complex I/III → superoxide production
edges.keyInsight: Damaged mitochondria are ROS factories

edges.mechanismDescription: ROS oxidizes guanine → 8-oxo-dG; mtDNA vulnerable due to proximity to ETC and no histones
edges.keyInsight: Direct chemical damage: ROS + mtDNA → ox-mtDNA

edges.mechanismDescription: ROS damages Ca2+ handling proteins (MCU, NCLX) → impaired efflux → matrix Ca2+ accumulation
edges.keyInsight: ROS impairs Ca2+ homeostasis → overload

edges.mechanismDescription: Ca2+ binds mPTP regulatory site (CypD) → conformational change → pore opening
edges.keyInsight: Ca2+ is the canonical mPTP trigger; CsA (CypD inhibitor) blocks it

edges.mechanismDescription: mPTP opening → inner membrane stress → triggers VDAC oligomerization in outer membrane
edges.keyInsight: Inner membrane breach triggers outer membrane pore formation

edges.mechanismDescription: FEN1 endonuclease cleaves ox-mtDNA into 500-650 bp fragments; exit via mPTP/VDAC pores
edges.keyInsight: FEN1 cleavage + VDAC pores = ox-mtDNA export

edges.mechanismDescription: VDAC oligomers form large pores → allow ox-mtDNA fragment exit; VBIT-4 blocks release
edges.keyInsight: Physical pore = mtDNA exit route

edges.mechanismDescription: VDAC pores also allow non-oxidized mtDNA exit (less efficient than ox-mtDNA)
edges.keyInsight: Same pore, different cargo, different downstream sensor

edges.mechanismDescription: Oxidized mtDNA specifically binds NLRP3 → inflammasome assembly; non-ox does NOT activate NLRP3
edges.keyInsight: Oxidation state determines pathway: ox-mtDNA → NLRP3, non-ox → cGAS-STING

edges.mechanismDescription: Cytosolic dsDNA sensed by cGAS → cGAMP → STING → TBK1 → IRF3 → Type I IFN
edges.keyInsight: cGAS-STING drives aging-associated inflammation

edges.mechanismDescription: Route 1B: mtDNA escaped from lysosome also activates cGAS-STING; converges with Route 2
edges.keyInsight: Both routes converge on cGAS-STING

edges.mechanismDescription: Type I IFN from STING activates microglial inflammatory programs
edges.keyInsight: cGAS-STING → IFN → microglial activation

edges.mechanismDescription: Mitochondrial ROS attacks membrane PUFAs → lipid peroxidation
edges.keyInsight: Mito ROS feeds the ferroptosis cascade

edges.mechanismDescription: NLRP3-derived IL-1beta, IL-18 activate microglia via cytokine receptors → transcriptional reprogramming
edges.keyInsight: Inflammasome output drives microglial activation

edges.mechanismDescription: NF-kB signaling stabilizes HIF-1alpha under normoxic conditions (pseudohypoxia)
edges.keyInsight: Activation creates pseudohypoxia → metabolic reprogramming

edges.mechanismDescription: HIF-1alpha binds HREs in GLUT1, HK2, PFKFB3, LDHA, PDK1 promoters → Warburg effect
edges.keyInsight: HIF-1alpha is necessary and sufficient for glycolytic switch

edges.mechanismDescription: HIF-1alpha transcriptionally induces PKM2 expression
edges.keyInsight: Part of HIF-1alpha/PKM2 feed-forward loop

edges.mechanismDescription: Nuclear PKM2 dimer phosphorylates HIF-1alpha at Thr328 → enhanced transactivation
edges.keyInsight: Feed-forward: PKM2 amplifies HIF-1alpha → metabolic lock-in

edges.mechanismDescription: Glycolysis → excess acetyl-CoA/citrate → SREBP1 cleavage + nuclear translocation
edges.keyInsight: Metabolic shift provides lipogenic substrates

edges.mechanismDescription: SREBP1 induces FASN, ACC, SCD1, DGAT2 → de novo lipogenesis → TAG → lipid droplets
edges.keyInsight: SREBP1 is master switch for lipid droplet formation

edges.mechanismDescription: LDAM are defined by lipid droplet accumulation; LD load correlates with dysfunction
edges.keyInsight: Lipid droplet accumulation = LDAM state

edges.mechanismDescription: LDAM show ~50% reduced phagocytic capacity; DGAT2 degrader restores it
edges.keyInsight: LDAM dysfunction is treatable by targeting lipid accumulation

edges.mechanismDescription: LDAM maintain elevated IL-1beta, TNF-alpha, IL-6 even after stimulus removed
edges.keyInsight: LDAM create self-sustaining inflammatory state

edges.mechanismDescription: IL-1alpha + TNF-alpha + C1q from microglia → necessary and sufficient for A1 induction
edges.keyInsight: Triple knockout (IL-1alpha/TNF/C1q) abolishes A1 formation

edges.mechanismDescription: A1 astrocytes secrete unknown toxin killing neurons and oligodendrocytes
edges.keyInsight: A1 astrocytes are neurotoxic (gain of toxic function)

edges.mechanismDescription: LDAM reduced phagocytic capacity → cannot clear Abeta efficiently
edges.keyInsight: LDAM → clearance failure → Abeta accumulation (late-onset mechanism)

edges.mechanismDescription: LDAM cytokines perpetuate neuroinflammatory state → more microglial activation
edges.keyInsight: Self-amplifying feed-forward loop (LDAM → cytokines → more activation)

edges.mechanismDescription: NLRP3 → NF-kB → binds two NF-kB elements in BACE1 promoter → 2-3 fold upregulation
edges.keyInsight: Inflammation drives Abeta PRODUCTION (not just clearance failure)

edges.mechanismDescription: BACE1 cleaves APP at beta-site → C99. BACE1-/- mice have zero Abeta
edges.keyInsight: Rate-limiting step; BACE1 inhibitors failed due to Notch side effects

edges.mechanismDescription: Gamma-secretase cleaves C99 → Abeta40 (~90%) + Abeta42 (~10%); Abeta42 more aggregation-prone
edges.keyInsight: Sequential BACE1 → gamma-secretase processing

edges.mechanismDescription: Abeta monomers → dimers → oligomers (most toxic) → protofibrils → fibrils; nucleation-dependent
edges.keyInsight: Oligomers are THE toxic species (not monomers, not fibrils)

edges.mechanismDescription: Oligomers bind PrPc, mGluR5, NMDAR, alpha7-nAChR → synaptic dysfunction
edges.keyInsight: Multi-receptor binding explains diverse synaptic effects

edges.mechanismDescription: Oligomer binding inhibits LTP, disrupts calcium signaling, causes spine retraction
edges.keyInsight: Synaptotoxicity without cell death (early stage)

edges.mechanismDescription: Continued aggregation → protofibrils → mature fibrils → insoluble neuritic plaques
edges.keyInsight: Plaques may be less toxic than oligomers (sequestration)

edges.mechanismDescription: Microglia barrier around plaques compacts them; prevents oligomer release from plaque surface
edges.keyInsight: Compaction is PROTECTIVE; TREM2 loss → diffuse plaques → more toxic oligomers

edges.mechanismDescription: Stage 2 DAM (TREM2-dependent) localize to plaques and compact them
edges.keyInsight: DAM provide protective plaque barrier

edges.mechanismDescription: Abeta oligomers activate microglia via CD36, TREM2, RAGE, TLR2/4 receptors
edges.keyInsight: Feed-forward: Abeta → microglia → inflammation → BACE1 → more Abeta

edges.mechanismDescription: GSK3beta phosphorylates tau at Ser199, Ser202, Thr231, Ser396, Ser404; major tau kinase
edges.keyInsight: GSK3beta = primary tau kinase (more sites than any other)

edges.mechanismDescription: p38 MAPK phosphorylates tau at Ser199, Ser202, Thr205; activated by IL-1beta on neurons
edges.keyInsight: Direct link: inflammation → tau phosphorylation via p38

edges.mechanismDescription: PP2A accounts for >70% of tau dephosphorylation; 20-40% reduced in AD brain
edges.keyInsight: Net pTau = phosphorylation rate - dephosphorylation rate

edges.mechanismDescription: NLRP3 activation → IL-1beta → Wnt pathway inhibition → GSK3beta activation
edges.keyInsight: NLRP3 drives tau via GSK3beta (Ising 2019: NLRP3-/- reduces pTau)

edges.mechanismDescription: IL-1beta from NLRP3 activates neuronal p38 MAPK via IL-1R1
edges.keyInsight: Second pathway: NLRP3 → p38 → tau phosphorylation

edges.mechanismDescription: pTau detaches from microtubules → adopts pathological conformation → self-aggregates into beta-sheet PHF
edges.keyInsight: Hyperphosphorylation is the aggregation trigger

edges.mechanismDescription: PHF accumulation → mature NFTs; released upon neuronal death
edges.keyInsight: NFT burden correlates with cognition better than Abeta plaques

edges.mechanismDescription: Neurons and microglia package tau into exosomes; dead neurons also release directly
edges.keyInsight: Exosomal tau enables prion-like spreading

edges.mechanismDescription: Exosomal tau taken up by distant neurons → seeds templated misfolding (Braak staging)
edges.keyInsight: Mechanism for stereotypical entorhinal → hippocampal → neocortical spread

edges.mechanismDescription: Tau seeds template misfolding of native tau in recipient neurons → new aggregation cascade
edges.keyInsight: Prion-like propagation: self-amplifying in each new region

edges.mechanismDescription: LDAM phagocytose tau but cannot degrade it → package into exosomes → spread tau pathology
edges.keyInsight: Microglia are VECTORS for tau spreading (depletion halts propagation)

edges.mechanismDescription: Phagocytosed tau seeds activate NLRP3-ASC inflammasome → inflammation precedes tau pathology in new area
edges.keyInsight: Tau-NLRP3 feedforward loop drives disease progression

edges.mechanismDescription: NFT accumulation leads to neuronal death; ghost tangles remain after cell death
edges.keyInsight: NFTs are endpoint of intracellular tau pathology

edges.mechanismDescription: pTau feeds into the existing tau_aggregation node (general aggregation)
edges.keyInsight: Connects new detailed tau pathway to existing framework

edges.mechanismDescription: CBS catalyzes: homocysteine + serine → cystathionine; astrocyte-predominant
edges.keyInsight: First step: homocysteine → cystathionine (CBS)

edges.mechanismDescription: Homocysteine is CBS substrate; elevated homocysteine = AD risk factor
edges.keyInsight: Elevated homocysteine in AD reflects pathway dysfunction

edges.mechanismDescription: CSE catalyzes: cystathionine → cysteine + alpha-ketobutyrate. CSE-/- has dramatically reduced H2S
edges.keyInsight: CSE is the key neuron-predominant enzyme

edges.mechanismDescription: Cysteine is rate-limiting substrate for glutathione synthesis
edges.keyInsight: Cysteine supply determines antioxidant capacity

edges.mechanismDescription: H2S modifies protein cysteine thiols (-SH) to persulfides (-SSH); typically protective
edges.keyInsight: H2S → persulfidation is a major protective PTM

edges.mechanismDescription: H2S sulfhydrates GSK3beta → INHIBITS kinase activity → reduced tau phosphorylation
edges.keyInsight: Counter-mechanism to NLRP3-driven GSK3beta activation

edges.mechanismDescription: Sulfhydrated GSK3beta is enzymatically inactive; H2S donors reduce pTau at PHF-1, AT8 epitopes
edges.keyInsight: H2S pathway is the BRAKE on tau pathology

edges.mechanismDescription: Oxidized mtDNA (8-oxo-dG containing) specifically binds NLRP3 LRR domain and triggers inflammasome assembly. Non-oxidized mtDNA does NOT activate NLRP3
Cytosolic dsDNA (including non-oxidized mtDNA) is sensed by cGAS → 2\
Functional PINK1/Parkin mitophagy clears damaged mitochondria BEFORE mtDNA can escape → prevents mtDNA release
Tau aggregates also activate NLRP3 (creates feedforward loop)
Caspase-1 cleaves pro-IL-18 to mature IL-18; IL-18 promotes IFN-γ and enhances neuroinflammation
IL-18 induces IFN-γ production and amplifies inflammatory cascades; elevated in AD brain and CSF
Type I interferon signaling (from STING, mtDNA) induces interferon-stimulated genes; ISG signature elevated in AD microglia
ISGs drive microglial activation and proinflammatory phenotype; IRM (interferon-responsive microglia) are distinct subtype
C1q required for A1 astrocyte induction (with IL-1α + TNF)
TREM2-positive microglia compact plaques into dense cores, reducing toxicity by sequestering oligomers
Aβ oligomers activate classical complement cascade → C1q/C3 tag synapses → microglia CR3-mediated pruning. Synapse loss occurs BEFORE plaques form.
Aβ oligomers inhibit PP2A via SET/I2PP2A upregulation; PP2A is the major tau phosphatase (>70%)
Tau is released via exosomes; exosomal tau is more aggregation-prone and seeds misfolding in recipient cells
Homocysteine is substrate for CBS (cystathionine β-synthase); elevated homocysteine is AD risk factor
Cystathionine is substrate for CSE (cystathionine γ-lyase)
Cysteine is rate-limiting substrate for glutathione (GSH) synthesis
Sulfhydrated GSK3β is inactive → reduced tau phosphorylation at Ser396, Ser404

C1q increases ~300-fold in aged brain (primarily from microglia). MECHANISM UNKNOWN per Stephan 2013. Likely intermediates: microglial senescence, oxidative stress, or myelin debris accumulation. Reactivation of developmental pruning pathway.
Synapse density is STRONGEST anatomical correlate of cognitive function - better than plaques or tangles
