Counterintuitively, functional iron deficiency (low labile Fe²⁺) may initially PROTECT from ferroptosis, but impairs mitochondrial function
REST nuclear localization declines with age in vulnerable neurons
Lipid-laden astrocytes contribute to neuroinflammation via altered lipid signaling
TREM2 signaling required for transition from homeostatic to DAM phenotype
Stage 1 DAM → Stage 2 DAM requires TREM2 signaling
R47H and other AD-risk variants reduce TREM2 surface expression/signaling
ADAM10/17 cleave TREM2 ectodomain → soluble TREM2 (sTREM2) in CSF
Aging promotes senescent TREM2+ microglia with SASP-like phenotype; these may be harmful
Senescent TREM2+ microglia secrete inflammatory factors and fail to maintain tissue homeostasis
DAM Stage 2 microglia have enhanced phagocytic capacity (TREM2-dependent). DAM upregulate lipid metabolism genes (APOE, LIPA, LPL) and phagocytic receptors to clear debris.
Hyperphosphorylated tau at T217 is actively secreted from neurons and released upon neuronal damage. pTau217 in plasma closely tracks tau PET signal and predicts progression.

OPCs differentiate into mature myelinating oligodendrocytes. Process requires: (1) exit from cell cycle, (2) expression of MBP/MOG/PLP1, (3) cholesterol availability for myelin synthesis. Declines with age due to OPC senescence, LINGO1↑, Nogo receptor activation.

A1 reactive astrocytes secrete saturated lipids that are toxic to oligodendrocytes. This is one mechanism of white matter pathology in AD. A1 astrocytes are induced by C1q + IL-1α + TNF-α from activated microglia.

OPCs become exhausted and senescent with age. Mechanisms include: (1) replicative senescence, (2) epigenetic changes reducing differentiation capacity, (3) accumulation of myelin debris inhibiting OPC function. Results in declining remyelination capacity.

Pro-inflammatory cytokines including IL-1β increase cellular stress on lysosomes. NF-κB activation downstream of IL-1R impairs TFEB nuclear translocation, reducing lysosomal biogenesis. Chronic inflammation creates a vicious cycle of lysosomal dysfunction.

Type I IFN signaling impairs mitochondrial quality control. ISG expression alters cellular metabolism and reduces PINK1/Parkin-mediated mitophagy efficiency. This completes the mtDNA-STING inflammation loop.

Synapse loss releases tonic microglial inhibition. Neurons express CX3CL1 (fractalkine) which binds microglial CX3CR1 to maintain quiescence. Synapse loss reduces this inhibitory signal, promoting microglial activation.
Intact BBB protects OPCs from peripheral insults. BBB breakdown exposes OPCs to serum factors (fibrinogen, albumin, IgG) that inhibit OPC differentiation and survival.
OLs preferentially accumulate iron due to high metabolic demand for myelination. Iron required for cholesterol synthesis, cytochrome oxidase, and fatty acid desaturation.
Biological sex determines baseline estrogen levels; menopause causes precipitous decline in women
Estrogen provides neuroprotection via anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, neurotrophic effects; loss increases vulnerability
Biological sex determines testosterone levels; higher in males; testosterone may have neuroprotective effects
Testosterone reduces neuroinflammation and Aβ accumulation; low testosterone associated with AD risk in men
Estrogen loss in menopause → increased visceral adipose tissue (VAT); VAT produces inflammatory cytokines
VAT secretes IL-6, TNF-α, leptin → systemic and neuroinflammation; links metabolic syndrome to AD risk
Depleted lysosome pool → insufficient degradation capacity → pH rises >5.5, ↓cathepsin activity, cargo accumulation. NOTE: This is an inverse relationship - low lysosome stock increases dysfunction
edges.keyInsight: PANTHOS pattern - autolysosomes fail to acidify, becoming Aβ storage depots

edges.mechanismDescription: Phospho-inhibited AMPK cannot activate ULK1; ULK1 remains phosphorylated/inhibited by mTORC1
edges.keyInsight: AMPK-ULK1 axis is critical for autophagy initiation

edges.mechanismDescription: Reduced degradation capacity (↓cathepsins, ↓v-ATPase, high pH) → cargo builds up. Stock-flow: inflow unchanged, outflow reduced
Incomplete degradation of proteins/lipids → oxidation → cross-linking → lipofuscin granules (irreversible)
edges.keyInsight: Lipofuscin formation is IRREVERSIBLE - no known degradation pathway

edges.mechanismDescription: Lipofuscin directly destabilizes lysosomal membrane through physical/oxidative damage → permeabilization
edges.keyInsight: Also triggers atypical necroptosis (RIPK1/RIPK3/MLKL independent)

edges.mechanismDescription: Lysosomal membrane permeabilization → cathepsins (including active cathepsin B) leak into cytosol
Cytosolic cathepsin B promotes NLRP3-ASC complex assembly
edges.keyInsight: Establishes LMP → cathepsin B → NLRP3 as general danger-sensing mechanism

edges.mechanismDescription: DNase II requires pH <5 for activity. Lysosomal pH >5.5 → DNase II inactive → mtDNA persists undegraded in autolysosome
edges.keyInsight: LANDMARK PAPER - established mtDNA escape as inflammatory trigger

edges.mechanismDescription: Cytosolic mtDNA (CpG-rich, bacterial-like) sensed by cGAS → 2\
edges.keyInsight: Brain-specific: STING activation triggers reactive microglial transcriptional states

edges.mechanismDescription: NLRP3 recruits ASC → ASC oligomerizes → recruits pro-caspase-1 → caspase-1 auto-activates
edges.keyInsight: LANDMARK PAPER - First demonstration that NLRP3-/- protects from AD pathology

edges.mechanismDescription: Caspase-1 cleaves pro-IL-1β (31 kDa) to mature IL-1β (17 kDa)
cGAS produces 2\
STING → TBK1 → IRF3 phosphorylation → IRF3 dimerization → nuclear translocation → IFN-α/β transcription
NLRP3 signaling activates GSK-3β (tau kinase)
edges.keyInsight: LANDMARK PAPER - NLRP3 → tau hyperphosphorylation

edges.mechanismDescription: NLRP3 signaling inhibits PP2A (major tau phosphatase)

GSK-3β phosphorylates tau at multiple pathological sites: pSer199, pSer202/Thr205 (AT8), pThr231, pSer396/Ser404 (PHF-1). Priming at Ser-404 enables phosphorylation cascade. Lithium inhibits GSK-3β competitively vs Mg²⁺.
edges.keyInsight: GSK-3β is the principal tau kinase; lithium IC50 ≈ 1-2 mM overlaps therapeutic range

edges.mechanismDescription: PP2A is the major tau phosphatase; reduced activity → phospho-tau accumulates
Aβ oligomers are phagocytosed by microglia → lysosomal rupture → NLRP3 activation
Cytokines and DAMPs activate microglia from homeostatic state
Microglial activation triggers NF-κB nuclear translocation
NF-κB signaling stabilizes HIF-1α under normoxia
Lipid droplet accumulation defines LDAM phenotype
edges.keyInsight: LANDMARK PAPER - Discovery of LDAM phenotype

edges.mechanismDescription: LDAM phenotype characterized by defective phagocytosis
LDAM microglia produce C1q
Activated microglia produce IL-1α
IL-1α required for A1 astrocyte induction (with TNF + C1q)
edges.keyInsight: LANDMARK PAPER - IL-1α + TNF + C1q are NECESSARY and SUFFICIENT for A1 induction

edges.mechanismDescription: TNF required for A1 astrocyte induction (with IL-1α + C1q)
mTORC1 stabilizes HIF-1α via increased translation (4E-BP1 phosphorylation) and decreased degradation (reduced PHD activity). This occurs INDEPENDENT of hypoxia in inflammatory microglia.
edges.keyInsight: mTOR-HIF1α axis validated in human AD tissue. Old female microglia show STRONGER shift.

edges.mechanismDescription: NF-κB → IRG1 (ACOD1) transcription. IRG1 diverts aconitate from TCA cycle to produce itaconate. Key metabolic adaptation to chronic inflammation.
edges.keyInsight: Mills 2018 Nature: IRG1 is master regulator of inflammatory metabolic reprogramming

edges.mechanismDescription: LDAM phenotype + iron loading + lysosomal dysfunction = "super-seeder" state. Iron impairs lysosomal function, worsening Aβ aggregation instead of clearance.

edges.keyInsight: PARADIGM REVERSAL: Microglia seed plaques, not clear them. Explains why TREM2 agonists may be counterproductive.

edges.mechanismDescription: IL-1β is a major driver of neuroinflammation; activates microglia and astrocytes
Type I interferons drive ISG expression and neuroinflammatory state
Ferroptotic cell death reduces neuronal population
Neuronal dysfunction impairs synaptic transmission and cognitive function
Misfolded tau forms PHF seeds that template further aggregation
BBB breakdown allows peripheral immune cell infiltration and plasma protein leakage → neuroinflammation
Hypolipidated APOE4 is less effective at promoting Aβ clearance via LRP1 and BBB transport
Progressive neuronal dysfunction leads to death; AD reduces life expectancy by 4-8 years after diagnosis
Tau pathology correlates with regional brain atrophy better than amyloid; neuronal loss → volume loss
edges.keyInsight: Brain volume loss correlates with tau, not amyloid

edges.mechanismDescription: Regional brain volumes correlate with cognitive function; hippocampal volume predicts memory
CSF Aβ42 decreases as brain Aβ accumulates (sequestered into plaques); CSF Aβ42/40 ratio is diagnostic
CSF p-tau181, p-tau217, p-tau231 are elevated in AD; reflect neuronal tau pathology
edges.keyInsight: p-tau is most specific CSF biomarker for AD

edges.mechanismDescription: Lysosomal de-acidification → autolysosomes become Aβ production sites (PANTHOS). Aβ produced INSIDE neurons, not just secreted
edges.keyInsight: PARADIGM SHIFT: Aβ is produced in dysfunctional autolysosomes, not just at plasma membrane

edges.mechanismDescription: Aggregated tau acts as seed, templating misfolding of normal tau in connected neurons
edges.keyInsight: Tau spreading follows neural connectivity (Braak staging)

edges.mechanismDescription: Tau aggregates disrupt axonal transport, impair proteostasis, cause synaptic dysfunction
H₂S sulfhydrates GSK3β at Cys218 → inhibits kinase activity → reduces tau phosphorylation
edges.keyInsight: CSE loss → ↓H₂S → GSK3β hyperactive → tau pathology

edges.mechanismDescription: CSE provides cysteine via transsulfuration; cysteine is rate-limiting for glutathione (GSH) synthesis
edges.keyInsight: Links transsulfuration to ferroptosis protection

edges.mechanismDescription: CSE expression/activity declines with age → H₂S and cysteine depletion
IL-1β and TNF-α from neuroinflammation activate p38 MAPK, a tau kinase
Aggregated tau assembles into paired helical filaments (PHF) as canonical aggregation product

Neuronal hyperactivity causes tau to mislocalize from axon to somatodendritic compartment. CRITICAL: Dampening activity with TTX or CNQX PREVENTED tau missorting, proving hyperactivity is UPSTREAM, not downstream, of tau pathology. Mechanism: Activity-dependent Ca influx may trigger tau release from microtubules.
edges.keyInsight: CAUSAL PROOF: Activity precedes tau changes; blocking activity prevents tau pathology

edges.mechanismDescription: C3 cleavage deposits iC3b on synapses → recognized by microglial CR3 (CD11b/CD18)
Aβ oligomers induce C1q expression/deposition on synapses
BDNF promotes spine formation and stabilization. TrkB signaling activates Rac1/Cdc42 → actin polymerization → spine morphogenesis. Critical for learning-related structural plasticity.
Aβ oligomers cause rapid spine retraction within hours. Involves calcineurin activation, AMPA receptor internalization, and cofilin-mediated actin depolymerization. Early event in AD pathology.
IL-6/IL-1β → JAK2 → STAT3 → HAMP promoter → hepcidin transcription. Links neuroinflammation to iron maldistribution
Fe²⁺ + H₂O₂ → Fe³⁺ + OH• + OH⁻ (Fenton reaction). Hydroxyl radicals attack membrane PUFA → lipid peroxides
edges.keyInsight: DISCOVERY paper for ferroptosis (Dixon 2012)

edges.mechanismDescription: GSH is essential cofactor for GPX4. GSH depletion → GPX4 inactive → ferroptosis
Chronological aging drives accumulation of senescent cells through replicative exhaustion, oxidative damage, and iron accumulation
edges.keyInsight: Age is the primary driver of cellular senescence

edges.mechanismDescription: Senescent cells secrete pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, IL-8, IL-1β, TNF-α) as part of SASP phenotype
edges.keyInsight: Targeted ferroptosis in senescent cells is therapeutic strategy

edges.mechanismDescription: Ferroportin degradation blocks iron export → total brain iron increases despite cytosolic iron deficiency
edges.keyInsight: Brain iron accumulates with age; accelerated in AD

edges.mechanismDescription: Accumulated iron is stored in ferritin cages (Fe³⁺); ferritin increases to detoxify iron but TRAPS it
Iron locked in ferritin → cytosolic labile iron pool depleted → functional iron deficiency despite total iron increase
Iron trapped in lysosomes cannot be released → functional iron deficiency in cytosol
Iron accumulation directly drives cellular senescence; iron chelation reverses senescent phenotype
edges.keyInsight: Iron chelation or ferroptosis induction in senescent cells is therapeutic

edges.mechanismDescription:
PARADOX: Microglial senescence shows OPPOSITE volume relationship.
      Lund 2026: 23/25 correlations POSITIVE (more senescence → larger volume).

      Interpretation: Senescent microglia may help maintain brain structure,
      possibly through reduced inflammatory activity or support functions.

      This contrasts sharply with excitatory neurons where senescence → atrophy.
edges.keyInsight: Cell-type matters: microglia senescence may be protective

edges.mechanismDescription: Domain interaction impairs ABCA1-mediated lipidation → hypolipidated APOE4
REST coordinates with Nrf2 in stress resistance program
Nrf2 induces GPX4 and other ferroptosis-protective genes
APOE4 impairs cholesterol trafficking → cholesterol trapped in endo-lysosomes (NPC-like phenotype)
edges.keyInsight: APOE4 mimics Niemann-Pick disease phenotype

edges.mechanismDescription: Cholesterol accumulation in lysosomes impairs lysosomal function and autophagy
APOE4 impairs lipid transport → lipid droplet accumulation in astrocytes
edges.keyInsight: APOE4 astrocytes show lipid metabolism dysfunction

edges.mechanismDescription: Aging leads to nuclear REST depletion in vulnerable neurons; more severe in AD
REST depletion de-represses apoptotic/neurodegenerative genes; neurons lose stress resistance
edges.keyInsight: REST preservation is potential therapeutic target

edges.mechanismDescription: Myelin breakdown stresses oligodendrocytes; compensatory remyelination attempts fail under chronic sulfatide depletion
DAM microglia form protective barrier around plaques, compacting them to reduce toxicity
R47H and other AD-risk variants reduce TREM2 surface expression/signaling
TREM2 loss-of-function variants block Stage 1 → Stage 2 DAM transition, locking microglia in dysfunctional state
Blocked DAM transition prevents microglia from acquiring phagocytic phenotype needed for Aβ clearance
edges.keyInsight: TREM2 variants impair protective microglial responses

edges.mechanismDescription: CypA → NF-κB → MMP9 transcription and activation
MMP9 degrades basement membrane and tight junction proteins → BBB leakage
Glymphatic system clears Aβ and other waste; impairment → Aβ accumulation
MMP9 damages pericytes; sPDGFRβ in CSF is biomarker of pericyte injury
edges.keyInsight: CSF sPDGFRβ predicts cognitive decline in APOE4 carriers

edges.mechanismDescription: Meningeal lymphatic function declines with age; impairs CNS waste drainage
edges.keyInsight: Lymphatic rejuvenation may be therapeutic target

edges.mechanismDescription: Meningeal lymphatics drain glymphatic efflux; lymphatic dysfunction impairs overall clearance
Efficient ISF Aβ clearance reduces oligomer accumulation
Aging disrupts DAPC anchoring → AQP4 redistributes from endfeet to parenchymal membrane. AQP4 polarization ratio declines with age, contributing to 80-90% glymphatic decline
Sleep deprivation impairs AQP4 polarization maintenance. AQP4 SNPs moderate the relationship between sleep and brain Aβ burden in humans
edges.keyInsight: HUMAN EVIDENCE: 1 night sleep deprivation → increased Aβ in hippocampus/thalamus

edges.mechanismDescription: Astrocyte endfeet ensheath >99% of cerebral blood vessels and support BBB integrity via: (1) Laminin/dystroglycan signaling to pericytes; (2) Release of sonic hedgehog (SHH) supporting tight junctions; (3) Physical barrier limiting perivascular infiltration
edges.keyInsight: Endfoot coverage essential for BBB maintenance

edges.mechanismDescription: Astrocyte endfeet form the glymphatic "gate" enabling CSF-ISF exchange at the perivascular space. AQP4 on endfeet mediates water flux; endfoot swelling/retraction alters perivascular space geometry

A1 reactive astrocyte conversion causes endfoot retraction and structural damage. A1 astrocytes lose normal astrocyte functions including endfoot maintenance, AQP4 polarization, and BBB support. C3 complement released by A1s may damage endfeet
edges.keyInsight: A1 astrocyte conversion damages the endfoot-BBB-glymphatic interface

edges.mechanismDescription: Astrocyte endfeet contain lysosomes that process perivascular debris and Aβ. Endfoot damage impairs local lysosomal clearance capacity. Astrocyte lysosomes also process material cleared via glymphatic system
edges.keyInsight: Endfoot lysosomes are part of the brain waste clearance system

edges.mechanismDescription: Astrocyte endfeet express α7 nicotinic acetylcholine receptors (nAChRs). Cholinergic signaling via α7 nAChRs supports endfoot function and BBB integrity through the splenic anti-inflammatory pathway (reduced systemic TNF-α/IL-1β → preserved tight junctions)
edges.keyInsight: Cholinergic system supports BBB via α7 nAChR on astrocyte endfeet

edges.mechanismDescription: Aging causes astrocyte endfoot retraction from blood vessels, contributing to BBB breakdown and AQP4 depolarization. DAPC components decline with age, loosening AQP4 anchoring

LRP1 is the primary receptor for Aβ clearance at the BBB. ApoE4 reduces LRP1 expression and function, leading to impaired Aβ transcytosis from brain to blood. This establishes BBB dysfunction as UPSTREAM of amyloid accumulation.
edges.keyInsight: BBB clearance failure PRECEDES amyloid accumulation

edges.mechanismDescription: BBB breakdown reduces Aβ clearance via two mechanisms: (1) reduced LRP1-mediated efflux, (2) peripheral proteins entering brain can nucleate Aβ aggregation. BBB dysfunction is detectable before amyloid PET positivity.
edges.keyInsight: Vascular dysfunction is UPSTREAM of amyloid pathology

edges.mechanismDescription: BBB breakdown impairs LRP1-mediated Aβ transcytosis from brain to blood. Pericyte loss → reduced Aβ clearance across endothelium.
edges.keyInsight: BBB dysfunction impairs Aβ clearance and predicts cognitive decline

edges.mechanismDescription: Basal forebrain cholinergic neurons (nbM) are selectively vulnerable to tau pathology
Loss of cholinergic neurons → reduced ACh synthesis and release
ACh is essential for attention, learning, memory. Basis for AChEI therapy
edges.keyInsight: AChEIs (donepezil, rivastigmine) provide symptomatic benefit

edges.mechanismDescription: Neuroinflammation damages oligodendrocytes and myelin → white matter hyperintensities on MRI
edges.keyInsight: White matter pathology is early AD feature

edges.mechanismDescription: White matter pathology leads to myelin breakdown and oligodendrocyte loss
Myelin breakdown slows conduction velocity and disrupts neural circuits
edges.keyInsight: Myelin-focused AD hypothesis: late-myelinating regions most vulnerable

edges.mechanismDescription: Mature oligodendrocytes maintain myelin sheaths. When OLs die (A1 astrocyte toxicity, APOE4 cholesterol failure), myelin degenerates. Loss of mature OLs → myelin breakdown → axonal dysfunction.
edges.keyInsight: OL death precedes myelin loss; myelin is stable short-term even without OLs

edges.mechanismDescription: Remyelination capacity declines with age due to: (1) OPC exhaustion/senescence, (2) ↑LINGO1 inhibiting OPC differentiation, (3) altered macrophage/microglia clearance of myelin debris, (4) cholesterol metabolism changes. This creates a window where demyelination becomes irreversible.
edges.keyInsight: The 22-year prodrome suggests white matter pathology is an early, potentially modifiable target

edges.mechanismDescription: Myelin breakdown leads to detectable white matter pathology on MRI (↓fractional anisotropy, ↑mean diffusivity, white matter hyperintensities). This occurs BEFORE gray matter changes and predicts cognitive decline.
edges.keyInsight: WM pathology is an early biomarker and potential intervention target

edges.mechanismDescription: White matter pathology disrupts long-range projections from basal forebrain cholinergic neurons to cortex and hippocampus. The cholinergic system has the longest axons in the brain and is particularly vulnerable to WM damage. This may explain why cholinergic deficits appear early in AD.
edges.keyInsight: WM damage may cause cholinergic dysfunction by disconnection, not just BFCN death

edges.mechanismDescription: BMP (bis(monoacylglycero)phosphate) is a lysosome-specific lipid that facilitates lipid degradation by activating acid sphingomyelinase. ↑BMP is a compensatory response to lysosomal stress - the cell attempts to enhance lipid catabolism. BMP elevation is seen in lysosomal storage disorders and in AD brains.
edges.keyInsight: BMP is a biomarker for lysosomal stress; elevation may be compensatory

edges.mechanismDescription: Myelin debris inhibits OPC differentiation through Nogo receptor activation. Chronic demyelination exhausts the OPC pool. This completes the OPC-myelin-white matter degeneration loop.
edges.keyInsight: Myelin debris inhibits OPC differentiation, perpetuating demyelination

edges.mechanismDescription: GPX4 is the master brake on OL ferroptosis. GPX4 reduces lipid peroxides in OL membranes; when GPX4 is depleted, high-iron OLs undergo ferroptotic death.
edges.keyInsight: GPX4 is THE master brake on OL ferroptosis

edges.mechanismDescription: High OL iron content + declining GPX4 with age → Fenton chemistry → lipid peroxidation in PUFA-rich myelin membranes → ferroptotic OL death.
edges.keyInsight: OL ferroptosis is a direct mechanism of demyelination

edges.mechanismDescription: Myelin debris activates astrocytes toward A1 phenotype via TLR2/4 signaling. Creates bidirectional amplification loop: A1 astrocytes kill OLs → myelin breakdown → more A1 activation.
edges.keyInsight: Myelin debris activates astrocytes → bidirectional amplification loop

edges.mechanismDescription: PS1/PS2 mutations increase ER-mitochondria contacts (MAM) → enhanced Ca²⁺ transfer
edges.keyInsight: LANDMARK: MAM hypothesis of AD (Area-Gomez)

edges.mechanismDescription: MAM hyperconnectivity → excessive Ca²⁺ transfer from ER to mitochondria via IP3R-VDAC-MCU
Excessive Ca²⁺ transfer → mitochondrial Ca²⁺ overload → mPTP opening
γ-secretase is enriched at MAM; MAM expansion increases Aβ production
PS1 mutations cause ER Ca²⁺ leak dysfunction; may cause ER Ca²⁺ overfilling or depletion
edges.keyInsight: ER calcium dysregulation precedes Aβ pathology in fAD

edges.mechanismDescription: ER Ca²⁺ stores drive Ca²⁺ flux to mitochondria via MAM
Elevated FSH (post-menopausal) may directly contribute to neurodegeneration via C/EBPβ-δ-secretase pathway
edges.keyInsight: FSH blockade is potential therapeutic target for women

edges.mechanismDescription: X chromosome carries lysosomal genes (ATP6AP2, SLC9A7, ATP6AP1, LAMP2); XX females have dosage compensation but may have advantage
edges.keyInsight: X-linked escape may protect females via enhanced lysosomal function

edges.mechanismDescription: Women with AD show paradoxical negative iron-ferritin correlation (opposite of normal); suggests iron storage mechanism failure
edges.keyInsight: Female-specific iron dysregulation may explain higher AD prevalence

edges.mechanismDescription: Iron storage failure → increased labile iron → ferroptosis vulnerability; female-specific pathway
Endfoot retraction creates gap between blood vessel and glia limitans → perivascular space enlarges → visible on MRI as white matter hyperintensities around vessels.
edges.keyInsight: Earliest MRI biomarker of endfoot dysfunction - 18 years before symptoms

edges.mechanismDescription: Endfoot fragmentation disrupts DAPC anchoring complex → AQP4 redistributes from endfeet to parenchymal membrane → loss of polarized water transport.
edges.keyInsight: Connects structural endfoot damage to glymphatic dysfunction

edges.mechanismDescription: Endfoot damage → AQP4 depolarization → loss of polarized CSF-ISF exchange → impaired glymphatic clearance of Aβ and tau.
edges.keyInsight: Endfoot damage creates vicious cycle: impaired clearance → more Aβ → more damage

edges.mechanismDescription: Ammonia causes endocytosis of mature APP from astrocyte plasma membrane and targets it to the endoplasmic reticulum (not lysosome). This is a novel amyloidogenic pathway distinct from neuronal APP processing.

APP in astrocyte ER is cleaved by resident BACE1 (β-secretase) and presenilin-1 (γ-secretase) to produce Aβ42 specifically — the principal component of senile plaques. First evidence that ammonia directly induces AD pathogenesis.

Excess extracellular glutamate triggers excitotoxic cascade:
      1. NMDAR overactivation → Ca²⁺ influx
      2. Mitochondrial calcium overload → ROS production
      3. Calpain activation → cytoskeletal damage
      4. Neuronal death (necrotic or delayed apoptotic)
Gantenerumab reduced amyloid by 66 Centiloids but only 28% reached amyloid-negative status (vs 68-80% for approved drugs). Subcutaneous dosing limited brain exposure.
edges.keyInsight: Target engagement threshold matters more than direction - 28% amyloid-negative was insufficient

edges.mechanismDescription: Latrepirdine claimed mitochondrial, NMDA, 5-HT6, and H1 mechanisms from in vitro data, but none were validated in humans. $725M Phase 3 showed zero efficacy.
edges.keyInsight: Multiple weak mechanisms ≠ one strong effect; polypharmacology without target validation fails

edges.mechanismDescription: FAILED HYPOTHESIS: Anti-inflammatory → cognition. Propentofylline APPROVED for canine cognitive dysfunction but FAILED human AD trials at 18 months. This documents a NEGATIVE result - targeting neuroinflammation directly did NOT improve cognition in humans, possibly because intermediates (synaptic loss, neuronal death) were already established.
edges.keyInsight: NEGATIVE RESULT: Targeting neuroinflammation alone is insufficient once downstream damage (synaptic loss, neuronal death) is established

edges.mechanismDescription:
Ferroptotic cell death releases polyanion cofactors:
      - RNA released from dying cells
      - DNA fragments
      - HSPGs from disrupted membranes
      These stabilize tau and Aβ aggregation.
edges.keyInsight: Cell death creates permissive environment for aggregation

edges.mechanismDescription:
Fichou 2018 (PNAS): Tau seeds are "exhausted after one generation" without cofactor supplementation.
      Polyanion cofactors (HSPGs, RNA) are REQUIRED for sustained tau aggregation.
      NOT truly prion-like self-templating.
edges.keyInsight: Cofactors are REQUIRED, not optional - tau does not self-template

edges.mechanismDescription: Polyanions also promote Aβ fibril formation and stability.

Yuan 2016: Microglia form protective barriers around plaques.
      TREM2-dependent recruitment → compact fibrillar core → sequester toxic oligomers.
      Dense-core plaques are PROTECTIVE, not harmful.
edges.keyInsight: Dense-core plaques = successful containment

edges.mechanismDescription: Estrogen acts via ERα/ERβ receptors to increase BDNF, promote spine formation, reduce APP processing, and enhance mitochondrial function.
Hormone replacement therapy restores estrogen levels. Critical window hypothesis: HRT within 5 years of menopause may be neuroprotective; later may be harmful.
edges.keyInsight: Timing matters: early HRT may protect, late HRT may harm

edges.mechanismDescription: Estrogen increases BDNF expression and promotes dendritic spine formation, enhancing synaptic plasticity.
Estrogen reduces amyloidogenic APP processing, decreasing Aβ production.
Chronic cortisol elevation enhances tau hyperphosphorylation and promotes tau pathology.
Chronic cortisol causes hippocampal atrophy and synaptic dysfunction, impairing plasticity.
Melatonin regulates circadian rhythm, which controls sleep-dependent glymphatic clearance. Also has direct antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties.

APOE4 impairs lipid metabolism and iron homeostasis through altered apolipoprotein-mediated iron transport and reduced ferroxidase activity, leading to accumulation of brain iron. APOE4 also disrupts the blood-brain barrier integrity and reduces clearance of iron-laden lipoproteins, promoting iron accumulation in neural tissue.

APOE4 isoform affects cholesterol metabolism and transport in the brain through differences in lipid binding and receptor interactions compared to APOE2/E3. APOE4 carriers show altered brain cholesterol homeostasis, including changes in cholesterol uptake, efflux, and local synthesis regulation.

Aging impairs cellular stress response systems including the Nrf2-ARE pathway, reducing Nrf2 activity and its capacity to mount antioxidant and cytoprotective responses. This decline in Nrf2 signaling contributes to accumulation of oxidative stress and neurodegeneration characteristic of aging and Alzheimer's disease.

Aging is associated with decreased AMPK activity through multiple mechanisms including reduced NAD+ levels, impaired mitochondrial function, and altered metabolic signaling. This decline in AMPK activity during aging contributes to reduced autophagy, mitochondrial quality control, and metabolic homeostasis that underlies neurodegeneration.

Aging impairs mitochondrial function and antioxidant defense systems, leading to increased generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) in mitochondria. Age-related accumulation of damaged mitochondria and reduced capacity for mitophagy further exacerbates mitochondrial ROS production.

Aging is associated with progressive decline in cellular antioxidant defenses, including reduced glutathione (GSH) synthesis and increased GSH consumption, leading to diminished cellular glutathione levels and impaired redox homeostasis.

Aging impairs antioxidant defense systems (including reduced expression of catalase, SOD, and Nrf2-dependent pathways) and increases mitochondrial dysfunction, leading to elevated reactive oxygen species (ROS) production and accumulation of oxidative stress. Age-related decline in cellular maintenance mechanisms and energy metabolism further exacerbates oxidative damage in the aging brain.

Aging is associated with progressive accumulation of iron in the brain, particularly in regions vulnerable to neurodegeneration. Age-related decline in iron homeostasis mechanisms, including reduced ferroportin expression and impaired iron export, leads to iron accumulation in neural tissue.

Aging is associated with increased basal p38 MAPK activation in neuronal and glial cells, contributing to neuroinflammation and neurodegeneration. Age-related accumulation of cellular stress, oxidative damage, and impaired proteostasis directly trigger p38 MAPK phosphorylation through stress-response pathways.

Aging leads to increased oxidative stress, mitochondrial dysfunction, and cellular hypoxia, which activate HIF-1alpha stabilization as a compensatory response. HIF-1alpha stabilization increases in aged tissues due to accumulation of damage and impaired prolyl hydroxylase function.

Aging-associated decline in immune system function, reduced blood-brain barrier integrity, increased circulating damage-associated molecular patterns (DAMPs), and age-related accumulation of senescent cells directly trigger microglial activation and astrocytic responses, increasing neuroinflammation. Age-related impairment of proteostasis and mitochondrial function also amplifies inflammatory signaling pathways in the brain.

Aging impairs microglial and macrophage phagocytic capacity through reduced expression of phagocytic receptors, altered mitochondrial function, and increased cellular senescence. This age-related decline in phagocytic function compromises clearance of amyloid-beta and tau pathology, contributing to Alzheimer's pathogenesis.

Aging is associated with increased basal Type I interferon (IFN) signaling and a pro-inflammatory interferon response, partly through accumulation of cytosolic nucleic acids and activation of pattern recognition receptors. This age-related increase in Type I IFN production contributes to chronic neuroinflammation and has been implicated in age-related cognitive decline and neurodegeneration.

Aging leads to progressive dysregulation of calcineurin signaling through accumulation of oxidative stress, mitochondrial dysfunction, and altered calcium homeostasis. Increased calcineurin activity in aged brains contributes to tau hyperphosphorylation and synaptic dysfunction, hallmarks of neurodegeneration.

Aging leads to progressive decline in CaMKII expression and activity through accumulated oxidative stress, protein misfolding, and mitochondrial dysfunction. Age-related changes in calcium homeostasis and reduced synaptic plasticity capacity impair CaMKII-dependent signaling cascades.

Aging impairs SERCA2a (sarco/endoplasmic reticulum calcium ATPase) function through oxidative stress, post-translational modifications, and reduced expression, leading to compromised calcium homeostasis in neurons. This age-related SERCA dysfunction contributes to intracellular calcium dysregulation, a hallmark pathology in Alzheimer's disease neurodegeneration.

Aging is associated with progressive dysfunction of iron homeostasis mechanisms, including reduced expression of iron export proteins (ferroportin) and increased iron uptake, leading to iron accumulation in the brain. Age-related decline in hepcidin regulation and transferrin receptor dysfunction further contributes to iron dysregulation in aging brains.

Aging is directly associated with declining estrogen levels, particularly in females through ovarian senescence and reduced estrogen production during and after menopause. This age-related decline in estrogen represents a physiological consequence of aging processes affecting reproductive endocrine function.

Aging is associated with declining ovarian function and reduced production of progesterone, particularly after menopause when ovarian steroid synthesis diminishes. This represents a direct hormonal consequence of age-related reproductive senescence.

Aging is associated with a decline in testosterone production due to reduced testicular function and decreased hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal (HPG) axis activity. This is a well-characterized endocrine consequence of advancing age in males.

Aging is associated with increased visceral adipose tissue accumulation through altered metabolic regulation, mitochondrial dysfunction, and changes in hormonal signaling (growth hormone, sex hormones). Age-related decline in energy expenditure and physical activity combined with dysregulation of adipogenesis pathways lead to preferential visceral fat deposition.

Aging alters the composition and function of the gut microbiota through multiple mechanisms including reduced microbial diversity, changes in intestinal barrier function, altered bile acid metabolism, and age-related immune dysregulation. These age-dependent changes directly promote a dysbiotic state characterized by increased pathogenic bacteria and reduced beneficial commensals.

Lipidated APOE, particularly APOE-containing lipoproteins, directly binds to LRP1 (lipoprotein receptor-related protein 1) on the neuronal cell surface, facilitating endocytic uptake of APOE and associated lipids. This interaction is a well-characterized molecular event fundamental to APOE-mediated lipid transport and clearance in the brain.

Lipidated APOE directly binds to LRP1 (lipoprotein receptor-related protein 1) on endothelial cells, facilitating the internalization and clearance of APOE-lipid complexes across the blood-brain barrier. This interaction is a well-characterized molecular mechanism of APOE-mediated lipoprotein uptake.

APOE is required for brain iron homeostasis, and transferrin receptor (TfR) is the primary cellular iron uptake mechanism. Lipidated APOE regulates iron trafficking and homeostasis, which directly influences transferrin receptor expression and function as a compensatory response to altered iron availability.

Lipidated APOE, particularly the APOE4 isoform, impairs the clearance and promotes the aggregation of amyloid-beta, leading to increased formation and accumulation of Abeta oligomers. APOE lipidation state affects its ability to bind and solubilize Abeta, influencing oligomerization pathways.

APOE, particularly when lipidated, interacts with TREM2 on microglia to regulate their transcriptional phenotype and functional capacity, including phagocytic activity. This TREM2-APOE pathway directly influences microglial phagocytic capability through modulation of cholesterol metabolism and lipid handling necessary for phagocytic function.

ABCA1 is an ATP-binding cassette transporter that mediates the efflux of cholesterol and phospholipids to apolipoprotein E, which is a direct substrate for lipidation by ABCA1. This lipidation of APOE is essential for the formation of discoidal HDL-like particles and mature lipoprotein complexes.

ABCA1 mediates cholesterol and lipid efflux from cells; increased ABCA1 activity reduces intracellular lipid accumulation by promoting lipid export, thereby decreasing astrocyte lipid droplet formation and content.

ABCA1 is an ATP-binding cassette transporter that mediates cholesterol efflux from cells; increased ABCA1 activity promotes cellular cholesterol export, which can increase lysosomal cholesterol content as lysosomes are major sites of cholesterol storage and metabolism in cells with high cholesterol flux.

ABCA1 mediates cholesterol and lipid efflux from cells, including microglia and astrocytes. Enhanced ABCA1 activity promotes lipidation of apolipoprotein E (apoE), which increases apoE-mediated Aβ clearance and reduces interstitial fluid (ISF) Aβ levels through enhanced brain clearance mechanisms.

ABCA1 activity regulates cholesterol efflux and cellular lipid homeostasis, which influences APOE expression in brain cells (microglia and astrocytes). ABCA1-mediated cholesterol excretion affects cellular stress responses and gene regulatory networks that control APOE transcription.

ABCA1 is an ATP-binding cassette transporter that mediates cholesterol efflux from cells, including brain cells. By transporting cholesterol out of cells, ABCA1 activity directly reduces intracellular and brain cholesterol levels.

APOE4 genotype influences APOE expression levels in the brain through genetic and post-transcriptional mechanisms. APOE4 carriers show altered APOE protein metabolism and expression patterns compared to APOE3 carriers, with functional consequences for lipid homeostasis and glial cell function.

APOE4 genotype impairs iron homeostasis and promotes iron accumulation in the brain through dysregulation of iron-regulatory proteins and increased oxidative stress, which is particularly evident in hippocampal subfields. This occurs via APOE4's reduced ability to manage lipid and metal homeostasis compared to other APOE isoforms.

APOE4 genotype synergizes with sleep disruption to accelerate amyloid-beta pathology, suggesting that APOE4 carriers may be more susceptible to sleep disruption effects or that APOE4 itself predisposes to impaired sleep homeostasis through altered lipid metabolism and neuroinflammatory pathways affecting sleep-wake regulation.

APOE4 protein has reduced ability to protect against lipid peroxidation and free radical damage compared to APOE2/E3 isoforms, leading to accumulation of oxidative stress in neurons and glial cells. APOE4 also impairs mitochondrial function and antioxidant defense mechanisms, directly increasing reactive oxygen species (ROS) production.

APOE4 genotype increases neuroinflammatory responses through enhanced microglial activation and cytokine production, while APOE3 provides neuroprotective anti-inflammatory effects. This occurs via differential APOE4-mediated signaling through lipoprotein receptors and TLR4, leading to sustained pro-inflammatory states.

APOE4 genotype enhances neuroinflammatory responses including complement activation and microglia-mediated synaptic pruning through altered lipid metabolism and ApoE protein structure, leading to increased C1q deposition and complement-dependent removal of synapses. APOE4's impaired ability to suppress neuroinflammation compared to APOE3 creates a permissive environment for complement cascade activation.

APOE4 genotype promotes neurodegeneration through multiple pathways including impaired amyloid-beta clearance, tau pathology exacerbation, neuroinflammation, and mitochondrial dysfunction, leading to reduced neuronal survival and decreased neuronal count in vulnerable brain regions.

APOE4 genotype influences amyloid-beta and tau pathology accumulation in the brain, which is reflected in altered cerebrospinal fluid biomarker levels (decreased Aβ42, increased phosphorylated tau and total tau). This represents a direct biological effect of APOE4's role in lipid metabolism and amyloid clearance on the biochemical composition of CSF.

APOE4 genotype impairs myelination and oligodendrocyte function through cholesterol dysregulation, exacerbates synapse loss and neurodegeneration, leading to cognitive decline. Multiple mechanistic pathways converge on neuronal dysfunction and loss of synaptic connectivity.

APOE4 genotype increases Alzheimer's disease risk and severity through impaired amyloid clearance and tau pathology, which accelerates neurodegeneration and mortality. The increased AD pathology burden associated with APOE4 directly contributes to earlier death through progressive neuronal loss and cognitive decline.

Neuronal cell death directly reduces brain volume through loss of viable neurons. Conversely, reduced brain volume reflects accumulated neuronal death, establishing a mechanistic link where pathological processes (tau aggregation) drive cell death that manifests as brain atrophy.

Neuronal cell death directly reduces brain volume through loss of viable neurons. Conversely, reduced brain volume reflects accumulated neuronal death, establishing a mechanistic link where pathological processes (tau aggregation) drive cell death that manifests as brain atrophy.

Brain atrophy (reduced brain volume) compromises white matter integrity through loss of structural support and disruption of axonal organization, as evidenced by spatial correlations between gray matter loss and white matter compromise in neurological conditions. Reduced brain volume in Alzheimer's disease directly impacts white matter tract organization and myelination patterns, leading to compromised white matter function.

Biological sex (female) directly determines reproductive biology and ovarian function, which inevitably leads to menopause as a natural physiological transition when ovarian hormone production ceases.

Biological sex (particularly female sex) directly determines reproductive physiology and hormonal status, which causally leads to menopause as a biological developmental stage characterized by cessation of ovarian function and menstrual cycles.

Biological sex (female) directly causes menstrual iron loss through the physiological shedding of the endometrial lining during menstruation, which contains blood and hemoglobin. This is a fundamental biological consequence of the menstrual cycle in menstruating individuals.

Biological sex influences systemic iron homeostasis through sex hormone-dependent regulation of hepcidin, the master iron regulatory hormone. Estrogen suppresses hepcidin expression while androgens can modulate iron metabolism, thereby directly affecting endothelial hepcidin response and iron availability in the cerebral vasculature.

Biological sex (particularly female sex) influences hepcidin expression through estrogen-mediated signaling pathways. Estrogen suppresses hepcidin transcription via alterations in STAT3 signaling and IL-6 pathway modulation, resulting in sex-based differences in hepcidin levels.

Biological sex influences systemic hepcidin levels through sex hormone-dependent regulation of hepcidin expression in hepatocytes. Estrogen suppresses hepcidin transcription while androgens have less inhibitory effects, resulting in higher hepcidin levels in females compared to males.

Biological sex influences metabolic syndrome risk through sex hormone-dependent regulation of metabolic processes and gut microbiota composition, with androgens and estrogens having distinct effects on glucose metabolism, lipid metabolism, and insulin sensitivity.

Biological sex influences circadian rhythm regulation and sleep architecture through sex hormone signaling (estrogen, progesterone, testosterone) affecting sleep-wake cycle generators in the hypothalamus and brainstem. Sex differences in sleep physiology are well-documented, with women showing greater vulnerability to sleep disruption, particularly during hormonal transitions (menopause, menstrual cycle).

Biological sex influences oxidative stress levels through sex hormone-dependent regulation of antioxidant defenses and mitochondrial function. Estrogen provides neuroprotective effects through antioxidant enzyme upregulation and mitochondrial preservation, while testosterone has variable effects; post-menopausal females show reduced estrogen-mediated protection leading to increased oxidative stress vulnerability.

Biological sex influences mitochondrial ROS production through sex hormone regulation of mitochondrial function and antioxidant defenses. Estrogen provides neuroprotective effects by enhancing mitochondrial efficiency and antioxidant capacity, while androgens have differential effects on mitochondrial ROS generation, leading to sex-dependent variations in mitochondrial oxidative stress.

Biological sex influences iron homeostasis through sex hormone-dependent regulation of hepcidin, the master iron regulator, resulting in sex-based differences in brain iron accumulation. Women typically have lower total body and brain iron due to menstrual iron loss and estrogen-mediated hepcidin suppression, while men accumulate higher iron levels over the lifespan.

Biological sex influences brain iron accumulation through sex hormone-dependent regulation of iron metabolism genes (particularly transferrin and hepcidin) and differential expression of iron transporters in the brain. Estrogen modulates ferroportin expression and iron efflux, while testosterone affects iron uptake mechanisms, leading to sex-dependent differences in brain iron levels.

Lipid droplets serve as lipid storage organelles that release free fatty acids and cholesterol, which are substrates for SREBP1 activation. Increased lipid availability from astrocyte lipid droplets directly activates SREBP1 signaling through mTORC1/PTEN-dependent pathways and LKB1 inhibition, promoting lipid synthesis.

Astrocyte lipid droplet accumulation impairs mitochondrial function and increases oxidative stress, which depletes glutathione (GSH) as the primary antioxidant defense system is overwhelmed. Lipid droplet-associated lipotoxicity in astrocytes reduces their capacity to synthesize and maintain glutathione levels.

Astrocyte lipid droplets undergo lipid oxidation through mitochondrial dysfunction and reduced antioxidant capacity, generating lipid peroxides as byproducts of peroxidative metabolism. Lipid peroxides accumulate as lipid droplets are mobilized for energy or membrane synthesis under neuroinflammatory conditions characteristic of Alzheimer's disease.

Glutathione (GSH) is a critical antioxidant that protects astrocytes from oxidative stress. Reduced GSH levels or impaired GSH metabolism leads to increased oxidative stress in astrocytes, which triggers lipid peroxidation and accumulation of lipids in lipid droplets as a compensatory response to metabolic dysfunction and lipotoxicity.

Lipid droplet accumulation in astrocytes promotes a pro-inflammatory A1 phenotype through metabolic stress and activation of inflammatory pathways including NF-κB signaling. Lipid droplets serve as substrates for lipid mediator synthesis that drive astrocyte neuroinflammatory activation.

Astrocyte lipid droplets serve as energy reserves that can be mobilized to support aerobic glycolysis through β-oxidation-derived acetyl-CoA and ATP production, thereby regulating the metabolic switching capacity of astrocytes. In AD pathology, impaired lipid droplet metabolism in astrocytes compromises their ability to sustain glycolytic flux and lactate shuttle support to neurons, contributing to neuroenergetic dysfunction.

Astrocyte lipid droplets serve as energy substrates that can be mobilized during metabolic stress; their breakdown generates acetyl-CoA and reduces oxygen availability, which stabilizes HIF-1alpha through decreased prolyl hydroxylase activity and increased PHD inhibition under hypoxic conditions.

Lipid droplets in astrocytes accumulate lipids including cholesterol and oxidized lipids that can activate pattern recognition receptors and trigger inflammasome assembly. The lipid-laden microenvironment promotes NLRP3 inflammasome activation through DAMPs and lipid-derived danger signals.

Astrocyte lipid droplets accumulate during neuroinflammation and their mobilization/metabolism can drive sustained production of pro-inflammatory cytokines through altered energy metabolism and activation of inflammatory pathways. Lipid droplet-derived fatty acids and their metabolites (including oxidized lipids) activate pattern recognition receptors and inflammasomes in astrocytes, perpetuating cytokine secretion.

Astrocyte lipid droplets serve as intracellular lipid storage organelles that can mobilize and release lipids including cholesterol and cholesterol esters to support neuronal function and maintain brain lipid homeostasis. Conversely, brain cholesterol levels and lipoprotein-mediated cholesterol delivery influence astrocyte lipid droplet formation and composition as part of whole-brain lipid metabolism regulation.

Astrocyte lipid droplets serve as intracellular lipid storage organelles that can mobilize and release lipids including cholesterol and cholesterol esters to support neuronal function and maintain brain lipid homeostasis. Conversely, brain cholesterol levels and lipoprotein-mediated cholesterol delivery influence astrocyte lipid droplet formation and composition as part of whole-brain lipid metabolism regulation.

CSF biomarkers (phosphorylated tau, total tau, amyloid-beta 42) reflect pathological processes in the brain that directly damage neuronal structure and function, leading to reduced cognitive performance. The biomarkers serve as objective measures of neuropathology that correlates with and predicts cognitive decline.

Neuronal loss/dysfunction alters the composition and levels of CSF biomarkers (tau, phospho-tau, amyloid-beta) that reflect ongoing neurodegeneration. Changes in neuronal count directly influence the abundance of these molecular markers in cerebrospinal fluid through reduced production and altered clearance of pathological proteins.

Cell death in Alzheimer's disease, particularly neuronal apoptosis and neurodegeneration, releases intracellular contents and biomarkers (phosphorylated tau, amyloid-beta, neurofilament light chain, phosphorylated tau181) into the cerebrospinal fluid. These biomarkers are direct products and indicators of the underlying neuronal death processes.

CSF biomarkers (phosphorylated tau, amyloid-beta, and other neurodegeneration markers) reflect neuroinflammation and neuronal damage that can compromise BBB integrity through activation of inflammatory pathways and loss of tight junction proteins. Conversely, BBB compromise allows peripheral immune infiltration and altered CSF composition, increasing pathological protein concentrations detected as biomarkers.

CSF biomarkers (phosphorylated tau, amyloid-beta, and other neurodegeneration markers) reflect neuroinflammation and neuronal damage that can compromise BBB integrity through activation of inflammatory pathways and loss of tight junction proteins. Conversely, BBB compromise allows peripheral immune infiltration and altered CSF composition, increasing pathological protein concentrations detected as biomarkers.

CSF biomarkers (p-tau, p-tau181, phosphorylated forms) reflect pathological tau and amyloid burden that directly impair lysosomal proteolytic capacity and autophagy-lysosomal pathway function in neurons. Conversely, lysosomal dysfunction impairs clearance of pathological proteins, leading to their accumulation and increased CSF biomarker levels.

CSF biomarkers (p-tau, p-tau181, phosphorylated forms) reflect pathological tau and amyloid burden that directly impair lysosomal proteolytic capacity and autophagy-lysosomal pathway function in neurons. Conversely, lysosomal dysfunction impairs clearance of pathological proteins, leading to their accumulation and increased CSF biomarker levels.

Familial AD mutations (particularly in PSEN1/PSEN2 and APP) alter cellular processing pathways and increase amyloidogenic processing through BACE1, leading to elevated BACE1 activity and expression. This occurs through both transcriptional upregulation and post-translational stabilization of BACE1 protein in response to mutant-induced proteostatic stress.

Familial AD mutations in APP (such as Swedish, London, and other variants) directly alter APP processing by enhancing β-secretase and γ-secretase cleavage, thereby increasing production of amyloidogenic Aβ peptides. These mutations either increase APP expression levels or change the protein structure to favor pathogenic proteolytic processing pathways.

Familial AD mutations (APP, PSEN1, PSEN2) increase amyloid-beta production and GSK3β activation, which directly phosphorylates tau at multiple sites, leading to tau hyperphosphorylation. This is a well-established mechanistic pathway demonstrated in transgenic animal models carrying familial AD mutations.

Familial AD mutations in presenilin-1, presenilin-2, and APP alter calcium signaling through disrupted IP3 receptor and ryanodine receptor function, leading to excessive Ca2+ release from the endoplasmic reticulum into mitochondria and causing mitochondrial calcium overload.

Familial AD mutations (particularly in PSEN1/2 and APP) directly alter presenilin function and γ-secretase activity, leading to dysregulation of calcium homeostasis and increased mitochondrial Ca²⁺ uptake through enhanced IP3R and RYANR signaling.

CypA (Cyclophilin A) promotes mitochondrial dysfunction and oxidative stress through its interaction with the adenine nucleotide translocator (ANT), which can indirectly compromise lysosomal function through impaired autophagy-lysosomal clearance and calcium dysregulation. CypA's role in promoting the mitochondrial permeability transition pore (mPTP) opening leads to cellular stress that overwhelms lysosomal capacity and promotes lysosomal dysfunction.

Cyclophilin A (CypA) is a peptidyl-prolyl isomerase that interacts with mitochondrial proteins and can facilitate opening of the mitochondrial permeability transition pore (mPTP), leading to increased mitochondrial ROS production. CypA-mediated dysfunction of mitochondrial oxidative phosphorylation complexes results in enhanced superoxide generation.

CypA (Cyclophilin A) is released from neurons and glial cells during neuroinflammation and neurodegeneration, where it acts on CD147 receptors on endothelial cells and pericytes, triggering matrix metalloproteinase (MMP) activation and tight junction protein degradation. This leads to disruption of blood-brain barrier (BBB) integrity and increased BBB permeability.

CypA (Cyclophilin A) can disrupt tight junction protein integrity through its interaction with CD147 and subsequent inflammatory pathway activation, compromising the blood-brain barrier (BBB) function critical in AD pathology. CypA-mediated MMP upregulation and ROS generation can lead to degradation and dysfunction of tight junction proteins like ZO-1, occludin, and claudins.

CypA (Cyclophilin A) promotes mitochondrial dysfunction and oxidative stress through its interaction with the TLR4/NF-κB pathway and mitochondrial permeability transition pore, leading to increased generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) that directly cause lipid peroxidation. Additionally, CypA-mediated neuroinflammation in AD exacerbates oxidative damage to lipids in neuronal membranes and organelles.

CypA (Cyclophilin A) acts as a protein chaperone that can become oxidized under stress conditions and also facilitates the translocation of proteins across membranes; dysregulation of CypA has been shown to impair mitochondrial function and enhance ROS production, thereby increasing oxidative stress in neuronal cells.

CypA (Cyclophilin A) is released from neurons and binds to CD147 on astrocytes, triggering inflammatory signaling cascades that promote astrocyte activation and polarization toward the pro-inflammatory A1 phenotype. This process involves NF-κB and MAPK pathway activation, which drives A1 astrocyte differentiation.

CypA (Cyclophilin A) is released from cells during stress/damage and acts as a danger-associated molecular pattern (DAMP) that binds to CD147 receptors on immune cells and microglia, directly triggering IL-1β production and secretion through NF-κB and MAPK pathway activation.

Cyclophilin A (CypA) is released from damaged neurons and acts as a damage-associated molecular pattern (DAMP) that binds to CD147 and toll-like receptors on microglia and astrocytes, triggering pro-inflammatory cytokine production and neuroinflammatory cascades. CypA also promotes mitochondrial dysfunction and oxidative stress, which further amplify neuroinflammatory responses through microglial activation.

CypA (Cyclophilin A) impairs microglial and macrophage phagocytic function by promoting inflammatory signaling and oxidative stress, which dysregulates the phagocytic machinery and reduces the ability of immune cells to clear amyloid-beta and cellular debris. Elevated extracellular CypA acts as a danger-associated molecular pattern (DAMP) that activates CD55 and promotes pro-inflammatory responses that compromise phagocytic capacity.

LRP1 (lipoprotein receptor-related protein 1) on the cell surface functions as a major clearance receptor for amyloid-beta (Aβ) from the interstitial fluid (ISF), mediating Aβ uptake into cells and subsequent degradation or transcytosis across the blood-brain barrier. Loss or dysfunction of LRP1 surface expression directly reduces Aβ clearance capacity, leading to increased ISF Aβ accumulation.

LRP1 (low-density lipoprotein receptor-related protein 1) functions as a major endocytic receptor on the cell surface that directly binds and internalizes amyloid-beta (Aβ) peptides, thereby facilitating their clearance from the extracellular space. Increased surface expression of LRP1 enhances the rate of Aβ uptake and clearance through receptor-mediated endocytosis.

GBA (glucocerebrosidase) catalyzes the hydrolysis of glucosylceramide to glucose and ceramide in lysosomes. Impaired GBA activity leads to accumulation of glucosylceramide and secondary lysosomal cholesterol accumulation, as cholesterol metabolism is dysregulated in lysosomes with lipid storage defects. GBA dysfunction indirectly affects lysosomal cholesterol homeostasis through disrupted lipid trafficking and metabolism.

Lysosomal cholesterol accumulation can impair TFEB (transcription factor EB) translocation and function, reducing the expression of lysosomal biogenesis genes. Conversely, reduced lysosomal biogenesis decreases lysosomal degradative capacity, leading to cholesterol accumulation within lysosomes.

Lysosomal cholesterol accumulation can impair TFEB (transcription factor EB) translocation and function, reducing the expression of lysosomal biogenesis genes. Conversely, reduced lysosomal biogenesis decreases lysosomal degradative capacity, leading to cholesterol accumulation within lysosomes.

Lysosomal cholesterol accumulation alters membrane composition and fluidity, disrupting the proton pump function (V-ATPase) and impairing the maintenance of the acidic lysosomal environment, thereby decreasing lysosomal pH homeostasis.

v-ATPase activity is essential for lysosomal acidification and cholesterol efflux from lysosomes; reduced v-ATPase activity impairs lysosomal cholesterol clearance, leading to increased lysosomal cholesterol accumulation. Conversely, elevated lysosomal cholesterol can feedback inhibit v-ATPase function through effects on lysosomal membrane composition and proton gradient maintenance.

Lysosomal cholesterol accumulation impairs lysosomal function and autophagy, which are critical for microglial phagocytic capacity and clearance of amyloid-beta and protein aggregates. Cholesterol-enriched lysosomes reduce the efficiency of microglial phagocytosis by limiting lysosomal degradative capacity and impairing the autophagy-lysosomal pathway essential for microglial activation and phagocytic function.

Lysosomal cholesterol accumulation impairs lysosomal function and autophagy, leading to lipid accumulation and lipid droplet formation in microglia as these cells attempt to sequester excess lipids through alternative storage mechanisms. This represents a metabolic adaptation of microglia to dysfunctional lysosomal cholesterol processing.

Lysosomal cholesterol accumulation and brain cholesterol homeostasis are interconnected through lysosomal lipid trafficking and cellular cholesterol sensing mechanisms. Lysosomes serve as cholesterol storage and distribution centers; impaired lysosomal cholesterol efflux directly affects bioavailable brain cholesterol pools, while altered brain cholesterol demands feedback-regulate lysosomal cholesterol processing.

Lysosomal cholesterol accumulation and brain cholesterol homeostasis are interconnected through lysosomal lipid trafficking and cellular cholesterol sensing mechanisms. Lysosomes serve as cholesterol storage and distribution centers; impaired lysosomal cholesterol efflux directly affects bioavailable brain cholesterol pools, while altered brain cholesterol demands feedback-regulate lysosomal cholesterol processing.

Metabolic syndrome components (obesity, insulin resistance, dyslipidemia, hyperglycemia) directly impair mitochondrial function through increased lipid accumulation, oxidative stress, and glucose dysmetabolism, leading to enhanced mitochondrial ROS production. Insulin resistance and hyperglycemia promote mitochondrial electron transport chain dysfunction and uncoupling, amplifying ROS generation.

Metabolic syndrome components (obesity, insulin resistance, hyperglycemia) directly activate mTORC1 signaling through enhanced amino acid availability, growth factor signaling (insulin/IGF1), and reduced AMPK-mediated inhibition. This hyperactivation of mTORC1 contributes to neurodegeneration by promoting excessive protein synthesis, autophagy impairment, and mitochondrial dysfunction.

Metabolic syndrome induces insulin resistance and hyperglycemia, which activate mTORC1 signaling and promote TFEB phosphorylation, thereby inhibiting TFEB nuclear translocation and impairing autophagy-lysosomal clearance of pathogenic proteins.

Metabolic syndrome components (hyperglycemia, dyslipidemia, insulin resistance) impair iron homeostasis through oxidative stress and mitochondrial dysfunction, leading to accumulation of labile iron in vulnerable brain regions. Dysregulated iron metabolism and increased reactive oxygen species generation promote the formation of bioavailable iron pools that drive neurodegeneration.

Metabolic syndrome components (hyperglycemia, dyslipidemia, insulin resistance) directly impair mitochondrial function and reduce antioxidant capacity, leading to increased reactive oxygen species (ROS) production and oxidative stress. Chronic hyperglycemia and lipid accumulation in metabolic syndrome also promote NADPH oxidase activation and suppress expression of antioxidant enzymes (SOD, catalase).

Metabolic syndrome components (insulin resistance, dyslipidemia, hyperglycemia) impair neuronal insulin signaling and increase neuroinflammation, leading to increased BACE1 transcription and proteolytic processing of amyloid precursor protein. High-fat diet and hyperglycemia models show BACE1 upregulation through activation of NF-κB and stress response pathways.

Metabolic syndrome, characterized by high-fat diet and obesity, activates p38 MAPK signaling in hypothalamic and other tissues through inflammatory pathways. High-fat diet-induced metabolic dysfunction directly triggers p38 and JNK MAPK activation as part of the inflammatory response, as demonstrated by NFATc3 deficiency protecting against HFD-induced p38 activation.

Metabolic syndrome components (insulin resistance, hyperglycemia, dyslipidemia) trigger neuroinflammation and metabolic stress that promote microglial activation and subsequent A1 astrocyte polarization through cytokine signaling (IL-1β, TNF-α, IL-6). A1 astrocytes are neurotoxic reactive astrocytes induced by pro-inflammatory signals that exacerbate neurodegeneration in the AD context.

Metabolic syndrome, characterized by insulin resistance, obesity, and dyslipidemia, impairs mitochondrial function and glucose metabolism in brain cells, forcing neurons to shift from oxidative phosphorylation to increased reliance on glycolytic ATP production (the glycolytic switch). This metabolic reprogramming occurs as neurons attempt to maintain energy homeostasis under conditions of impaired mitochondrial efficiency and oxidative stress.

Metabolic syndrome components (obesity, insulin resistance, dyslipidemia, hyperglycemia) directly activate innate immune pathways and metabolic endotoxemia, leading to increased IL1B production by macrophages and microglia through TLR4/NF-κB signaling.

Metabolic syndrome components (obesity, insulin resistance, dyslipidemia, hypertension) activate innate immune pathways through multiple routes: chronic hyperglycemia and lipotoxicity trigger microglial activation via TLR4 signaling; circulating metabolic endotoxins (LPS from dysbiotic microbiota) and advanced glycation end products (AGEs) directly stimulate neuroinflammatory responses; adipose tissue-derived pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-6) cross the compromised blood-brain barrier.

Loss of nuclear lamina integrity directly causes disruption of the nuclear lamina structure through mechanical failure or proteolytic degradation of lamin proteins (Lamin A/C). Nuclear lamina disruption is a direct structural consequence of compromised lamina integrity.

Nuclear lamina integrity is required for proper nuclear import and export of proteins through nuclear pore complexes; REST (Repressor Element 1 Silencing Transcription factor) requires intact nuclear envelope function for appropriate nucleocytoplasmic transport, and lamina disruption can lead to REST accumulation in the cytoplasm as a consequence of impaired nuclear import machinery function.

An intact nuclear lamina maintains nuclear envelope integrity and prevents aberrant nuclear translocation of proteins like REST. Nuclear lamina disruption, common in neurodegeneration, allows dysregulated REST nuclear accumulation and altered transcriptional activity.

Nuclear lamina integrity maintains proper nuclear envelope barrier function, which prevents aberrant mtDNA release into the cytoplasm. Disruption of nuclear lamina (e.g., via lamin A/C mutations or degradation) can compromise nuclear envelope integrity and indirectly affect cellular stress responses that promote mitochondrial dysfunction and mtDNA escape.

An intact nuclear lamina maintains nuclear envelope integrity and proper nucleocytoplasmic transport regulation, preventing aberrant release of mitochondrial DNA into the cytosol. Nuclear lamina disruption, observed in neurodegeneration and aging, compromises the barrier function that normally compartmentalizes mitochondrial DNA, allowing mtDNA to accumulate in the cytosol where it triggers inflammatory pathways via cGAS-STING signaling.

Nuclear lamina integrity maintains nuclear envelope barrier function, preventing cytoplasmic DNA leakage that would otherwise activate the cytosolic DNA sensor cGAS. Loss of nuclear lamina integrity (via lamin mutations or proteolysis) allows accumulation of cytoplasmic DNA, which directly activates cGAS through pattern recognition.

Nuclear lamina integrity is essential for proper nuclear import and export of transcription factors and signaling molecules that regulate interferon-stimulated gene (ISG) expression. Disruption of the nuclear lamina impairs nucleocytoplasmic transport, preventing normal ISG response activation during innate immune challenges.

Nuclear lamina integrity maintains proper nuclear envelope barrier function and prevents cytoplasmic DNA/RNA from activating cytosolic DNA sensors that trigger STING pathway activation. Lamina disruption allows aberrant cytoplasmic nucleic acids to accumulate, which directly activate STING signaling.

Nuclear lamina disruption activates the cGAS-STING pathway through cytoplasmic DNA leakage, which triggers Type I interferon (IFN-β and IFN-α) production as part of the innate immune response. Loss of nuclear lamina integrity is a hallmark of neurodegeneration in Alzheimer's disease and compromises the nuclear barrier, allowing accumulation of pathogenic proteins and aberrant immune activation.

Nuclear lamina disruption compromises the nuclear envelope barrier, allowing increased translocation of mitochondrial DNA from the mitochondrial matrix into the cytosol through disrupted nuclear-mitochondrial compartmentalization and impaired quality control mechanisms.

Nuclear lamina disruption compromises the nuclear envelope barrier, allowing accumulation of cytoplasmic DNA in the nucleus or allowing nuclear DNA fragments to escape to the cytoplasm, where they activate the cytosolic DNA sensor cGAS (cyclic GMP-AMP synthase). cGAS recognizes aberrant nuclear/cytoplasmic DNA as a danger-associated molecular pattern (DAMP) and becomes catalytically active to produce cGAMP.

Nuclear lamina disruption activates the cGAS-STING pathway and innate immune signaling, leading to increased interferon-stimulated gene (ISG) expression as part of the interferon response. This occurs through loss of nuclear envelope integrity, which allows cytoplasmic access to dsDNA and activation of pattern recognition receptors.

Nuclear lamina disruption compromises the nuclear envelope barrier function, allowing cytoplasmic DNA and RNA to enter the nucleus or nuclear contents to leak into the cytoplasm, triggering cGAS-STING pathway activation and downstream Type I interferon production.

Nuclear lamina disruption compromises the structural integrity of the nuclear envelope, leading to increased passive leak of Ca²⁺ from the ER/nuclear envelope into the cytosol and disrupted nuclear-cytoplasmic Ca²⁺ homeostasis. Additionally, lamina breakdown impairs nuclear import of Ca²⁺ regulatory proteins, reducing cytosolic Ca²⁺ buffering capacity.

Nrf2 activation directly increases expression of antioxidant response elements (ARE) that regulate glutathione synthesis genes, particularly through upregulation of glutathione S-transferases and gamma-glutamylcysteine synthetase. This is a canonical Nrf2-ARE pathway function where Nrf2 binds to ARE sequences in the promoter regions of genes encoding glutathione biosynthetic and metabolic enzymes.

Nrf2 is a master transcription factor that activates antioxidant response elements (AREs) to induce expression of cytoprotective genes including antioxidant enzymes (SOD, catalase, glutathione peroxidase) and phase II detoxifying enzymes that directly reduce lipid peroxides by catalyzing their reduction to lipid alcohols.

Nrf2 activation induces the transcription of antioxidant response elements (AREs) and upregulates cytoprotective genes including HO-1, NQO1, and SOD, which directly neutralize reactive oxygen species (ROS) and reduce oxidative stress levels.

Nrf2 is a master transcription factor that binds to antioxidant response elements (ARE) in the promoter regions of genes encoding glutathione synthesis enzymes, including GCLC and GCLM subunits of γ-glutamylcysteine synthetase, directly increasing GSH biosynthesis.

GSK3β inhibition leads to increased Nrf2 activity by preventing Nrf2 phosphorylation and subsequent degradation, allowing Nrf2 to accumulate and translocate to the nucleus. This is part of a coordinated pathway where GSK3β acts as a negative regulator of Nrf2 stability.

Nrf2 activation upregulates cystathionine γ-lyase (CSE) and cystathionine β-synthase (CBS), the key enzymes responsible for H2S production from cysteine and homocysteine metabolism. This occurs through Nrf2 binding to antioxidant response elements (AREs) in the promoter regions of these genes.

Nrf2 activation increases expression of antioxidant response elements (AREs) that regulate cysteine metabolism and biosynthesis enzymes, which provide substrate (free cysteines) for protein sulfhydration reactions. Sulfhydration of proteins like KEAP1 and other regulatory proteins can further modulate Nrf2 signaling through positive feedback mechanisms.

Neuronal loss reduces the number of synaptic connections and functional neural circuits, which directly decreases the capacity for long-term potentiation as LTP requires intact pre- and post-synaptic neurons to generate sustained synaptic strengthening. Fewer neurons means fewer synapses available to undergo potentiation.

A1 astrocytes, which are activated in response to neurodegeneration and neuroinflammation in Alzheimer's disease, can contribute to neuronal loss through production of pro-inflammatory cytokines, complement factors, and other neurotoxic mediators. Conversely, neuronal loss/damage triggers reactive astrogliosis and A1 astrocyte differentiation.

IL1B (interleukin-1 beta) is a pro-inflammatory cytokine that promotes neuroinflammation and neurodegeneration in Alzheimer's disease, directly contributing to neuronal death and reduced neuronal count through activation of inflammatory pathways and promotion of apoptosis.

Reactive A1 astrocytes, activated by neuroinflammatory signals in AD, produce neurotoxic factors (complement proteins, cytokines) and withdraw supportive metabolites from neurons, directly contributing to neuronal death and reduced neuronal count. The loss of neurons is a downstream consequence of A1 astrocyte activation.

MMP9 (matrix metalloproteinase-9) directly cleaves and degrades tight junction proteins including claudins, occludin, and ZO-1 through its protease activity, disrupting the blood-brain barrier integrity.

MMP9 (matrix metalloproteinase-9) directly degrades tight junction proteins (claudins, occludin, ZO-1) and components of the extracellular matrix that maintain blood-brain barrier integrity, leading to BBB compromise and increased paracellular permeability.

MMP9 is a matrix metalloproteinase that can proteolytically cleave the extracellular domain of LRP1 (low-density lipoprotein receptor-related protein 1), reducing its surface localization and functional availability. This cleavage reduces LRP1's capacity to mediate amyloid-beta clearance and endocytosis.

MMP9 (matrix metalloproteinase-9) degrades tight junction proteins and the blood-brain barrier (BBB) extracellular matrix, increasing BBB permeability and facilitating infiltration of peripheral immune cells into the brain parenchyma.

MMP9 (matrix metalloproteinase-9) is a serine protease capable of degrading amyloid-beta peptides in the interstitial fluid (ISF). MMP9 cleaves Aβ monomers and oligomers, thereby reducing ISF Aβ levels through direct proteolytic degradation.

MMP9 (matrix metalloproteinase-9) can degrade the blood-brain barrier and increase ISF (interstitial fluid) tau levels by facilitating tau protein transport from the perivascular space into the interstitial fluid and promoting tau oligomerization through proteolytic processing.

MMP9 (matrix metalloproteinase-9) degrades extracellular matrix components and can cleave adhesion molecules and receptors on microglial surfaces, thereby modulating microglial activation state and phagocytic capacity. MMP9 activity influences the inflammatory microenvironment that regulates microglial phagocytic function toward amyloid-beta and neuronal debris.

Sleep disruption impairs the coordinated coupling between sleep-wake cycles and glymphatic clearance mechanisms, reducing the efficiency of interstitial fluid exchange and metabolic waste removal during sleep. This disruption diminishes the sleep-dependent enhancement of glymphatic function that is critical for maintaining brain homeostasis and memory consolidation.

Sleep disruption impairs the glymphatic system's clearance function and reduces the circadian-regulated autophagy-lysosomal pathway, thereby decreasing overall autophagy flux. During sleep, increased interstitial space and enhanced glymphatic clearance facilitate removal of protein aggregates and cellular debris through autophagy-dependent mechanisms.

Sleep deprivation directly increases mitochondrial ROS production through disruption of redox metabolism and impaired mitochondrial quality control mechanisms. Sleep loss compromises mitochondrial autophagy and electron transport chain function, leading to increased reactive oxygen species accumulation.

Sleep disruption reduces glymphatic clearance of interstitial fluid solutes, including amyloid-beta, leading to increased ISF Aβ accumulation. Sleep deprivation impairs aquaporin-4-dependent interstitial fluid-cerebrospinal fluid exchange, the primary mechanism for clearing metabolic waste including Aβ from the brain.

Sleep disruption impairs glymphatic system function, which is the primary mechanism for clearing interstitial solutes including amyloid-beta (Aβ) from the brain during sleep. Restoration of sleep through optogenetic targeting of GABAergic neurons in Alzheimer's disease models improves glymphatic clearance capacity and reduces Aβ pathology.

Sleep disruption activates microglia through circadian rhythm disruption and impaired glymphatic clearance, which directly triggers NLRP3 inflammasome assembly and activation, leading to IL-1β and IL-18 production.

Sleep loss directly triggers astrocytic phagocytosis and microglial activation in the cerebral cortex, which are key neuroinflammatory responses. Sleep disruption impairs glymphatic clearance, leading to accumulation of inflammatory mediators and sustained microglial/astrocytic activation.

Sleep deprivation directly triggers microglial activation and cytokine-storm-like responses through disruption of circadian-regulated immune homeostasis and NF-κB signaling pathways. Neopterin-mediated microglial activation during sleep loss results in sustained IL-6 and pro-inflammatory cytokine secretion via impaired glucocorticoid signaling.

Sleep disruption impairs the glymphatic system's clearance capacity and reduces ATP production in mitochondria, leading to diminished function of ATP-dependent Ca2+ pumps (SERCA and Na+/Ca2+ exchangers), which directly results in mitochondrial Ca2+ overload. Additionally, sleep loss increases neuronal activity and synaptic transmission, elevating intracellular Ca2+ that accumulates in mitochondria when buffering capacity is compromised.
