Pericytes are structural components of the blood-brain barrier that directly regulate the size, integrity, and fluid dynamics of the perivascular space through their contractile properties and tight junction interactions with the endothelium. Pericyte loss or dysfunction leads to enlargement of perivascular spaces and impaired clearance of interstitial solutes, a hallmark of cerebral amyloid angiopathy and neurodegeneration in Alzheimer's disease.

Pericytes are structural components of the blood-brain barrier that directly interact with and support endothelial cells through physical contact and paracrine signaling, which maintains tight junction protein expression and stability. Loss of pericyte coverage or dysfunction leads to disruption of tight junction protein organization and function.

Pericyte dysfunction and loss in Alzheimer's disease leads to blood-brain barrier breakdown and neuroinflammation, which stimulates microglial activation and astrocytic responses that produce and upregulate MMP9 expression. Pericytes normally suppress MMP9 through maintenance of BBB integrity and anti-inflammatory signaling.

Pericytes regulate blood-brain barrier (BBB) function and vascular stability through physical interactions with endothelial cells, thereby influencing the microenvironment that controls endothelial iron import machinery and expression. Pericyte dysfunction or loss impairs BBB integrity and vascular homeostasis, which can alter endothelial cell iron handling and transporter regulation.

LRP1 (lipoprotein receptor-related protein 1) functions as a clearance receptor for amyloid-beta, mediating its endocytosis and degradation. Reduced LRP1 expression or function leads to impaired Aβ clearance and accumulation of Aβ oligomers.

LRP1 (lipoprotein receptor-related protein 1) functions as an apolipoprotein E receptor that mediates the clearance and transport of amyloid-beta from the brain, primarily by facilitating Aβ efflux across the blood-brain barrier. Loss or reduced LRP1 function leads to impaired Aβ clearance, resulting in accumulation and increased amyloid plaque formation.

LRP1 (lipoprotein receptor-related protein 1) functions as a key transporter at the blood-brain barrier, mediating clearance of amyloid-beta and other AD-related proteins from brain parenchyma across the BBB. Loss or dysfunction of LRP1 compromises BBB integrity and impairs clearance, leading to amyloid accumulation and neurodegeneration.

LRP1 mediates clearance of amyloid-beta and other neurotoxic ligands across the blood-brain barrier, and also regulates tight junction protein expression and endothelial cell function. Loss of LRP1 function impairs BBB integrity by reducing transcytotic clearance of amyloid-beta and compromising endothelial barrier maintenance.

LRP1 mediates transcytosis and clearance of amyloid-beta across the blood-brain barrier, and its dysfunction impairs endothelial tight junction integrity through altered signaling pathways and reduced clearance of neurotoxic aggregates that compromise BBB function.

LRP1 functions as an endocytic receptor that mediates clearance of amyloid-beta and other ligands; its expression on microglia regulates their phagocytic capacity and activation state, thereby directly influencing microglial phagocytosis of pathological substrates including amyloid-beta and neuronal debris.

LRP1 regulates clearance of amyloid-beta and other toxic proteins from the brain parenchyma, and dysfunction of LRP1-mediated clearance impairs interstitial fluid dynamics and glymphatic system function. LRP1 is essential for maintaining proper perivascular space integrity and fluid flow dynamics that drive glymphatic clearance.

LRP1 is a key receptor involved in ApoE-mediated clearance of amyloid-beta and regulation of blood-brain barrier integrity. LRP1 dysfunction impairs glymphatic and meningeal lymphatic clearance pathways by disrupting proper interstitial fluid dynamics and perivascular transport mechanisms that are essential for meningeal lymphatic drainage function.

LRP1 mediates clearance of amyloid-beta and other proteins from the interstitial fluid (ISF) through receptor-mediated endocytosis and blood-brain barrier transport, thereby regulating ISF volume dynamics and clearance efficiency. Loss of LRP1 function impairs solute clearance and can affect ISF volume homeostasis.

Pericyte PDGFRβ signaling maintains blood-brain barrier integrity and pericyte-endothelial interactions; dysfunction of this pathway increases BBB permeability and promotes infiltration of peripheral immune cells into the brain parenchyma.

PDGFRbeta signaling in pericytes regulates vascular stability and blood-brain barrier integrity; loss of pericyte PDGFRbeta function compromises BBB function and increases neuroinflammation, leading to lysosomal dysfunction and damage in adjacent cells through increased neuroinflammatory mediators and impaired clearance mechanisms.

PDGFRβ signaling in pericytes regulates vascular stability and blood-brain barrier integrity; loss of pericyte PDGFRβ signaling leads to increased MMP9 expression and activation, contributing to BBB breakdown and neuroinflammation in Alzheimer's disease pathology.

Pericyte PDGFRβ signaling maintains blood-brain barrier (BBB) integrity by stabilizing pericyte-endothelial interactions and supporting tight junction protein expression and localization. Loss of PDGFRβ signaling in pericytes leads to pericyte dropout and compromised tight junction protein function.

Pericyte PDGFRβ signaling maintains blood-brain barrier (BBB) integrity through stabilization of endothelial tight junctions and pericyte-endothelial interactions. Loss of pericyte PDGFRβ function compromises BBB integrity, leading to increased CSF albumin ratio (albumin leakage from blood into CSF).

Pericyte PDGFRbeta signaling maintains blood-brain barrier integrity and pericyte-endothelial cell interactions, which regulate capillary diameter and interstitial space geometry. Dysfunction of PDGFRbeta signaling leads to pericyte loss, capillary collapse, and increased interstitial space fraction as a compensatory expansion.

Pericyte PDGFRβ signaling maintains blood-brain barrier integrity and cerebral blood flow through PDGF-BB/PDGFRβ coupling, which directly regulates pericyte contractility and vascular stability. Dysfunction of PDGFRβ in pericytes leads to impaired fluid dynamics and altered interstitial fluid (ISF) volume clearance in the brain parenchyma.

Pericyte PDGFRβ signaling maintains pericyte-endothelial cell interactions and blood-brain barrier (BBB) integrity, which directly affects the structural organization and patency of the perivascular space. Loss of PDGFRβ signaling in pericytes leads to pericyte detachment, BBB disruption, and expansion of perivascular spaces, a hallmark of neurodegeneration.

Sleep-wake cycles regulate glymphatic system function, which controls cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) circulation and clearance through the brain parenchyma. During sleep, particularly slow-wave sleep, increased aquaporin-4 water channel activity and interstitial space expansion enhance CSF-interstitial fluid exchange, thereby affecting CSF volume dynamics and clearance patterns.

During sleep, particularly slow-wave sleep, the glymphatic system becomes more active, increasing interstitial fluid (ISF) volume and flow to facilitate clearance of metabolic waste including amyloid-beta and tau. Conversely, wakefulness reduces glymphatic ISF volume as aquaporin-4 water channels show reduced activity.

Sleep state transitions, particularly the shift from wakefulness to sleep, directly modulate glymphatic system activation through changes in interstitial space volume and aquaporin-4 water channel function. The sleeping brain state enables enhanced cerebrospinal fluid-interstitial fluid exchange coupling that is suppressed during wakefulness.

Peripheral immune cells infiltrating the brain (including monocytes, macrophages, and T cells) directly activate and promote NLRP3 inflammasome assembly through pattern recognition receptor signaling and pro-inflammatory cytokine production in response to amyloid-beta and other damage-associated molecular patterns (DAMPs).

Peripheral immune cells that infiltrate the brain (such as monocytes and T cells) interact with microglia and can modulate their activation state and phagocytic capacity through cytokine signaling and direct cell-cell contact. These peripheral immune signals can enhance or suppress microglial phagocytosis of pathogenic substrates including amyloid-beta and tau.

Peripheral immune cells in the brain (including those recruited during neuroinflammation) activate microglial TAK1 signaling, which directly promotes the conversion of resting astrocytes to neurotoxic A1 astrocytes. This occurs through microglial production of pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1β, C1q) that drive A1 astrocyte polarization and associated cognitive impairment.

Peripheral immune cells that infiltrate the brain parenchyma can directly interact with and activate microglia through cytokine/chemokine signaling and antigen presentation, promoting their differentiation into disease-associated microglia (DAM) phenotype. This represents a key cross-talk mechanism between innate and adaptive immunity in neuroinflammatory responses.

Peripheral immune cells that infiltrate the brain (including monocytes, macrophages, and T cells) are a primary source of IL1B production in the neuroinflammatory microenvironment of Alzheimer's disease. These infiltrating cells produce IL1B in response to amyloid-beta and tau pathology, contributing to neuroinflammation.

Peripheral immune cells that infiltrate the brain parenchyma (including T cells and monocytes/macrophages) directly interact with and activate microglia through pattern recognition receptor signaling, cytokine production, and cell-cell contact mechanisms. This peripheral immune cell infiltration drives microglial activation state transitions and amplifies neuroinflammatory cascades characteristic of Alzheimer's disease.

Peripheral immune cells infiltrate the brain parenchyma through blood-brain barrier dysfunction and directly release pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6) and chemokines that activate resident microglia and astrocytes, amplifying neuroinflammatory cascades. This peripheral-to-central immune activation is a well-characterized mechanism in neurodegeneration including Alzheimer's disease.

Peripheral immune cells infiltrating the brain parenchyma directly interact with resident glial cells (microglia) and release danger-associated molecular patterns (DAMPs) and inflammatory cytokines that activate the NLRP3 inflammasome complex. Activated peripheral immune cells in the CNS serve as a primary source of pro-inflammatory signals that trigger NLRP3 assembly and activation, leading to caspase-1 dependent maturation of IL-1β and IL-18.

Peripheral immune cells infiltrating the brain (such as CAR T cells or circulating immune cells) directly secrete cytokines upon activation. Once present in the brain parenchyma, these cells are activated by local inflammatory signals and microglia activation, leading to sustained cytokine production including IL-8, TNF-α, and IL-6.

Peripheral immune cells infiltrating the brain parenchyma, particularly activated microglia and infiltrating macrophages/monocytes, express and activate the STING (Stimulator of Interferon Genes) pathway in response to cytosolic DNA sensing, leading to type I interferon production and neuroinflammation in Alzheimer's disease.

Peripheral immune cells that infiltrate the brain (including microglia-associated macrophages and T cells) produce and release C1q as part of neuroinflammatory responses. C1q is a component of the classical complement cascade and is produced by immune cells and glial cells during CNS inflammation.

Peripheral immune cells that infiltrate the brain (including monocytes, macrophages, and T cells) produce and secrete C3 complement component as part of neuroinflammatory responses. These infiltrating cells are a significant source of C3 in the CNS during neurodegeneration.

Peripheral immune cells infiltrating the brain parenchyma, including microglia activation and peripheral macrophage/T cell infiltration, directly produce complement-activating signals through pattern recognition receptors and release of pro-inflammatory cytokines that activate the classical and alternative complement pathways on local neural tissue.

Peripheral immune cells that infiltrate the brain parenchyma can interact with meningeal lymphatic vessels and their drainage function, while meningeal lymphatic drainage capacity directly influences the recruitment and clearance of peripheral immune cells from the CNS. This bidirectional relationship is critical for maintaining CNS immune homeostasis.

Peripheral immune cells that infiltrate the brain parenchyma can interact with meningeal lymphatic vessels and their drainage function, while meningeal lymphatic drainage capacity directly influences the recruitment and clearance of peripheral immune cells from the CNS. This bidirectional relationship is critical for maintaining CNS immune homeostasis.

Peripheral immune cells that infiltrate the brain can differentiate into and promote Th1 (T helper 1) cell responses through antigen presentation and pro-inflammatory cytokine production (IFN-γ, TNF-α, IL-2) in the brain parenchyma. This represents a direct causal pathway where peripheral immune infiltration drives local Th1 polarization and accumulation.

Tight junction proteins (claudins, occludin, ZO-1) maintain the integrity of the blood-brain barrier and perivascular space boundaries. Disruption of these proteins increases paracellular permeability and alters the geometry of interstitial space, thereby directly affecting interstitial space fraction (ISF volume as percentage of brain tissue).

Tight junction proteins (claudins, occludin, ZO-1) maintain the blood-brain barrier integrity. Disruption of these proteins increases paracellular permeability, allowing enhanced clearance or accumulation of ISF Aβ across the BBB, directly affecting interstitial fluid Aβ concentrations.

Tight junction proteins (claudins, occludin, ZO-1) maintain blood-brain barrier integrity; their disruption increases paracellular transport and ISF clearance dysfunction, leading to tau accumulation in interstitial fluid. Compromised tight junctions impair the glymphatic system's ability to clear tau from the brain parenchyma.

Tight junction proteins (claudins, occludin, ZO-1) form the blood-brain barrier (BBB) and regulate paracellular transport. Disruption of tight junctions increases BBB permeability, which directly increases interstitial fluid (ISF) volume by allowing fluid extravasation into the brain parenchyma.

Tight junction proteins (claudins, occludin, ZO-1) maintain blood-brain barrier (BBB) integrity. Disruption of these proteins increases BBB permeability, allowing peripheral immune cells to infiltrate the brain parenchyma. In Alzheimer's disease, impaired tight junctions facilitate neuroinflammation through immune cell recruitment.

Tight junction proteins (claudins, occludin, ZO-1) form the blood-brain barrier and maintain perivascular space integrity by controlling paracellular transport and fluid dynamics. Disruption of tight junction proteins compromises BBB function and alters perivascular space homeostasis, affecting interstitial fluid drainage and solute clearance.

Disruption of tight junction proteins (claudins, occludin, ZO-1) at the blood-brain barrier compromises BBB integrity, allowing increased leakage of peripheral substances and immune factors into the brain parenchyma. This breach triggers neuroinflammation and oxidative stress that directly damages lysosomes through membrane permeabilization and impaired lysosomal function.

Tight junction protein disruption compromises blood-brain barrier integrity, allowing peripheral pathological factors to enter the CNS and accumulate in lysosomes of brain cells, impairing lysosomal clearance capacity. Additionally, BBB dysfunction leads to neuroinflammation and impaired clearance of misfolded proteins, overwhelming lysosomal degradation pathways.

Disruption or dysfunction of tight junction proteins (claudins, occludin, ZO-1) compromises blood-brain barrier integrity, allowing leukocyte infiltration and activation of microglial and astrocytic responses that drive neuroinflammation. Loss of BBB integrity is a direct consequence of tight junction dysfunction and a primary trigger for neuroinflammatory cascade activation.

During cell death, particularly ferroptosis and necrosis, cellular compartments including lysosomes rupture and lose membrane integrity, leading to direct release of sequestered iron from lysosomal iron stores into the cytoplasm. This iron release is a direct consequence of lysosomal membrane disruption during the cell death process.

Cell death processes, particularly in microglia and astrocytes, lead to the release and accumulation of damaged mitochondria. These fragmented mitochondria are then released extracellularly where they can propagate neuroinflammatory responses and further neurodegeneration in Alzheimer's disease.

Mitochondrial ROS production amplifies cell death pathways through oxidative stress-induced damage and activation of apoptotic cascades. Conversely, excessive cell death can trigger secondary mitochondrial dysfunction and sustained ROS generation, creating a feed-forward amplification loop.

mPTP (mitochondrial permeability transition pore) opening causes mitochondrial depolarization, calcium dysregulation, and release of pro-apoptotic factors (cytochrome c, AIF), directly triggering cell death pathways including apoptosis and necrosis. In Alzheimer's disease, amyloid-beta and tau pathology promote mPTP opening, which then executes neuronal death.

Neuronal cell death through ferroptosis and pyroptosis pathways depletes cellular glutathione by consuming antioxidant defenses and disrupting the Nrf2-mediated transcriptional regulation of glutathione synthesis genes. Cell death processes exhaust glutathione pools as cells attempt to counteract oxidative and ferroptotic stress.

GPX4 is a key ferroptosis suppressor that prevents lipid peroxidation-induced cell death. Loss or inhibition of GPX4 activity directly triggers ferroptotic cell death through accumulation of lipid reactive oxygen species.

Cell death, particularly neuronal apoptosis and necrosis, releases intracellular iron into the extracellular space and disrupts iron homeostatic mechanisms, increasing the labile iron pool. Iron accumulation in dying cells and subsequent release contributes to oxidative stress in Alzheimer's pathology.

Cell death, particularly ferroptosis, is a mechanism that produces lipid peroxides as a central executioner of cell death. During ferroptosis, iron-dependent lipid peroxidation is initiated, generating lipid peroxides that accumulate and trigger the cascade leading to cell death.

Cell death, particularly neuronal death through apoptosis and necrosis, triggers cellular senescence in surrounding cells as a stress response and through paracrine signaling from dying cells. Dying cells release damage-associated molecular patterns (DAMPs) and cytokines that induce p16/p21-mediated cell cycle arrest and senescence in neighboring cells.

Cell death, particularly ferroptosis, directly depletes glutathione (GSH) as ferroptotic pathways involve inhibition of the GSH-GPx4 antioxidant system, which consumes GSH in the process of lipid peroxidation neutralization. GSH depletion is both a trigger and consequence of ferroptotic cell death.

Active caspase-1 (the catalytically active form of caspase-1) directly promotes pyroptotic and apoptotic cell death pathways through cleavage of substrates like gasdermin D and pro-caspase-3, thereby amplifying cell death. In Alzheimer's disease, neuroinflammation-driven caspase-1 activation contributes to neuronal death.

Cell death, particularly pyroptosis, triggers NLRP3 inflammasome activation which directly produces and releases IL-18 as a mature inflammatory cytokine. NLRP3-mediated caspase-1 activation cleaves pro-IL-18 into active IL-18 that is secreted during pyroptotic cell death.

Cell death, particularly neuronal apoptosis and necrosis, triggers the release of danger-associated molecular patterns (DAMPs) and cellular contents that activate microglia and astrocytes to produce IL1B as part of the innate immune response. IL1B is a key pro-inflammatory cytokine released in response to neuronal cell death in Alzheimer's disease pathology.

Cell death, particularly neuronal apoptosis and pyroptosis, releases danger-associated molecular patterns (DAMPs) that activate the NLRP3 inflammasome complex through pattern recognition receptors. NLRP3 activation is a key downstream consequence of neuronal cell death in Alzheimer's disease pathology.

Neuronal cell death, particularly of neurons that form synaptic connections, directly results in loss of dendritic spines through physical elimination of the postsynaptic structures that comprise these spines. Cell death triggers dendritic spine retraction and elimination as a consequence of neuronal degeneration.

Neuronal cell death, a hallmark of Alzheimer's disease pathology, directly reduces the number and functional integrity of synapses through loss of presynaptic and postsynaptic neurons. Cell death eliminates the neuronal compartments required for synaptic transmission and maintenance.

Neuronal cell death, particularly of cholinergic and glutamatergic neurons, directly reduces the number of viable synapses and impairs synaptic transmission, thereby increasing synaptic vulnerability through loss of synaptic density and functional capacity. The remaining synapses in degenerating neural circuits become more susceptible to additional insults due to reduced neuronal support and altered network homeostasis.

Calpain activation during neurodegeneration cleaves cytoskeletal proteins and promotes proteolytic cascades that trigger neuronal cell death pathways, including apoptosis and necroptosis. This proteolytic activity directly contributes to neuronal loss in Alzheimer's disease models.

Cell death, particularly neuronal apoptosis and necrosis in Alzheimer's disease, results in loss of plasma membrane integrity and mitochondrial dysfunction, leading to uncontrolled release of intracellular calcium stores into the cytosol. Additionally, dying cells exhibit dysregulated calcium handling through impaired SERCA pumps and PMCA exchangers, causing cytosolic calcium accumulation.

Cell death, particularly through mitochondrial permeability transition pore (mPTP) opening and necrosis pathways, leads to uncontrolled mitochondrial Ca²⁺ release and dysregulation. VDAC1-mediated mitochondrial dysfunction in Alzheimer's disease models shows that cell death processes directly elevate mitochondrial Ca²⁺ levels through loss of Ca²⁺ homeostatic control.

Cell death processes, particularly necrotic and ferroptotic pathways, trigger mitochondrial calcium dysregulation through disrupted calcium handling mechanisms at mitochondria-associated membranes (MAMs) and altered mitochondrial membrane potential. This leads to pathological Ca²⁺ accumulation within mitochondria, which further perpetuates cell death in a feed-forward cycle.

Perivascular spaces are key components of the glymphatic system through which cerebrospinal fluid flows to clear interstitial solutes. AQP4 (aquaporin-4) localization on parenchymal astrocytic endfeet is essential for facilitating this CSF-interstitial fluid exchange, and dysfunction of perivascular space clearance directly affects AQP4-dependent glymphatic function and protein clearance including Aβ.

Perivascular space dysfunction disrupts the glymphatic system and blood-brain barrier integrity, leading to loss of astrocyte endfoot anchoring proteins (like agrin) and compromised aquaporin-4 localization at astrocyte endfeet, which are critical for maintaining endfoot structural integrity.

Enlarged perivascular spaces indicate compromised glymphatic clearance and impaired interstitial fluid drainage, which directly disrupts blood-brain barrier integrity through reduced clearance of toxic proteins and increased fluid accumulation. Conversely, BBB compromise allows fluid extravasation into perivascular spaces, enlarging them and further impairing drainage function.

Enlarged perivascular spaces indicate compromised glymphatic clearance and impaired interstitial fluid drainage, which directly disrupts blood-brain barrier integrity through reduced clearance of toxic proteins and increased fluid accumulation. Conversely, BBB compromise allows fluid extravasation into perivascular spaces, enlarging them and further impairing drainage function.

Perivascular spaces facilitate glymphatic clearance along cerebral vessels and their dysfunction directly compromises blood-brain barrier (BBB) integrity through impaired interstitial fluid-cerebrospinal fluid exchange and reduced clearance of BBB-disrupting substances. Conversely, BBB disruption increases perivascular space pathology by allowing immune cell infiltration and accumulation of neurotoxic proteins in perivascular regions.

Perivascular spaces facilitate glymphatic clearance along cerebral vessels and their dysfunction directly compromises blood-brain barrier (BBB) integrity through impaired interstitial fluid-cerebrospinal fluid exchange and reduced clearance of BBB-disrupting substances. Conversely, BBB disruption increases perivascular space pathology by allowing immune cell infiltration and accumulation of neurotoxic proteins in perivascular regions.

Perivascular spaces (Virchow-Robin spaces) are key pathways for glymphatic clearance of solutes from the brain parenchyma into cerebrospinal fluid. Albumin, a major CSF protein, is cleared through perivascular routes; dysfunction or changes in perivascular space integrity directly affects the clearance dynamics and concentration ratios of albumin in CSF.

Perivascular spaces facilitate CSF-interstitial fluid exchange through the glymphatic system, and soluble proteins including PDGF receptor beta (PDGFRb) are cleared or transported through these spaces as part of CSF-mediated clearance mechanisms. Perivascular space dysfunction impairs the clearance and homeostasis of proteins in the perivascular compartment, including those derived from or transported through these spaces.

The perivascular space (PVS) serves as a conduit for glymphatic fluid clearance, and maintenance of optimal PVS dimensions and fluid dynamics is necessary for proper polarization and function of aquaporin-4 (AQP4) water channels at astrocytic endfeet. Dysfunction or enlargement of perivascular spaces can disrupt the osmotic gradients and fluid flow patterns that regulate AQP4 polarization at the blood-brain interface.

The perivascular space (PVS), also known as the Virchow-Robin space, serves as a critical clearance pathway for interstitial solutes including amyloid-beta (Aβ). Aβ is transported from the interstitial fluid (ISF) into perivascular spaces via aquaporin-4-mediated fluid dynamics and glymphatic circulation, where it is subsequently cleared from the brain.

The perivascular space (PVS) functions as a clearance pathway for interstitial solutes including tau protein. Dysfunction or impaired clearance through perivascular spaces leads to accumulation of tau in the interstitial fluid (ISF).

LRP1 is a critical receptor for clearing amyloid-beta and other ligands from the perivascular space along arterial walls, while perivascular space integrity and function can be compromised by LRP1 dysfunction, affecting clearance mechanisms. The perivascular space serves as a major route for interstitial fluid drainage, and LRP1 expression on endothelial cells and pericytes lining these spaces regulates solute clearance through this pathway.

LRP1 is a critical receptor for clearing amyloid-beta and other ligands from the perivascular space along arterial walls, while perivascular space integrity and function can be compromised by LRP1 dysfunction, affecting clearance mechanisms. The perivascular space serves as a major route for interstitial fluid drainage, and LRP1 expression on endothelial cells and pericytes lining these spaces regulates solute clearance through this pathway.

Pericytes actively regulate perivascular space dimensions through their contractile properties and endothelial interactions; conversely, enlarged perivascular spaces in neurodegenerative conditions reflect pericyte dysfunction and reduced pericyte coverage. In Alzheimer's disease, pericyte loss contributes to perivascular space enlargement, while altered perivascular space dynamics may further compromise pericyte survival and function.

Pericytes actively regulate perivascular space dimensions through their contractile properties and endothelial interactions; conversely, enlarged perivascular spaces in neurodegenerative conditions reflect pericyte dysfunction and reduced pericyte coverage. In Alzheimer's disease, pericyte loss contributes to perivascular space enlargement, while altered perivascular space dynamics may further compromise pericyte survival and function.

Perivascular spaces facilitate clearance of solutes including iron through glymphatic mechanisms; impaired perivascular function reduces iron clearance leading to iron accumulation, while excessive iron can damage perivascular endothelial barriers and impair interstitial fluid dynamics.

Perivascular spaces facilitate clearance of solutes including iron through glymphatic mechanisms; impaired perivascular function reduces iron clearance leading to iron accumulation, while excessive iron can damage perivascular endothelial barriers and impair interstitial fluid dynamics.

Perivascular spaces serve as routes for glymphatic clearance and interstitial fluid drainage. Impaired perivascular clearance in Alzheimer's disease leads to accumulation of amyloid-beta and other toxic proteins, which triggers neuroinflammatory responses including the activation and polarization of resident astrocytes toward the pro-inflammatory A1 phenotype.

Perivascular spaces (PVS) form a critical clearance pathway for interstitial solutes including iron, functioning as part of the glymphatic system. The structural and functional integrity of perivascular spaces directly determines the efficiency of iron clearance from the brain interstitium into cerebrospinal fluid (CSF), with impaired PVS function reducing CSF iron clearance capacity.

Perivascular spaces serve as anatomical conduits for fluid movement that directly interfaces with meningeal lymphatic vessels; the structural integrity and patency of perivascular spaces regulates interstitial fluid flow dynamics that feed into meningeal lymphatic drainage pathways, while meningeal lymphatic function influences perivascular fluid clearance.

Perivascular spaces serve as anatomical conduits for fluid movement that directly interfaces with meningeal lymphatic vessels; the structural integrity and patency of perivascular spaces regulates interstitial fluid flow dynamics that feed into meningeal lymphatic drainage pathways, while meningeal lymphatic function influences perivascular fluid clearance.

Perivascular spaces (PVS) are the anatomical channels through which the glymphatic system operates, and their structural integrity and function directly enable sleep-dependent glymphatic clearance of interstitial solutes including amyloid-beta. The coupling between sleep and glymphatic function depends on aquaporin-4 water channel activity in perivascular spaces, which expands during sleep to facilitate fluid exchange.

cPLA2 activation liberates arachidonic acid from membrane phospholipids, which undergoes β-oxidation in mitochondria and generates ROS as a metabolic byproduct. Additionally, cPLA2-derived lysophospholipids can disrupt mitochondrial membrane integrity, further promoting ROS production.

cPLA2 (cytosolic phospholipase A2) catalyzes the release of arachidonic acid from membrane phospholipids, generating lipid mediators and reactive oxygen species that trigger apoptotic pathways and neuronal cell death. Excessive cPLA2 activation in neurodegenerative conditions leads to calcium dysregulation and mitochondrial dysfunction, promoting neuronal apoptosis.

cPLA2 activation releases arachidonic acid from membrane phospholipids, which generates lipid peroxides and promotes ferroptotic cell death through lipid ROS accumulation and iron-dependent processes. cPLA2-mediated phospholipid remodeling directly sensitizes cells to ferroptotic pathways.

cPLA2 activation generates lysophospholipids and arachidonic acid, which promote lipid peroxidation and iron-dependent ferroptosis through increased reactive oxygen species production and disruption of cellular redox homeostasis. In Alzheimer's disease, elevated cPLA2 activity contributes to neuronal vulnerability to ferroptotic cell death.

cPLA2 activation triggers phospholipid hydrolysis in neuronal membranes, releasing arachidonic acid and generating lipid peroxides that promote iron release from ferritin and transferrin, thereby increasing the labile iron pool. Enhanced labile iron subsequently catalyzes Fenton chemistry producing ROS, which is implicated in Alzheimer's neurodegeneration.

cPLA2 activation releases arachidonic acid from membrane phospholipids, which undergoes oxidative metabolism to produce reactive oxygen species (ROS) and lipid peroxides, directly increasing oxidative stress. This pathway is particularly relevant in neuroinflammation associated with Alzheimer's pathology.

cPLA2 (cytosolic phospholipase A2) catalyzes the hydrolysis of membrane phospholipids to release arachidonic acid and lysophospholipids, a process that triggers Ca²⁺ release from intracellular stores (IP3-mediated) and activates store-operated Ca²⁺ entry (SOCE), thereby increasing cytosolic Ca²⁺ levels.

DMT1 (divalent metal transporter 1) is the primary iron importer at the cell membrane, and iron imported via DMT1 is subsequently distributed to various cellular compartments including lysosomes. Dexras1 (dexamethasone-induced ras-related protein 1) has been implicated in iron homeostasis signaling and could modulate DMT1 activity or iron trafficking to lysosomes.

DMT1 (divalent metal transporter 1) is the primary iron importer at the blood-brain barrier and in brain cells, directly controlling iron uptake that contributes to total brain iron levels. Dexras1, a GTPase regulated by iron and nitric oxide, can modulate DMT1 expression and iron transport capacity, thereby regulating the accumulation of total brain iron.

Ferroportin is the primary iron exporter on lysosomal membranes that mediates the egress of iron from the lysosomal lumen to the cytoplasm. By transporting iron out of lysosomes, ferroportin directly regulates lysosomal iron content.

Ferroportin is the primary iron exporter in the brain, localizing to the blood-brain barrier and astrocytes. By regulating iron efflux from the brain parenchyma, ferroportin directly controls total brain iron levels through a negative feedback mechanism.

Ferroportin (iron exporter) and transferrin receptor (iron importer) are functionally coupled iron homeostasis regulators that work in opposing directions to maintain cellular iron balance. Ferroportin expression is post-transcriptionally regulated by iron-responsive elements (IREs) in an iron-dependent manner, while transferrin receptor expression responds to the same iron-sensing pathway through iron regulatory proteins (IRPs), creating a coordinated bidirectional regulatory relationship.

Ferroportin (iron exporter) and transferrin receptor (iron importer) are functionally coupled iron homeostasis regulators that work in opposing directions to maintain cellular iron balance. Ferroportin expression is post-transcriptionally regulated by iron-responsive elements (IREs) in an iron-dependent manner, while transferrin receptor expression responds to the same iron-sensing pathway through iron regulatory proteins (IRPs), creating a coordinated bidirectional regulatory relationship.

Ferroportin is the primary iron exporter in astrocytes, directly controlling intracellular iron levels and hence the distribution of iron between astrocytes and other brain cells. By regulating ferroportin expression and function, iron efflux from astrocytes is controlled, thereby determining astrocyte iron distribution patterns.

Ferroportin is the primary iron exporter on cells including brain microvascular endothelial cells and macrophages. By regulating cellular iron export, ferroportin activity directly influences the availability of iron in the cerebrospinal fluid and interstitial fluid, thereby affecting iron clearance mechanisms from the CNS.

Hepcidin is a circulating hormone that directly regulates ferroportin expression and localization on the cell surface. Hepcidin binds to ferroportin, causing its internalization and degradation, thereby decreasing systemic iron export and increasing serum iron levels.

Ferritin iron, particularly free iron released from ferritin degradation, participates in Fenton chemistry to generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) including within mitochondria. Excess labile iron catalyzes the conversion of hydrogen peroxide to hydroxyl radicals, directly increasing mitochondrial ROS production.

Ferritin iron, particularly Fe2+ released from ferritin under oxidative stress or acidic conditions, directly participates as a catalyst in the Fenton reaction (Fe2+ + H2O2 → Fe3+ + OH• + OH−), generating highly reactive hydroxyl radicals that cause oxidative damage.

Ferritin iron, particularly when iron is released from ferritin in oxidative conditions, generates reactive oxygen species (ROS) through Fenton chemistry, which directly oxidizes and depletes glutathione (GSH), the major intracellular antioxidant. Increased iron-catalyzed oxidative stress consumes glutathione as it is utilized to neutralize ROS via glutathione peroxidase.

Ferritin iron stores regulate GPX4 activity through iron availability, as GPX4 requires iron-containing cofactors and iron homeostasis influences the selenoprotein synthesis pathway and oxidative stress levels that modulate GPX4 expression and function.

Ferritin releases iron upon degradation or under reducing conditions, and this iron is a direct substrate for iron-sulfur cluster assembly through the iron-sulfur cluster synthesis machinery (ISC/CIA complexes). Iron-sulfur clusters require free iron (Fe2+/Fe3+) as their core component.

Ferritin releases iron through ferritinophagy, a selective autophagy pathway where ferritin-containing autophagosomes are trafficked to lysosomes for degradation and iron release into the lysosomal lumen. This is a well-characterized mechanism of iron mobilization where ferritin iron is directly transferred to lysosomal compartments via autophagosomal-lysosomal fusion.

NCOA4 is a selective autophagy receptor that directly binds ferritin and mediates ferritinophagy, the autophagic degradation of ferritin. This process releases iron from ferritin stores, thereby regulating the levels of ferritin-bound iron.

Ferritin iron can be released and mobilized from ferritin storage to contribute to the labile iron pool, which is incorporated into total brain iron compartments. Iron released from ferritin degradation or ferritinophagy directly increases bioavailable iron that becomes part of total brain iron stores.

Ferritin iron can catalyze the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) through Fenton chemistry, which directly oxidizes and depletes glutathione (GSH) as a compensatory antioxidant response. Elevated labile iron increases oxidative stress, driving GSH consumption faster than it can be regenerated.

Ferritin iron promotes α-synuclein oxidation and aggregation through iron-catalyzed generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), which destabilizes monomeric α-synuclein and accelerates its polymerization into pathogenic fibrils. Iron also directly binds to α-synuclein and modulates its conformational changes.

Deferiprone is an iron chelator that binds and removes ferritin-bound iron, reducing labile iron pools and ferritin iron content through chelation chemistry. This represents a direct pharmacological interaction where the chelator directly decreases the bioavailable iron associated with ferritin.

Ferritin serves as a circulating iron carrier protein that delivers iron to oligodendrocytes (OL), which require iron for myelin synthesis and maintenance through iron-dependent enzymes like ribonucleotide reductase. Iron uptake by oligodendrocytes is mediated by transferrin receptor-1 and potentially ferritin receptors, allowing ferritin-bound iron to be internalized and contribute to cellular iron content.

Ferritin releases iron upon degradation and iron mobilization, which becomes bioavailable for direct binding to tau protein at its iron-binding domains. Iron binding to tau alters tau conformation, promotes aggregation, and enhances pathological tau-tau interactions characteristic of Alzheimer's disease.

Ferritin is the primary iron storage protein that regulates systemic and local iron bioavailability. Astrocytes express ferritin and utilize circulating/locally available iron to maintain their iron distribution and homeostasis, with ferritin serving as the source of iron that astrocytes take up and redistribute.

Iron (Fe2+/Fe3+) serves as a critical cofactor for cystathionine β-synthase (CBS) and cystathionine γ-lyase (CSE), the two key enzymes in the transsulfuration pathway that produces hydrogen sulfide (H2S). Iron deficiency or iron overload can impair the activity of these iron-dependent enzymes, thereby modulating H2S production capacity.

Ferrous iron (Fe2+) can directly react with protein thiols to form disulfide bonds and oxidize cysteine residues, thereby reducing the availability of free sulfhydryl groups for sulfhydration reactions. Iron-catalyzed Fenton chemistry generates reactive oxygen species that promote cysteine oxidation, competing with the enzymatic sulfhydration process.

Ferritin iron can be released and participate in Fenton chemistry, generating reactive oxygen species (ROS) that directly damage mitochondrial membranes and impair calcium handling machinery, leading to dysregulated mitochondrial Ca²⁺ accumulation. Additionally, iron accumulation in mitochondria can directly interfere with electron transport chain function and calcium/H⁺ antiporter activity, causing mitochondrial Ca²⁺ overload.

Ferritin releases iron through ferroxidase activity and iron mobilization pathways, directly increasing the pool of cytosolic labile iron that is not bound to proteins. This represents the primary mechanism by which ferritin regulates intracellular iron homeostasis.

The Fenton reaction generates hydroxyl radicals (.OH) that cause oxidative damage to cellular proteins and lipids, which activates Nrf2 as a compensatory antioxidant response through oxidative stress sensing. Nrf2 is released from Keap1 inhibition when cellular thiols are oxidized by reactive oxygen species, allowing nuclear translocation and activation of antioxidant response elements.

The Fenton reaction generates hydroxyl radicals (•OH) that cause oxidative damage to lysosomal membranes, impairing lysosomal membrane integrity and function. Iron accumulation in AD brains catalyzes Fenton chemistry, leading to lysosomal membrane permeabilization (LMP) and release of cathepsins.

The Fenton reaction generates hydroxyl radicals (•OH) from ferrous iron and hydrogen peroxide, which directly damage lysosomal membranes and proteins through lipid peroxidation and oxidative modification. This leads to lysosomal membrane permeabilization, leakage of cathepsin proteases, and overall lysosomal dysfunction.

The Fenton reaction generates hydroxyl radicals and other reactive oxygen species (ROS) that directly activate the NLRP3 inflammasome through oxidative damage to mitochondria and direct modification of NLRP3 components, triggering inflammasome assembly and caspase-1 activation.

The Fenton reaction generates reactive oxygen species (ROS), particularly hydroxyl radicals, which directly damage lipids, proteins, and DNA. This iron-catalyzed oxidative stress triggers ferroptosis, an iron-dependent form of regulated cell death characterized by lipid peroxidation and mitochondrial dysfunction.

The Fenton reaction generates hydroxyl radicals (•OH) that oxidatively damage glutathione (GSH) through direct chemical oxidation, depleting the antioxidant pool. This oxidative stress-induced depletion of glutathione is a well-established consequence of iron-catalyzed free radical generation in neuronal cells.

The Fenton reaction generates hydroxyl radicals (•OH) that directly oxidize GPX4 protein and deplete its cofactor selenium, thereby reducing GPX4 enzymatic activity. GPX4 is a selenoprotein that catalyzes the reduction of lipid hydroperoxides, and oxidative damage from Fenton-derived radicals impairs its function.

The Fenton reaction generates hydroxyl radicals from ferrous iron and hydrogen peroxide, which can directly oxidatively damage iron-sulfur cluster [4Fe-4S] and [2Fe-2S] structures, causing their degradation and release of iron. This is a well-established chemical mechanism of iron-sulfur cluster vulnerability to radical-mediated oxidative stress.

The Fenton reaction catalyzes the conversion of hydrogen peroxide to highly reactive hydroxyl radicals in the presence of ferrous iron (Fe2+), directly generating reactive oxygen species (ROS) that constitute oxidative stress. This iron-dependent radical generation is a primary mechanism driving oxidative damage in neuronal cells.

The Fenton reaction generates reactive oxygen species (ROS), particularly hydroxyl radicals, which can cause oxidative DNA damage including double-strand breaks. This DNA damage activates the cGAS-STING pathway, where cGAS senses cytosolic DNA and becomes catalytically active to produce cGAMP.

The Fenton reaction generates reactive oxygen species (ROS), particularly hydroxyl radicals, which directly trigger oxidative stress that activates the NLRP3 inflammasome through pattern recognition of ROS-induced cellular damage and mitochondrial dysfunction.

The Fenton reaction generates reactive oxygen species (ROS), particularly hydroxyl radicals, which can cause oxidative damage to cellular components and activate STING through ROS-dependent pathways. STING activation occurs as part of the innate immune response to oxidative stress and can be triggered by mitochondrial damage and dsDNA release induced by ROS.

FSP1 (ferroptosis suppressor protein 1) is a radical-trapping antioxidant that directly reduces ubiquinone to ubiquinol in the inner mitochondrial membrane, which can regenerate reduced glutathione (GSH) through the mevalonate pathway and antioxidant defense systems by sparing GSH consumption during ferroptosis suppression.

FSP1 (ferroptosis suppressor protein 1) is a key negative regulator of ferroptosis that functions as a NAD(P)H-dependent oxidoreductase, catalyzing the reduction of ubiquinone to ubiquinol in the inner mitochondrial membrane, thereby directly suppressing ferroptotic cell death through lipid peroxide detoxification.

FSP1 (Ferroptosis Suppressor Protein 1) is a NAD(P)H-dependent oxidoreductase that directly catalyzes the reduction of coenzyme Q to its active form (ubiquinol), which functions as a lipophilic antioxidant in cell membranes to prevent ferroptotic lipid peroxidation. This enzymatic activity of FSP1 mechanistically confers ferroptosis resistance by maintaining cellular redox homeostasis.

FSP1 (ferroptosis suppressor protein 1) is a NAD(P)H oxidase that catalyzes the reduction of coenzyme Q to its antioxidant form (ubiquinol), directly counteracting ferroptosis by maintaining cellular redox homeostasis and preventing lipid peroxidation.

Nrf2 activation suppresses ferroptosis through the Nrf2-Keap1 pathway, which promotes antioxidant responses and iron/redox homeostasis, thereby reducing ferroptosis sensitivity and increasing ferroptosis resistance.

Glutathione is a critical antioxidant cofactor for glutathione peroxidase (GPx4) and other antioxidant systems that directly suppress ferroptosis. High glutathione levels promote ferroptosis resistance by maintaining lipid peroxide reduction capacity and preventing ferroptotic cell death.

Mitochondrial ROS accumulation triggers ferroptotic cell death through iron-dependent lipid peroxidation and depletion of antioxidant defenses, thereby reducing the population of ferroptosis-resistant cells. Conversely, ferroptosis resistance mechanisms (such as GPX4 upregulation or iron chelation) directly suppress mitochondrial ROS generation.

Elevated labile iron pool (LIP) promotes ferroptosis through Fenton chemistry-mediated lipid peroxidation and ROS generation, thereby reducing ferroptosis resistance. Conversely, ferroptosis-resistant cells actively suppress labile iron accumulation through upregulation of iron storage proteins (ferritin) and iron export mechanisms (ferroportin).

Oxidative stress triggers ferroptosis, a form of iron-dependent cell death characterized by lipid peroxidation and ROS accumulation. Ferroptosis-resistant cells have upregulated antioxidant defenses (e.g., GPX4, system xc-) that directly suppress oxidative stress through enhanced lipid peroxide reduction and antioxidant capacity.

Ferroptosis resistance mechanisms actively suppress ferroptotic cell death pathways through protective signaling cascades (e.g., FOXM1-AURKA-NRF2 axis, PRR11-DHODH axis) that prevent lipid peroxidation and mitochondrial dysfunction. Cells achieving ferroptosis resistance demonstrate reduced cell death rates as these protective mechanisms directly counter ferroptosis execution.

Elevated total brain iron promotes ferroptosis through Fenton chemistry and lipid peroxidation, making cells that accumulate iron more susceptible to ferroptotic death rather than resistant to it. Conversely, ferroptosis-resistant cells would be expected to have mechanisms limiting iron accumulation or sequestration, potentially through altered iron homeostasis pathways.

Ferroptosis-resistant states involve upregulation of iron homeostasis mechanisms that reduce labile iron availability, including decreased lysosomal iron accumulation through altered ferritinophagy and lysosomal iron export. Conversely, elevated lysosomal iron promotes ferroptotic cell death, making ferroptosis resistance mechanistically dependent on suppressing lysosomal iron accumulation.

Ferroptosis-resistant states involve upregulation of iron homeostasis mechanisms that reduce labile iron availability, including decreased lysosomal iron accumulation through altered ferritinophagy and lysosomal iron export. Conversely, elevated lysosomal iron promotes ferroptotic cell death, making ferroptosis resistance mechanistically dependent on suppressing lysosomal iron accumulation.

Ferroptosis-resistant cells escape iron-dependent death pathways, allowing continued metabolic activity and proliferation that can lead to cellular senescence accumulation. Conversely, ferroptosis induction reduces senescent cell burden by triggering death in senescence-prone populations.

Ferroptosis resistance mechanisms, through iron homeostasis and lipid peroxidation control via GPX4 and FSP1, directly preserve membrane structural integrity by preventing ferroptotic lipid peroxidation and membrane disruption.

Ferroptosis resistance can lead to cargo accumulation by preventing the autophagic-lysosomal clearance pathway that is activated during ferroptotic cell death. When cells resist ferroptosis, they fail to activate iron-dependent degradation mechanisms, allowing misfolded proteins and damaged organelles to accumulate.

Lipid droplets sequester lipids and represent a cellular lipid storage mechanism that can reduce ferroptotic sensitivity by limiting the availability of polyunsaturated fatty acids for lipid peroxidation, the key driver of ferroptosis. Cells with increased lipid droplet accumulation show enhanced ferroptosis resistance through reduced ferroptotic substrate availability.

Ferroptosis resistance activates compensatory autophagy pathways as cells suppress ferroptotic death mechanisms, leading to increased autophagic flux and autophagosome accumulation. This represents a direct metabolic shift where ferroptosis-resistant cells upregulate autophagy as an alternative degradation pathway to manage cellular stress and protein aggregates.

GPX4 (glutathione peroxidase 4) is a critical ferroptosis suppressor that catalyzes the reduction of lipid hydroperoxides, thereby preventing ferroptotic cell death. Loss or inhibition of GPX4 activity directly triggers ferroptosis through accumulation of lipid peroxides and iron-dependent oxidative stress.

Glutathione (GSH) is a critical antioxidant cofactor for glutathione peroxidase (GPx4), which prevents ferroptosis by reducing lipid hydroperoxides. Depletion of glutathione directly reduces GPx4 activity and allows ferroptosis to proceed, making glutathione a direct inhibitor of ferroptosis.

Ferroptosis is an iron-dependent form of cell death where labile iron accumulation drives lipid peroxidation; neuronal ferroptosis releases intracellular iron into the extracellular space and contributes to iron dysregulation, while elevated brain iron can sensitize neurons to ferroptotic death through increased Fenton chemistry and ROS generation.

Ferroptosis is an iron-dependent form of cell death where labile iron accumulation drives lipid peroxidation; neuronal ferroptosis releases intracellular iron into the extracellular space and contributes to iron dysregulation, while elevated brain iron can sensitize neurons to ferroptotic death through increased Fenton chemistry and ROS generation.

Mitochondrial ROS generation is a key driver of ferroptosis, as excessive ROS production overwhelms antioxidant defenses and triggers iron-dependent lipid peroxidation and ferroptotic cell death. Ferroptosis is fundamentally characterized by mitochondrial dysfunction and ROS accumulation as upstream initiating events.

Ferroptosis is an iron-dependent form of cell death driven by lipid peroxidation and characterized by excessive reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation, which directly produces and amplifies oxidative stress. The ferroptotic process inherently involves iron-catalyzed Fenton chemistry that generates hydroxyl radicals and other ROS, constituting a direct causal mechanism.

Nrf2 activation induces expression of antioxidant response elements (ARE) and ferroptosis-suppressor genes (including ferritin, SLC7A11/xCT, GPX4), which directly inhibit ferroptosis by reducing lipid peroxidation and maintaining iron homeostasis. This represents a direct protective mechanism where Nrf2 activity prevents ferroptosis execution.

Iron-sulfur cluster [4Fe-4S] biosynthesis and maintenance are critical for mitochondrial respiratory chain complexes and aconitase function. Ferroptosis is an iron-dependent cell death mechanism involving Fenton chemistry and lipid peroxidation, which disrupts iron-sulfur cluster integrity through oxidative damage and sequestration of iron cofactors into ferroptotic pathways, thereby compromising Fe-S cluster-dependent enzyme activities.

Ferroptosis is an iron-dependent form of cell death characterized by lethal lipid peroxidation driven by ferrous iron (Fe2+). During ferroptosis execution, cellular iron homeostasis is disrupted, leading to depletion of ferritin-bound iron stores as iron is mobilized from ferritin to participate in Fenton chemistry and lipid peroxidation reactions.

Ferroptosis, an iron-dependent form of cell death, can be modulated by tau pathology and iron dysregulation. Conversely, tau's iron-binding capacity influences cellular iron homeostasis, which directly regulates ferroptosis susceptibility through Fenton chemistry and lipid peroxidation.

Ferroptosis, an iron-dependent form of cell death, can be modulated by tau pathology and iron dysregulation. Conversely, tau's iron-binding capacity influences cellular iron homeostasis, which directly regulates ferroptosis susceptibility through Fenton chemistry and lipid peroxidation.

Ferroptosis is an iron-dependent form of regulated cell death characterized by lipid peroxidation and mitochondrial dysfunction. Phosphatidylserine (PS) exposure on the outer plasma membrane is a hallmark of ferroptotic cells, serving as an 'eat me' signal for recognition by phagocytes, analogous to apoptotic cells but via distinct ferroptosis-specific pathways.

Ferroptosis is an iron-dependent form of cell death involving lipid peroxidation and oxidative stress that directly damages lysosomal membranes through accumulation of reactive oxygen species and lipid peroxides. The ferroptotic cascade leads to lysosomal rupture and release of hydrolytic enzymes, which is a direct downstream consequence of the ferroptotic pathway.

Ferroptosis triggers the release of damage-associated molecular patterns (DAMPs) and iron-dependent reactive oxygen species that activate pattern recognition receptors upstream of NLRP3 inflammasome assembly. This leads to caspase-1 activation and IL-1β/IL-18 secretion, amplifying neuroinflammatory cascades.

Ferroptosis involves iron-dependent lipid peroxidation and oxidative stress that generates damage-associated molecular patterns (DAMPs), including oxidized lipids and nucleic acids, which can activate the cGAS-STING pathway through recognition of cytosolic DNA and pattern recognition.

Ferroptosis triggers release of damage-associated molecular patterns (DAMPs) and oxidized lipids that activate the cGAS-STING pathway through pattern recognition, leading to STING activation and downstream interferon responses.

Ferroptosis, an iron-dependent form of cell death, can be triggered or exacerbated by α-synuclein accumulation and aggregation in dopaminergic neurons. Conversely, ferroptotic cell death in substantia nigra neurons can lead to increased α-synuclein release and propagation as a secondary consequence of neuronal damage.

Ferroptosis, an iron-dependent form of regulated cell death, can promote tau hyperphosphorylation and aggregation into neurofibrillary tangles through oxidative stress and impaired protein quality control mechanisms. Iron accumulation and lipid peroxidation characteristic of ferroptosis can destabilize microtubule-associated proteins and enhance tau pathology.

Ferroptosis, an iron-dependent form of cell death characterized by lipid peroxidation, can trigger or exacerbate tau pathology through oxidative stress and neuroinflammation, which promote tau hyperphosphorylation and aggregation in vulnerable neurons.

Ferroptosis inhibitors are small molecules or compounds specifically designed to block ferroptotic cell death pathways by inhibiting iron-dependent lipid peroxidation and associated executioner mechanisms. By definition and mechanism of action, ferroptosis inhibitors directly suppress ferroptosis processes.

Ferroptosis is an iron-dependent form of cell death driven by lipid peroxidation. Deferiprone, an iron chelator, directly reduces intracellular iron availability, thereby inhibiting the ferroptotic pathway by limiting the Fenton chemistry required for ferroptosis propagation.

Ferroptosis is an iron-dependent form of cell death characterized by iron accumulation and lipid peroxidation. The execution of ferroptosis directly results in iron redistribution within dying cells and their microenvironment as iron-containing proteins and organelles are disrupted.

Iron is an essential cofactor for cytochrome c oxidase and other electron transport chain complexes; functional iron deficiency impairs oxidative phosphorylation and ATP production, leading to mitochondrial dysfunction and structural damage. Iron deficiency also reduces expression of iron-sulfur cluster proteins required for mitochondrial integrity and function.

Iron is a critical cofactor for ATP synthase and electron transport chain function in mitochondria. Functional iron deficiency impairs oxidative phosphorylation, leading to ATP depletion and failure of ATP-dependent Ca2+ extrusion pumps (SERCA and Na+/Ca2+ exchangers), directly resulting in mitochondrial Ca2+ accumulation.

Functional iron deficiency impairs the synthesis and function of iron-sulfur cluster-containing proteins essential for electron transport chain complexes (I, II, III), leading to increased electron leak and ROS generation. Additionally, iron deficiency reduces ferritin and transferrin-mediated iron homeostasis, compromising iron availability for catalase and other antioxidant enzymes, exacerbating mitochondrial oxidative stress.

Functional iron deficiency impairs mitochondrial cytochrome c oxidase activity and reduces ATP production, leading to decreased ATP-dependent mPTP closure and increased mPTP opening. Iron is essential for Complex IV function; its deficiency compromises oxidative phosphorylation and bioenergetics, promoting mitochondrial permeability transition.

Functional iron deficiency impairs mitochondrial electron transport chain function and ATP production, leading to energy failure and activation of apoptotic pathways in neurons. Iron deficiency also increases oxidative stress through compromised antioxidant enzyme synthesis, directly triggering cell death mechanisms.

Functional iron deficiency represents inadequate bioavailable iron despite normal or elevated total iron stores, which directly reduces the iron available for incorporation into brain iron pools and iron-dependent proteins. This physiological state mechanically decreases total brain iron accumulation since iron cannot be efficiently transported across the blood-brain barrier or utilized by brain cells when systemic iron availability is compromised.

Iron is a critical cofactor for hepcidin regulation and ferroportin stability. Functional iron deficiency triggers compensatory upregulation of iron acquisition pathways, and evidence suggests iron-dependent regulation of BACE1 expression through iron-responsive elements and iron-sensing transcription factors that modulate amyloidogenic proteolysis.

Iron deficiency impairs mitochondrial function and increases cellular reactive oxygen species (ROS), which activates p38 MAPK signaling through oxidative stress-dependent mechanisms. p38 MAPK activation is a canonical response to cellular iron depletion and oxidative stress in neuronal cells.

Iron is a critical cofactor for ribonucleotide reductase and DNA synthesis; functional iron deficiency impairs GSK-3β regulation and promotes tau kinase activity, while iron deficiency also reduces ferroxidase activity of ceruloplasmin, leading to oxidative stress that hyperphosphorylates tau through kinase activation.

Iron is an essential cofactor for cytochrome c oxidase and other components of the electron transport chain; functional iron deficiency impairs mitochondrial ATP production and energy metabolism, leading to dysregulation of SERCA pumps and Na+/Ca2+ exchangers, which causes increased mitochondrial Ca2+ uptake and accumulation.

Iron is a critical cofactor for calcineurin, the phosphatase that dephosphorylates and activates NFAT for nuclear translocation. Functional iron deficiency impairs calcineurin activity, thereby reducing NFAT dephosphorylation and nuclear accumulation.

Iron is a critical cofactor for cytochrome c oxidase (Complex IV) and other components of the electron transport chain. Functional iron deficiency impairs mitochondrial respiration and ATP synthesis by reducing the availability of iron for heme and iron-sulfur cluster biosynthesis in these complexes.

Ferroptosis is an iron-dependent form of programmed cell death characterized by lipid peroxidation and mitochondrial dysfunction. When cells become ferroptosis-vulnerable, they undergo this specific death pathway, directly resulting in cell death through ferroptotic mechanisms.

Glutathione (GSH) is a critical antioxidant that protects cells from ferroptotic cell death by maintaining redox balance and supporting glutathione peroxidase 4 (GPX4) function, which prevents lipid peroxidation. Depletion or reduction of glutathione directly increases ferroptosis vulnerability by removing this essential ferroptosis-suppressive mechanism.

Ferroptosis-vulnerable cells exhibit dysregulated iron homeostasis, and the labile iron pool (free, redox-active iron) is both a consequence of ferroptosis vulnerability and a driver of ferroptotic cell death through Fenton chemistry-mediated lipid peroxidation. Increased labile iron pool exacerbates ferroptosis vulnerability, while ferroptotic processes alter cellular iron compartmentalization.

Ferroptosis-vulnerable cells exhibit dysregulated iron homeostasis, and the labile iron pool (free, redox-active iron) is both a consequence of ferroptosis vulnerability and a driver of ferroptotic cell death through Fenton chemistry-mediated lipid peroxidation. Increased labile iron pool exacerbates ferroptosis vulnerability, while ferroptotic processes alter cellular iron compartmentalization.

Ferroptosis is an iron-dependent form of cell death characterized by lipid peroxidation. Cells undergoing ferroptosis accumulate lipid reactive oxygen species (ROS) and lipid peroxides as a direct consequence of uncontrolled lipid oxidation driven by iron-catalyzed Fenton chemistry.

Ferroptosis vulnerability is directly regulated by lysosomal iron levels, as ferroptosis is an iron-dependent cell death pathway initiated by iron-catalyzed lipid peroxidation via the Fenton reaction. Conversely, cells undergoing ferroptosis exhibit dysregulated lysosomal iron homeostasis and release of labile iron from lysosomes, creating a feedback loop.

Ferroptosis vulnerability is directly regulated by lysosomal iron levels, as ferroptosis is an iron-dependent cell death pathway initiated by iron-catalyzed lipid peroxidation via the Fenton reaction. Conversely, cells undergoing ferroptosis exhibit dysregulated lysosomal iron homeostasis and release of labile iron from lysosomes, creating a feedback loop.

Oxidative stress induces ferroptotic cell death through iron-dependent lipid peroxidation, making cells vulnerable to ferroptosis. Ferroptosis itself is fundamentally driven by excessive oxidative stress that overwhelms antioxidant defenses, particularly through depletion of glutathione and inhibition of glutathione peroxidase 4 (GPx4).

Iron accumulation increases ferroptosis vulnerability in neurons through Fenton chemistry-driven ROS generation and lipid peroxidation, while ferroptotic cell death releases intracellular iron stores, increasing total brain iron levels. This creates a pathological feedback loop relevant to neurodegeneration.

Iron accumulation increases ferroptosis vulnerability in neurons through Fenton chemistry-driven ROS generation and lipid peroxidation, while ferroptotic cell death releases intracellular iron stores, increasing total brain iron levels. This creates a pathological feedback loop relevant to neurodegeneration.

Ferroptosis-vulnerable cells exhibit dysregulated iron metabolism and impaired antioxidant defenses, leading to increased mitochondrial ROS generation. Conversely, elevated mitochondrial ROS promotes ferroptotic pathway activation by overwhelming cellular antioxidant systems and driving lipid peroxidation.

Ferroptosis-vulnerable cells exhibit dysregulated iron metabolism and impaired antioxidant defenses, leading to increased mitochondrial ROS generation. Conversely, elevated mitochondrial ROS promotes ferroptotic pathway activation by overwhelming cellular antioxidant systems and driving lipid peroxidation.

Ferroptosis, an iron-dependent form of cell death characterized by lipid peroxidation and mitochondrial dysfunction, triggers lysosomal membrane permeabilization and damage through accumulation of reactive oxygen species and iron-catalyzed Fenton reactions within lysosomal compartments. Ferroptotic cells exhibit compromised lysosomal integrity as a consequence of oxidative stress that exceeds lysosomal antioxidant capacity.

Ferroptosis-vulnerable cells undergoing ferroptotic death release iron and lipid peroxides that damage lysosomal membranes, causing lysosomal dysfunction. Conversely, lysosomal dysfunction impairs autophagy and iron homeostasis, increasing ferroptosis vulnerability in neurons.

Ferroptosis-vulnerable cells undergoing ferroptotic death release iron and lipid peroxides that damage lysosomal membranes, causing lysosomal dysfunction. Conversely, lysosomal dysfunction impairs autophagy and iron homeostasis, increasing ferroptosis vulnerability in neurons.

Ferroptosis-vulnerable cells undergoing ferroptotic stress trigger lysosomal membrane permeabilization (LMP), leading to direct release of sequestered iron from lysosomes into the cytoplasm. This iron release amplifies Fenton chemistry and propagates ferroptotic cell death.

Ferroptosis-vulnerable cells experience iron accumulation and lipid peroxidation that can disrupt lysosomal membrane integrity and alter proton gradient maintenance, directly affecting lysosomal pH. Conversely, lysosomal pH dysregulation impairs iron homeostasis and autophagy, rendering cells more susceptible to ferroptotic death through compromised ferritophagy and ferroptosis defense mechanisms.

Ferroptosis-vulnerable cells experience iron accumulation and lipid peroxidation that can disrupt lysosomal membrane integrity and alter proton gradient maintenance, directly affecting lysosomal pH. Conversely, lysosomal pH dysregulation impairs iron homeostasis and autophagy, rendering cells more susceptible to ferroptotic death through compromised ferritophagy and ferroptosis defense mechanisms.

Ferroptosis is an iron-dependent form of cell death characterized by lipid peroxidation and mitochondrial dysfunction. LMP (lysosomal membrane permeability) increase is a downstream consequence of ferroptotic stress, as ferroptosis-induced oxidative stress and lipid peroxidation directly compromise lysosomal membrane integrity, leading to increased LMP and release of lysosomal contents.

Nrf2 is a master transcription factor that activates antioxidant response elements (AREs) and upregulates expression of ferroptosis-protective proteins including ferritin, GPX4, and SLC7A11. Reduced Nrf2 activity impairs the antioxidant defense system, increasing cellular vulnerability to ferroptosis through decreased expression of ferroptosis-suppressing factors.

Ferroptotic cell death in neurons and glia triggers release of damage-associated molecular patterns (DAMPs) and inflammatory mediators that activate senescent cell programs, leading to increased secretion of SASP factors (IL-6, TNF-α, MCP-1, etc.). These ferroptosis-induced SASP factors propagate neuroinflammation and neurodegeneration in Alzheimer's disease pathology.

Ferroptosis-vulnerable cells undergoing ferroptotic death release damage-associated molecular patterns (DAMPs) and cellular debris that trigger senescence in neighboring cells through p38/p53 pathways and inflammatory signaling. Additionally, ferroptosis-induced lipid peroxidation generates oxidative stress that promotes senescence phenotypes in surviving cells in the microenvironment.

Ferroptosis-vulnerable cells experience iron accumulation and dysregulation as part of their ferroptotic pathway, which can trigger or exacerbate senescence programs. Cells undergoing ferroptosis display iron-dependent oxidative stress that activates senescence checkpoints, leading to elevated iron content in senescent cells as a consequence of ferroptotic stress.

Ferroptosis is an iron-dependent form of cell death characterized by iron-catalyzed lipid peroxidation. Cells undergoing ferroptosis experience disruption of iron homeostasis, leading to increased intracellular iron release and accumulation that drives the ferroptotic cascade through Fenton chemistry.

Ferroptosis-vulnerable cells exhibit dysregulated iron homeostasis, and ferritin (iron storage protein) levels are both a consequence of ferroptotic stress and a modulator of ferroptosis susceptibility. Ferroptosis involves iron-dependent lipid peroxidation, and ferritin iron availability directly influences the rate and severity of ferroptotic cell death.

Ferroptosis-vulnerable cells exhibit dysregulated iron homeostasis, and ferritin (iron storage protein) levels are both a consequence of ferroptotic stress and a modulator of ferroptosis susceptibility. Ferroptosis involves iron-dependent lipid peroxidation, and ferritin iron availability directly influences the rate and severity of ferroptotic cell death.

Iron-sulfur clusters are essential cofactors for mitochondrial electron transport chain complexes and aconitase. Their disruption or depletion impairs cellular iron homeostasis and redox balance, increasing vulnerability to ferroptosis by compromising antioxidant defense systems and iron-dependent protein function.

Ferroptosis is an iron-dependent form of cell death characterized by lipid peroxidation and membrane disruption, which directly triggers phosphatidylserine (PS) externalization to the outer membrane leaflet as an early apoptotic/death signal. PS exposure occurs as a consequence of the ferroptotic cell death process itself.

Ferroptosis vulnerability in neurons can be exacerbated by tau pathology (NFTs), while tau aggregation itself can increase cellular iron accumulation and ferroptotic stress through disruption of iron homeostasis and mitochondrial dysfunction. This creates a feedforward pathological loop where each process amplifies the other.

Ferroptosis vulnerability in neurons can be exacerbated by tau pathology (NFTs), while tau aggregation itself can increase cellular iron accumulation and ferroptotic stress through disruption of iron homeostasis and mitochondrial dysfunction. Ferroptosis-vulnerable neurons undergo iron-dependent lipid peroxidation and oxidative stress, which activates kinases (particularly GSK3β and CDK5) that directly phosphorylate tau at multiple sites. Ferroptotic cell death and associated ROS generation promote the kinase activity and phosphatase inhibition that drive tau hyperphosphorylation.

Ferroptosis-vulnerable neurons experiencing iron-dependent lipid peroxidation can undergo cellular stress that exacerbates Aβ aggregation through impaired protein quality control and lysosomal dysfunction. Conversely, Aβ aggregates promote ferroptosis vulnerability by disrupting mitochondrial function, depleting cellular antioxidants, and triggering iron accumulation.

Ferroptosis-vulnerable neurons experiencing iron-dependent lipid peroxidation can undergo cellular stress that exacerbates Aβ aggregation through impaired protein quality control and lysosomal dysfunction. Conversely, Aβ aggregates promote ferroptosis vulnerability by disrupting mitochondrial function, depleting cellular antioxidants, and triggering iron accumulation.

Ferroptosis-vulnerable neurons undergo iron-dependent lipid peroxidation, generating reactive oxygen species and oxidative stress that can trigger or accelerate tau hyperphosphorylation and aggregation. Ferroptotic cell death and the resulting neuroinflammation create a pathogenic microenvironment that promotes tau pathology progression.

Ferroptosis vulnerability in neurons can amplify tau pathology through iron-dependent ROS generation and lipid peroxidation, which promotes tau phosphorylation and paired helical filament (PHF) formation. Iron accumulation in vulnerable neurons exacerbates oxidative stress that drives tau pathological transformation.

Calcineurin activation promotes ferroptosis vulnerability by dephosphorylating and inactivating GPX4 and other ferroptosis-regulatory proteins, while also modulating iron homeostasis and increasing cellular ROS levels that sensitize neurons to ferroptotic cell death.

Calpain activation increases ferroptosis vulnerability by cleaving ferroptosis-regulatory proteins (such as GPX4 interacting partners and iron metabolism enzymes) and disrupting cellular iron homeostasis, thereby promoting ferroptotic cell death pathways.

CaMKII regulates calcium homeostasis and NMDAR signaling, which are critical modulators of ferroptosis sensitivity in neurons. Dysregulated CaMKII activity can alter intracellular calcium levels and redox balance, making neurons more vulnerable to ferroptotic cell death.

Ferroptosis is an iron-dependent form of cell death characterized by lipid peroxidation and mitochondrial dysfunction, which triggers calcium dysregulation and cytosolic Ca²⁺ accumulation through multiple mechanisms including mitochondrial calcium release and activation of calcium-permeable channels in response to oxidative stress.

Ferroptosis-vulnerable cells exhibit dysregulated mitochondrial calcium handling, where ferroptotic stress impairs mitochondrial calcium uptake capacity through oxidative damage to calcium channels and the NCLX antiporter, while excessive mitochondrial Ca²⁺ accumulation can trigger ferroptotic pathways through ROS generation and lipid peroxidation. This represents a bidirectional relationship where ferroptosis vulnerability both drives and is driven by aberrant mitochondrial calcium dynamics.

Ferroptosis-vulnerable cells exhibit dysregulated mitochondrial calcium handling, where ferroptotic stress impairs mitochondrial calcium uptake capacity through oxidative damage to calcium channels and the NCLX antiporter, while excessive mitochondrial Ca²⁺ accumulation can trigger ferroptotic pathways through ROS generation and lipid peroxidation. This represents a bidirectional relationship where ferroptosis vulnerability both drives and is driven by aberrant mitochondrial calcium dynamics.

Ferroptosis-vulnerable cells experience iron-dependent lipid peroxidation that disrupts mitochondrial membrane integrity, leading to Ca²⁺ dysregulation and mitochondrial Ca²⁺ overload. Conversely, mitochondrial Ca²⁺ overload impairs mitochondrial function and increases ROS production, sensitizing cells to ferroptotic death through ferroptosis-regulating pathways (GPX4 inhibition, System xc⁻ dysfunction).

Ferroptosis-vulnerable cells experience iron-dependent lipid peroxidation that disrupts mitochondrial membrane integrity, leading to Ca²⁺ dysregulation and mitochondrial Ca²⁺ overload. Conversely, mitochondrial Ca²⁺ overload impairs mitochondrial function and increases ROS production, sensitizing cells to ferroptotic death through ferroptosis-regulating pathways (GPX4 inhibition, System xc⁻ dysfunction).

Ferroptosis-vulnerable cells experience iron-dependent lipid peroxidation and oxidative stress, which disrupts mitochondrial calcium handling and ATP production, leading to mitochondrial Ca2+ overload. Conversely, mitochondrial Ca2+ overload impairs oxidative phosphorylation and promotes ROS generation, sensitizing cells to ferroptotic pathways.

Ferroptosis-vulnerable cells experience iron-dependent lipid peroxidation and oxidative stress, which disrupts mitochondrial calcium handling and ATP production, leading to mitochondrial Ca2+ overload. Conversely, mitochondrial Ca2+ overload impairs oxidative phosphorylation and promotes ROS generation, sensitizing cells to ferroptotic pathways.

Ferroptosis-vulnerable cells undergoing ferroptotic death exhibit dysregulated calcium and iron homeostasis, which directly triggers mitochondrial permeability transition pore (mPTP) opening as a downstream consequence of ferroptotic cascade activation.
