Increased MAM (mitochondria-associated ER membrane) distance disrupts calcium transfer from ER to mitochondria, impairing mitochondrial calcium buffering capacity and leading to increased mitochondrial ROS production through impaired oxidative phosphorylation efficiency and reduced antioxidant enzyme activity.

Increased MAM (mitochondria-associated membrane) distance disrupts the physical coupling between mitochondria and endoplasmic reticulum, reducing calcium transfer efficiency and leading to mitochondrial calcium overload, which directly triggers mPTP (mitochondrial permeability transition pore) opening.

Increased mitochondrial-associated endoplasmic reticulum (ER) membrane distance reduces the efficiency of calcium transfer from ER to mitochondria through IP3R-VDAC coupling, leading to ER calcium stress that triggers compensatory VDAC oligomerization and formation of megachannels. Reduced MAM proximity impairs mitochondrial calcium buffering capacity, promoting VDAC oligomerization as part of the apoptotic or stress response pathway.

IP3R (inositol 1,4,5-trisphosphate receptor) mediates Ca2+ release from the endoplasmic reticulum, which can modulate mitochondrial calcium uptake through MCU (mitochondrial calcium uniporter) in the inner mitochondrial membrane. IP3R-dependent Ca2+ signaling regulates MCU-mediated mitochondrial Ca2+ accumulation through direct cytoplasmic Ca2+ elevation.

IP3R (inositol 1,4,5-trisphosphate receptor) is a calcium channel located on the endoplasmic reticulum membrane that directly transports Ca²⁺ ions from the ER lumen into the cytoplasm. This cytoplasmic Ca²⁺ is then taken up by mitochondria through the mitochondrial calcium uniporter (MCU), thereby increasing mitochondrial Ca²⁺ levels.

IP3R (inositol 1,4,5-trisphosphate receptor) is a calcium channel located on the endoplasmic reticulum membrane that directly releases Ca²⁺ into the cytoplasm upon IP3 binding. This cytoplasmic Ca²⁺ is subsequently taken up by mitochondria through the mitochondrial calcium uniporter, causing mitochondrial Ca²⁺ overload, particularly when IP3R signaling is dysregulated.
IP3R activation in the endoplasmic reticulum releases Ca2+ into the cytoplasm, which directly activates calcineurin (a Ca2+/calmodulin-dependent phosphatase) through calmodulin binding.

IP3R activation on the endoplasmic reticulum releases intracellular calcium stores, and the resulting calcium influx directly activates calpain proteases, which are calcium-dependent cysteine proteases. This IP3R-mediated calcium release is a primary mechanism for calpain activation in neuronal cells.

IP3R and SERCA are functionally coupled components of intracellular calcium signaling; IP3R releases calcium from the ER lumen while SERCA pumps calcium back into the ER, creating a reciprocal regulatory relationship where their activities directly influence each other's function and expression.

IP3R and SERCA are functionally coupled components of intracellular calcium signaling; IP3R releases calcium from the ER lumen while SERCA pumps calcium back into the ER, creating a reciprocal regulatory relationship where their activities directly influence each other's function and expression.

IP3R (Inositol 1,4,5-trisphosphate receptor) is a calcium channel embedded in the endoplasmic reticulum membrane that directly participates in forming and maintaining mitochondria-associated ER membranes (MAM contact sites) through IP3R-VAPB-PTPIP51 tethering complexes. IP3R activity and localization regulate the stability and function of MAM structures by controlling ER-mitochondrial calcium transfer at these contact sites.

IP3R activation on the endoplasmic reticulum releases Ca2+ into the cytoplasm, which is subsequently taken up by mitochondria via the MCU (mitochondrial calcium uniporter). Excessive mitochondrial Ca2+ overload uncouples oxidative phosphorylation and increases electron leak from the electron transport chain, directly amplifying mitochondrial ROS production.

IP3R (inositol 1,4,5-trisphosphate receptor) mediates calcium release from intracellular stores, and excessive IP3R-dependent calcium mobilization leads to mitochondrial calcium overload, which directly triggers mPTP (mitochondrial permeability transition pore) opening through calcium-dependent mechanisms.

IP3R activation increases intracellular calcium release, which promotes mitochondrial calcium uptake through VDAC and drives oligomerization of VDAC monomers into pathological oligomeric pores. This IP3R-mediated calcium signaling pathway is a well-established driver of VDAC oligomerization and mitochondrial dysfunction in neurodegeneration.

IP3R (inositol 1,4,5-trisphosphate receptor) and GRP75 (glucose-regulated protein 75, also known as mortalin/HSP75) form a direct physical complex at the mitochondrial-associated ER membrane (MAM), where GRP75 acts as a chaperone protein that regulates IP3R-mediated Ca2+ transfer to mitochondria. This interaction is critical for ER-mitochondrial calcium signaling and has been implicated in Alzheimer's disease pathology through dysregulation of calcium homeostasis.

IP3R (inositol 1,4,5-trisphosphate receptor) and GRP75 (glucose-regulated protein 75, also known as mortalin/HSP75) form a direct physical complex at the mitochondrial-associated ER membrane (MAM), where GRP75 acts as a chaperone protein that regulates IP3R-mediated Ca2+ transfer to mitochondria. This interaction is critical for ER-mitochondrial calcium signaling and has been implicated in Alzheimer's disease pathology through dysregulation of calcium homeostasis.

IP3R-mediated calcium dysregulation activates calcium-dependent kinases (particularly GSK-3β and CDK5) that phosphorylate tau at multiple sites, leading to tau hyperphosphorylation and aggregation. Excessive IP3R signaling disrupts intracellular calcium homeostasis, promoting pathological tau modifications.

GRP75 is a mitochondrial chaperone protein that directly binds to and stabilizes MAM (mitochondria-associated ER membrane) contact sites through interaction with VAPB on the ER and TOM70 on mitochondria. This binding maintains the structural integrity and functional stability of MAM complexes.

GRP75 is a mitochondrial chaperone that directly facilitates tethering between mitochondria and endoplasmic reticulum through its interaction with VAPB and PTPIP51, thereby promoting MAM (mitochondria-associated ER membrane) formation and hyperconnectivity.

GRP75 (a mitochondrial chaperone) physically interacts with and stabilizes MCU (mitochondrial calcium uniporter) at the inner mitochondrial membrane, facilitating calcium uptake into mitochondria. This direct protein-protein interaction increases MCU function and calcium transport capacity.

GRP75 (heat shock protein 75) functions as a mitochondrial chaperone that regulates calcium homeostasis by modulating the interaction between IP3R and VDAC at mitochondrial membranes, thereby controlling mitochondrial calcium uptake and sequestration.

GRP75 (mtHsp70) functions as a key component of the mitochondrial calcium uptake machinery, particularly through its role in the MICU1-containing complex that regulates MCU activity. GRP75 modulates mitochondrial Ca²⁺ uptake capacity, and dysregulation of GRP75 function can lead to impaired calcium homeostasis and mitochondrial Ca²⁺ overload.

GRP75 (a mitochondrial chaperone heat shock protein) directly binds to ryanodine receptors (RYR) on the endoplasmic reticulum and mitochondrial membranes, regulating calcium channel function and calcium flux. This interaction is well-characterized in cellular stress responses and mitochondrial-ER coupling.

GRP75 (a mitochondrial chaperone heat shock protein) directly binds to ryanodine receptors (RYR) on the endoplasmic reticulum and mitochondrial membranes, regulating calcium channel function and calcium flux. This interaction is well-characterized in cellular stress responses and mitochondrial-ER coupling.

GRP75 (also known as mortalin/HSP60) is a mitochondrial chaperone protein that physically binds to and regulates SERCA (sarco/endoplasmic reticulum Ca2+-ATPase) function in the context of calcium homeostasis and ER-mitochondrial interactions. This binding relationship is part of the molecular machinery regulating Ca2+ signaling dysregulation implicated in Alzheimer's disease pathogenesis.

GRP75 (also known as mortalin/HSP60) is a mitochondrial chaperone protein that physically binds to and regulates SERCA (sarco/endoplasmic reticulum Ca2+-ATPase) function in the context of calcium homeostasis and ER-mitochondrial interactions. This binding relationship is part of the molecular machinery regulating Ca2+ signaling dysregulation implicated in Alzheimer's disease pathogenesis.

GRP75 (mitochondrial heat shock protein) physically interacts with Sigma1R at mitochondrial-associated endoplasmic reticulum membranes (MAMs), forming a functional complex that regulates calcium signaling and mitochondrial homeostasis. This interaction is critical for neuroprotection under stress conditions relevant to neurodegeneration.

GRP75 (mitochondrial heat shock protein) physically interacts with Sigma1R at mitochondrial-associated endoplasmic reticulum membranes (MAMs), forming a functional complex that regulates calcium signaling and mitochondrial homeostasis. This interaction is critical for neuroprotection under stress conditions relevant to neurodegeneration.

GRP75 (also known as mortalin/HSP60) is a mitochondrial chaperone protein that functions in maintaining mitochondrial integrity and ATP synthesis. By promoting proper mitochondrial protein folding and maintaining mitochondrial membrane potential, GRP75 reduces electron transport chain dysfunction that would otherwise generate excessive ROS.

GRP75 is a mitochondrial chaperone protein that functions to prevent calcium overload in the endoplasmic reticulum by facilitating proper calcium handling at mitochondrial-ER contact sites and supporting mitochondrial calcium uptake capacity. Loss or dysfunction of GRP75 impairs this calcium buffering function, leading to ER calcium accumulation and overload.

GRP75 (glucose-regulated protein 75, also known as mortalin or HSP60) is a mitochondrial chaperone that directly binds to presenilin-1 (PS1) at mitochondria-associated ER membranes (MAMs), facilitating the organization and function of this critical signaling platform. This interaction is essential for maintaining MAM integrity and regulating calcium homeostasis between the ER and mitochondria, processes implicated in Alzheimer's disease pathogenesis.

GRP75 (a mitochondrial chaperone protein) directly binds to presenilin-2 (PS2) at mitochondria-associated membranes (MAMs), facilitating the anchoring of PS2 to these contact sites between mitochondria and endoplasmic reticulum.

MAM (mitochondria-associated membrane) contact sites facilitate direct calcium transfer from the endoplasmic reticulum to mitochondria through IP3R-VDAC coupling, which can lead to mitochondrial calcium overload when excessive calcium is transferred across these tethering sites.

MAM (mitochondria-associated ER membranes) contact sites facilitate calcium transfer from the ER to mitochondria; excessive mitochondrial calcium uptake at these sites drives oxidative phosphorylation and increases electron transport chain activity, leading to elevated ROS production.

MAM (mitochondria-associated ER membranes) contact sites regulate calcium transfer between the ER and mitochondria; excessive calcium uptake through these sites drives mitochondrial calcium overload, which directly triggers mPTP (mitochondrial permeability transition pore) opening as a consequence of elevated matrix calcium and ROS production.

MAM contact sites (mitochondria-associated ER membranes) are direct physical tethering sites where VDAC oligomers in the outer mitochondrial membrane interact with IP3 receptors on the ER. Dysregulation of MAM contacts can promote the formation and stabilization of VDAC oligomeric complexes, which are pathologically associated with excessive Ca2+ transfer and neuronal dysfunction in Alzheimer's disease.

MAM (mitochondria-associated ER membranes) contact sites facilitate calcium transfer from the ER to mitochondria, which directly regulates MCU (mitochondrial calcium uniporter) activity and calcium uptake into the mitochondrial matrix. The physical proximity and tethering at MAM sites enables calcium-dependent activation and modulation of MCU function.

MAM (Mitochondria-Associated ER Membranes) contact sites facilitate direct transfer of Ca²⁺ from the endoplasmic reticulum to mitochondria through tethering proteins (VAPB, PTPIP51, Mfn2) and IP3 receptors positioned at the membrane interface, enabling efficient mitochondrial calcium uptake.

MAM (mitochondria-associated ER membrane) contact sites facilitate direct transfer of Ca²⁺ from the ER to mitochondria through IP3R and VDAC channels, enabling mitochondrial Ca²⁺ uptake that can lead to overload when excessive.

MAM (mitochondria-associated membrane) contact sites are specialized membrane junctions where presenilin-1 (PS1) localizes and functions. PS1 is an integral component of MAM structure and function, with PS1 localization at MAM contact sites being essential for proper calcium handling and mitochondrial-ER communication in the context of Alzheimer's disease pathology.

MAM (mitochondria-associated ER membrane) contact sites are dynamic protein complexes where PS2 (presenilin-2) localizes and functions as a critical structural component. PS2 at MAM participates in calcium signaling and lipid metabolism regulation between the ER and mitochondria.

MAM contact sites facilitate calcium transfer between the ER and mitochondria, and SERCA (sarco/endoplasmic reticulum calcium ATPase) is the primary ER calcium pump that maintains ER calcium stores and influences the calcium gradient that drives MAM-mediated calcium flux. Disruption of MAM contacts can impair SERCA function by reducing local calcium dynamics, while altered SERCA activity affects ER calcium homeostasis and the efficiency of MAM-mediated calcium signaling.

MAM contact sites facilitate calcium transfer between the ER and mitochondria, and SERCA (sarco/endoplasmic reticulum calcium ATPase) is the primary ER calcium pump that maintains ER calcium stores and influences the calcium gradient that drives MAM-mediated calcium flux. Disruption of MAM contacts can impair SERCA function by reducing local calcium dynamics, while altered SERCA activity affects ER calcium homeostasis and the efficiency of MAM-mediated calcium signaling.

MAM (mitochondria-associated ER membranes) contact sites regulate calcium homeostasis and lipid metabolism, which are critical for Sigma-1 receptor (Sigma1R) localization and function at these membrane microdomains. Sigma1R acts as a chaperone protein enriched at MAM sites where it regulates ER-mitochondrial interactions and calcium signaling.

MAM contact sites facilitate direct transfer of lipids and calcium between mitochondria and ER, which localizes APP and γ-secretase to these membrane microdomains, thereby increasing the probability of amyloidogenic cleavage events that generate Aβ. Enhanced ER-mitochondrial contacts increase local APP concentration and optimize conditions for PSEN1/γ-secretase-mediated amyloidogenic processing.

MAM (mitochondria-associated ER membrane) contact sites facilitate calcium transfer from ER to mitochondria and enable direct transfer of lipids and proteins between organelles. Dysregulation of MAM contacts impairs calcium homeostasis and ER-mitochondrial communication, which directly increases β-secretase (BACE1) activity and amyloidogenic APP processing, thereby increasing Aβ production.

MAM (mitochondria-associated ER membrane) contact sites regulate calcium transfer between ER and mitochondria; dysregulation of MAM function alters intracellular calcium homeostasis, which directly activates calcineurin, a calcium/calmodulin-dependent phosphatase. In AD pathology, impaired MAM integrity reduces calcium buffering capacity and calcium signaling precision, leading to aberrant calcineurin activation.

MAM (mitochondria-associated ER membrane) contact sites facilitate calcium transfer from the ER to mitochondria, which is essential for maintaining cytoplasmic Ca2+ oscillations that activate CaMKII through calmodulin binding. Enhanced MAM functionality directly increases local calcium availability at sites where CaMKII resides, thereby increasing CaMKII activation.

MAM (mitochondria-associated ER membrane) contact sites facilitate calcium transfer and lipid exchange between the ER and mitochondria, which directly influences lysosomal membrane permeabilization (LMP) through calcium-dependent lysosomal destabilization and altered mitochondrial function. Disrupted MAM contacts can impair calcium homeostasis and mitochondrial ATP production, both critical regulators of lysosomal integrity and acid hydrolase function.

MAM (mitochondria-associated ER membranes) contact sites regulate calcium transfer between ER and mitochondria; disruption of MAM integrity impairs mitochondrial calcium buffering capacity and increases mitochondrial dysfunction, leading to lysosomal damage through impaired autophagy flux and calcium dysregulation.

MAM contact sites facilitate calcium transfer from ER to mitochondria, which is critical for mitochondrial function and autophagy-lysosome pathway regulation. Disruption of MAM contacts impairs calcium signaling, reduces mitochondrial ATP production, and compromises lysosomal acidification and proteolytic capacity, directly leading to lysosomal dysfunction.

MAM (mitochondria-associated ER membrane) contact sites facilitate calcium transfer from ER to mitochondria; disrupted calcium homeostasis at these sites can lead to mitochondrial dysfunction and release of danger signals (ROS, mtDNA, cardiolipin) that directly activate NLRP3 inflammasome assembly through pattern recognition.

MAM (mitochondria-associated ER membrane) contact sites facilitate the transfer of lipids and calcium between the ER and mitochondria, and disrupted MAM integrity can impair mitochondrial quality control mechanisms, leading to increased cytosolic mtDNA release from damaged mitochondria through increased outer membrane permeabilization.

MAM (mitochondria-associated ER membrane) contact sites facilitate calcium transfer from ER to mitochondria, leading to mitochondrial calcium overload and oxidative stress that damages mitochondrial DNA through ROS generation. This direct spatial coupling increases mtDNA oxidative damage.

MAM contact sites facilitate calcium transfer from ER to mitochondria; elevated mitochondrial calcium increases electron transport chain activity and ROS production, which drives Fenton reaction-mediated free radical generation from ferrous iron.

MAM (mitochondria-associated ER membrane) contact sites facilitate calcium transfer from the ER to mitochondria, which increases mitochondrial oxidative metabolism and reactive oxygen species (ROS) production. Elevated ROS directly promotes lipid peroxidation of membrane lipids, particularly polyunsaturated fatty acids in the inner mitochondrial membrane.

MAM contact sites facilitate calcium transfer from ER to mitochondria; excessive Ca2+ uptake by mitochondria impairs oxidative phosphorylation and increases ROS production, directly generating oxidative stress.

MCU (mitochondrial calcium uniporter) transports Ca2+ into mitochondria; excessive mitochondrial Ca2+ overload directly triggers opening of the mitochondrial permeability transition pore (mPTP), leading to loss of mitochondrial membrane potential and cell death.

MCU (Mitochondrial Calcium Uniporter) mediates Ca2+ influx into mitochondria; excessive mitochondrial Ca2+ overload directly activates oxidative phosphorylation and electron transport chain uncoupling, thereby increasing mitochondrial ROS production through increased electron leak.

MCU (Mitochondrial Calcium Uniporter) is the primary channel responsible for transporting Ca²⁺ from the cytoplasm into the mitochondrial matrix down its electrochemical gradient. This is a direct, well-established molecular transport function.

MCU (Mitochondrial Calcium Uniporter) is the primary channel responsible for Ca²⁺ uptake into the mitochondrial matrix. Increased MCU activity or expression directly facilitates Ca²⁺ influx across the inner mitochondrial membrane, leading to mitochondrial Ca²⁺ overload when uptake exceeds extrusion capacity.

MCU (Mitochondrial Calcium Uniporter) is the primary channel responsible for transporting Ca²⁺ from the cytosol into the mitochondrial matrix. By regulating the flux of calcium across the inner mitochondrial membrane, MCU directly controls cytosolic Ca²⁺ levels through competitive transport dynamics.

MCU (mitochondrial calcium uniporter) and RYR (ryanodine receptor) are both key regulators of intracellular calcium homeostasis. MCU mediates calcium uptake into mitochondria while RYR releases calcium from the sarcoplasmic/endoplasmic reticulum, and their coordinated activity directly influences cytoplasmic and mitochondrial calcium dynamics.

MCU (mitochondrial calcium uniporter) and RYR (ryanodine receptor) are both key regulators of intracellular calcium homeostasis. MCU mediates calcium uptake into mitochondria while RYR releases calcium from the sarcoplasmic/endoplasmic reticulum, and their coordinated activity directly influences cytoplasmic and mitochondrial calcium dynamics.

MCU (mitochondrial calcium uniporter) mediates Ca2+ influx into mitochondria; elevated mitochondrial Ca2+ directly activates calcineurin, a Ca2+/calmodulin-dependent phosphatase localized to mitochondria and other compartments.

MCU (mitochondrial calcium uniporter) mediates Ca2+ entry into mitochondria; increased mitochondrial calcium activates CaMKII through calcium-binding to its calmodulin domain, leading to CaMKII autophosphorylation and activation.

MCU (mitochondrial calcium uniporter) and Mfn2 (mitofusin-2) functionally interact in mitochondrial calcium handling and mitochondrial dynamics. MCU activity influences mitochondrial calcium uptake which affects mitochondrial function and morphology that Mfn2 regulates, while Mfn2-mediated mitochondrial fusion can influence the distribution and function of MCU complexes.

MCU (mitochondrial calcium uniporter) and Mfn2 (mitofusin-2) functionally interact in mitochondrial calcium handling and mitochondrial dynamics. MCU activity influences mitochondrial calcium uptake which affects mitochondrial function and morphology that Mfn2 regulates, while Mfn2-mediated mitochondrial fusion can influence the distribution and function of MCU complexes.

NFAT (Nuclear Factor of Activated T cells) is a transcription factor that can regulate the expression of IP3R (Inositol 1,4,5-trisphosphate receptor) genes. NFAT activation leads to translocation to the nucleus where it binds to promoter regions and enhances transcription of calcium signaling genes including IP3R subtypes.

NFAT (Nuclear Factor of Activated T cells) can translocate to the nucleus upon calcium signaling and regulate transcription of genes involved in calcium homeostasis, including RYR (Ryanodine Receptor) genes. RYRs are calcium release channels whose expression can be modulated by NFAT-dependent transcriptional regulation in response to calcium-dependent signaling cascades.

Nuclear NFAT (particularly NFAT1/NFATc2) acts as a transcription factor that can promote pro-apoptotic gene expression and neuroinflammatory pathways, including upregulation of death receptors and pro-inflammatory cytokines that drive neuronal cell death in neurodegenerative contexts.

NFAT (Nuclear Factor of Activated T cells) is a transcription factor that directly binds to NFAT binding sites in the IL1B promoter region, increasing IL1B gene transcription and production of the pro-inflammatory cytokine IL-1β.

NFAT (Nuclear Factor of Activated T cells) is a well-characterized transcription factor that directly binds to NFAT response elements in the promoter regions of pro-inflammatory cytokine genes, including IL18, to drive their transcriptional expression.

Nuclear NFAT transcription factors regulate the expression of pro-inflammatory genes including caspase-1, and NFAT activation leads to increased caspase-1 transcription and subsequent activation in microglial and immune cells contributing to neuroinflammation in AD.

Nuclear NFAT functions as a transcription factor that directly binds to DNA regulatory elements in the NLRP3 inflammasome gene promoter, transcriptionally upregulating NLRP3 expression and assembly into its active oligomeric form.

NFAT transcription factors regulate the expression of genes encoding upstream kinases and scaffolding proteins that directly activate p38 MAPK signaling cascades. Additionally, NFAT nuclear translocation can induce calcineurin-dependent pathways that converge on p38 MAPK phosphorylation and activation.

Nuclear NFAT (particularly NFAT1/NFATc2) acts as a transcription factor that directly binds to NFAT-binding sites in the promoter regions of genes encoding pro-inflammatory cytokines and chemokines, driving the transcriptional activation of A1 astrocyte-specific genes including IL-6, TNF-α, and complement components (C3, C1q). NFAT nuclear translocation, triggered by neuroinflammatory signals in AD pathology, serves as a master regulator of the A1 astrocyte polarization program.

NFAT nuclear translocation activates pro-inflammatory transcriptional programs that promote the conversion of resting microglia and astrocytes into reactive A1 phenotypes through calcineurin-NFAT signaling. NFAT acts as a direct transcriptional regulator of inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, TNF-α, IL-1β) and chemokines that drive A1 astrocyte differentiation.

NFAT nuclear translocation activates transcriptional programs that increase GSK3β activity and reduce phosphatase activity, leading to tau hyperphosphorylation. NFAT signaling also promotes inflammatory responses that indirectly enhance tau pathology through kinase activation.

Mitochondrial Ca²⁺ overload activates the electron transport chain and increases metabolic demand, leading to increased electron leak from complexes I and III that directly generates superoxide and other ROS species. Additionally, Ca²⁺ overload impairs the function of ROS-scavenging enzymes like Mn-SOD, further elevating mitochondrial ROS production.

Mitochondrial Ca²⁺ overload directly activates RYR (ryanodine receptor) channels through Ca²⁺-induced Ca²⁺ release (CICR) mechanisms, where elevated mitochondrial Ca²⁺ sensitizes RYR to further Ca²⁺ release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum, creating a pathological amplification loop that exacerbates neuronal excitotoxicity in Alzheimer's disease.

Mitochondrial Ca²⁺ overload activates cytosolic phospholipase A2 (cPLA2) through Ca²⁺-dependent mechanisms, as cPLA2 contains EF-hand motifs that bind Ca²⁺ and are required for its activation. This leads to increased phospholipid hydrolysis and release of arachidonic acid, contributing to neuroinflammation and neurodegeneration in Alzheimer's disease.

Mitochondrial Ca²⁺ overload activates phospholipase A2 and increases electron transport chain dysfunction, both of which directly promote lipid peroxidation through increased reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation and release of polyunsaturated fatty acids in mitochondrial membranes.

Mitochondrial Ca²⁺ overload triggers excessive ROS production and activates oxidative stress pathways that deplete glutathione through increased consumption by glutathione peroxidase and direct oxidation. Additionally, Ca²⁺ overload impairs mitochondrial function and ATP production, reducing the capacity for glutathione synthesis and regeneration.

Mitochondrial Ca²⁺ overload triggers ferroptosis through increased ROS production and lipid peroxidation, which directly inactivates GPX4 (glutathione peroxidase 4), a critical ferroptosis suppressor enzyme. GPX4 requires reduced glutathione (GSH) as a cofactor, and excessive mitochondrial Ca²⁺-driven oxidative stress depletes GSH and promotes GPX4 protein degradation or functional impairment through oxidative modification.

Mitochondrial Ca²⁺ overload triggers opening of the mitochondrial permeability transition pore (mPTP) and uncouples oxidative phosphorylation, directly reducing ATP synthesis capacity. Excessive Ca²⁺ influx into the mitochondrial matrix impairs the electron transport chain and ATP synthase function.

Mitochondrial Ca²⁺ overload triggers opening of the mitochondrial permeability transition pore (mPTP) and causes mitochondrial dysfunction, leading to release of cathepsins and other hydrolases that directly compromise lysosomal membrane integrity (LMP). Ca²⁺-dependent proteases activated during mitochondrial Ca²⁺ overload can directly degrade lysosomal membrane proteins, inducing membrane permeabilization.

Mitochondrial Ca²⁺ overload triggers ferroptosis through iron-dependent ROS generation and depletion of the ferroptosis suppressor GPX4. Excessive mitochondrial Ca²⁺ impairs oxidative phosphorylation, increases electron leakage in the ETC, and promotes iron accumulation in mitochondria, creating conditions that favor ferroptotic cell death.

Elevated mitochondrial Ca²⁺ promotes mitochondrial dysfunction and ROS production, which drives iron-catalyzed Fenton reactions that generate highly reactive hydroxyl radicals. This represents a direct mechanistic link where mitochondrial Ca²⁺ overload amplifies oxidative stress through the Fenton pathway.

Excessive mitochondrial Ca²⁺ overload disrupts iron homeostasis and increases ROS production, which drives ferroptotic cell death through lipid peroxidation and iron-dependent mechanisms. Mitochondrial Ca²⁺-induced energy depletion and MPTP opening further sensitize cells to ferroptosis pathways.

Mitochondrial Ca²⁺ overload activates calcium-dependent phospholipase A2, which depletes phospholipid substrates required for GPX4 (glutathione peroxidase 4) enzymatic activity and lipid peroxidation defense. Elevated mitochondrial Ca²⁺ also promotes ferroptosis pathways through increased ROS production, which can inactivate GPX4 through oxidative modification.

Elevated mitochondrial Ca²⁺ overload activates mitochondrial phospholipase A2 and promotes electron transport chain dysfunction, leading to increased reactive oxygen species (ROS) production that directly oxidizes mitochondrial and cellular lipids to form lipid peroxides.

Mitochondrial Ca²⁺ levels directly influence presenilin-1 (PS1) localization and function at mitochondria-associated ER membranes (MAMs), where PS1 serves as a critical component of the γ-secretase complex and modulates Ca²⁺ transfer between organelles. Elevated mitochondrial Ca²⁺ can feedback-regulate PS1 activity and its positioning at MAM contact sites, which are crucial for both Aβ production and mitochondrial dysfunction in Alzheimer's disease.

Mitochondrial Ca²⁺ levels regulate PS2 localization and function at mitochondria-associated membranes (MAMs), where PS2 forms part of the IP3R-PACS2-sigma-1R complex that facilitates ER-mitochondrial Ca²⁺ transfer. Conversely, PS2 at MAMs directly influences mitochondrial Ca²⁺ uptake through its role in regulating IP3R-mediated ER Ca²⁺ release and subsequent mitochondrial sequestration.

Mitochondrial Ca²⁺ levels regulate PS2 localization and function at mitochondria-associated membranes (MAMs), where PS2 forms part of the IP3R-PACS2-sigma-1R complex that facilitates ER-mitochondrial Ca²⁺ transfer. Conversely, PS2 at MAMs directly influences mitochondrial Ca²⁺ uptake through its role in regulating IP3R-mediated ER Ca²⁺ release and subsequent mitochondrial sequestration.

Mitochondrial Ca²⁺ directly binds to the calcium release channel RYR (ryanodine receptor), which is located on the endoplasmic reticulum membrane. This binding can modulate RYR channel activity and influence calcium-induced calcium release (CICR) mechanisms critical for neuronal function.

Mitochondrial Ca²⁺ accumulation directly regulates VDAC1 channel function through allosteric mechanisms and post-translational modifications, while VDAC1 simultaneously controls mitochondrial Ca²⁺ uptake and efflux as the primary Ca²⁺ channel in the outer mitochondrial membrane. This creates a feedback regulatory relationship critical for mitochondrial calcium homeostasis.

Mitochondrial Ca²⁺ accumulation directly regulates VDAC1 channel function through allosteric mechanisms and post-translational modifications, while VDAC1 simultaneously controls mitochondrial Ca²⁺ uptake and efflux as the primary Ca²⁺ channel in the outer mitochondrial membrane. This creates a feedback regulatory relationship critical for mitochondrial calcium homeostasis.

Elevated mitochondrial Ca²⁺ overload triggers mitochondrial dysfunction, membrane depolarization, and opening of the mitochondrial permeability transition pore (mPTP), which directly causes mtDNA release into the cytoplasm. Cytoplasmic mtDNA then activates innate immune pathways (cGAS-STING) contributing to neuroinflammation in Alzheimer's disease.

Elevated mitochondrial Ca²⁺ activates mitochondrial oxidative stress pathways, generating superoxide flashes that directly damage mitochondrial DNA (mtDNA) through oxidative lesions, leading to mtDNA release into the cytosol. The dysregulation of mitochondrial calcium signaling drives mtDNA damage and subsequent cytosolic appearance of damaged mtDNA fragments.

Elevated mitochondrial Ca²⁺ activates GSK3beta through calcium/calmodulin-dependent protein kinase II (CaMKII)-mediated phosphorylation or through direct dephosphorylation of inhibitory Ser9 phosphorylation by calcineurin, a Ca²⁺-dependent phosphatase. This leads to GSK3beta hyperactivation, a key driver of tau pathology and neurodegeneration in Alzheimer's disease.

Elevated mitochondrial Ca²⁺ activates Ca²⁺/calmodulin-dependent protein kinase II (CaMKIV) and other Ca²⁺-sensitive kinases, which phosphorylate and inactivate GSK3B's inhibitor PP2A, leading to increased GSK3B phosphorylation and activation. Additionally, Ca²⁺ overload promotes mitochondrial dysfunction and oxidative stress, which impairs GSK3B inhibitory pathways (e.g., AKT signaling), allowing GSK3B to become hyperactive.

Mitochondrial Ca²⁺ influx activates ATP synthase and oxidative phosphorylation through the tricarboxylic acid cycle and electron transport chain, directly increasing mitochondrial ATP production. Ca²⁺-dependent dehydrogenases in the TCA cycle (isocitrate dehydrogenase, α-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase, malate dehydrogenase) are activated by Ca²⁺, enhancing NADH/FADH₂ generation and thus driving ATP synthesis.

Mfn2 (Mitofusin-2) is a key tethering protein that directly facilitates the formation and maintenance of mitochondria-associated ER membranes (MAM contact sites) by mediating interactions between mitochondrial and endoplasmic reticulum membranes. Mfn2 acts as a direct molecular bridge that stabilizes MAM architecture and regulates the proximity between these two organelles.

Mfn2 is a mitochondrial fusion protein that directly regulates mitochondria-associated ER membrane (MAM) tethering and connectivity. Loss or dysfunction of Mfn2 disrupts the physical and functional interactions between mitochondria and endoplasmic reticulum, reducing MAM formation and connectivity.

Mfn2 (Mitofusin-2) is a mitochondrial fusion protein that regulates mitochondrial morphology and function, including calcium handling capacity. Through its role in maintaining mitochondrial architecture and cristae organization, Mfn2 influences the efficiency of mitochondrial calcium uptake and storage via the calcium uniporter and related mechanisms.

Mfn2 (mitofusin-2) is a key regulator of mitochondrial dynamics and tethering between mitochondria and endoplasmic reticulum (ER). Loss or dysfunction of Mfn2 impairs mitochondrial calcium uptake capacity and ER-mitochondrial calcium transfer coupling, thereby promoting mitochondrial Ca²⁺ overload under stress conditions.

Mfn2 (Mitofusin 2) regulates mitochondrial-ER calcium transfer through its role in mitochondrial dynamics and ER-mitochondria tethering. Loss or dysfunction of Mfn2 impairs mitochondrial calcium uptake capacity, leading to increased ER calcium retention and ER Ca²⁺ overload.

Mfn2 (Mitofusin-2) regulates mitochondrial fusion and calcium handling by controlling mitochondrial morphology and ER-mitochondria tethering through its interaction with OPA1 and PACS2. Loss of Mfn2 impairs mitochondrial calcium uptake capacity and mitochondrial network organization, leading to increased cytoplasmic calcium accumulation and secondary mitochondrial calcium overload stress.

Mfn2 (Mitofusin-2) is a mitochondrial fusion protein that regulates mitochondrial dynamics and calcium handling. Mfn2 dysfunction or loss of function impairs mitochondrial calcium buffering capacity and promotes mitochondrial permeability transition pore (mPTP) opening through dysregulation of mitochondrial calcium homeostasis and increased ROS production.

Mfn2 (Mitofusin 2) regulates mitochondrial dynamics and outer membrane permeability, with evidence suggesting it influences VDAC (voltage-dependent anion channel) oligomerization state through effects on OPA1-mediated cristae organization and mitochondrial stress responses.

Mfn2 and GRP75 are core components of the mitochondrial protein import machinery (TIM23 complex) and physically interact to facilitate the translocation of proteins across the inner mitochondrial membrane. GRP75 acts as a motor protein in the matrix while Mfn2 anchors the translocase complex at the outer membrane, enabling direct protein-protein interaction.

Mfn2 and GRP75 are core components of the mitochondrial protein import machinery (TIM23 complex) and physically interact to facilitate the translocation of proteins across the inner mitochondrial membrane. GRP75 acts as a motor protein in the matrix while Mfn2 anchors the translocase complex at the outer membrane, enabling direct protein-protein interaction.

Mfn2 (Mitofusin-2) is a mitochondrial fusion GTPase that promotes mitochondrial fusion and maintains mitochondrial network integrity. By facilitating fusion, Mfn2 helps dilute damaged mitochondrial components and enables quality control mechanisms, thereby directly preventing or reducing accumulation of damaged mitochondria through mitophagy and fission pathways.

Mfn2 (Mitofusin 2) is a critical regulator of mitochondrial dynamics and quality control. Loss or dysfunction of Mfn2 impairs mitochondrial fusion capacity and compromises the ability of cells to selectively remove damaged mitochondria through mitophagy, as Mfn2 dysfunction disrupts the coordinated mitochondrial dynamics necessary for proper mitophagy execution.

Mfn2 (Mitofusin-2) is a mitochondrial outer membrane GTPase that mediates mitochondrial fusion and plays a critical role in mitochondrial quality control. Mfn2 directly facilitates the recognition, tethering, and engulfment of damaged mitochondria by autophagosomes, thereby promoting the formation and maturation of mitophagosomes.

Mfn2 (mitofusin-2) is a key mitochondrial fusion protein that maintains mitochondrial integrity and membrane potential. Loss or dysfunction of Mfn2 compromises mitochondrial outer membrane integrity, leading to increased release of mtDNA into the cytoplasm as escaped mtDNA.

Mfn2 (Mitofusin-2) is a key regulator of mitochondrial fusion and quality control. Loss or dysfunction of Mfn2 impairs mitochondrial dynamics and autophagy, leading to accumulation of damaged mitochondria and increased mitochondrial ROS production.

RYR (ryanodine receptor) and VDAC1 (voltage-dependent anion channel 1) are both mitochondrial calcium channels that functionally interact at mitochondrial membranes to regulate calcium dynamics. RYR mediates calcium release from the ER while VDAC1 facilitates calcium uptake into mitochondria, and their coordinated activity regulates cellular calcium homeostasis and bioenergetics.

RYR (ryanodine receptor) and VDAC1 (voltage-dependent anion channel 1) are both mitochondrial calcium channels that functionally interact at mitochondrial membranes to regulate calcium dynamics. RYR mediates calcium release from the ER while VDAC1 facilitates calcium uptake into mitochondria, and their coordinated activity regulates cellular calcium homeostasis and bioenergetics.

RYR (ryanodine receptor) activation increases intracellular Ca2+ release from the endoplasmic reticulum, which directly activates calpain proteases through Ca2+-dependent mechanisms. Elevated cytoplasmic calcium is a well-established direct activator of calpain protease activity.

RYR (ryanodine receptor) is a calcium release channel in the endoplasmic reticulum that directly participates in the formation and maintenance of mitochondrial-associated membranes (MAM contact sites) through its interaction with IP3R and VAPB at the ER-mitochondrial interface. RYR-mediated calcium signaling and its physical positioning at MAM sites regulate the structural organization and functional capacity of these membrane contact sites.

RYR (ryanodine receptor) channels on the ER regulate calcium release, and dysregulation of RYR-mediated Ca2+ signaling directly promotes excessive MAM (mitochondria-associated ER membrane) formation and hyperconnectivity as a compensatory response to restore calcium homeostasis.

RYR (ryanodine receptor) mediates calcium release from the endoplasmic reticulum, and PS1 (presenilin-1) localizes to mitochondria-associated ER membranes (MAM) where it regulates calcium transfer between ER and mitochondria. RYR activity directly influences calcium dynamics at MAM microdomains, while PS1 can modulate RYR-mediated calcium signaling through its function at these contact sites.

RYR (ryanodine receptor) mediates calcium release from the endoplasmic reticulum, and PS1 (presenilin-1) localizes to mitochondria-associated ER membranes (MAM) where it regulates calcium transfer between ER and mitochondria. RYR activity directly influences calcium dynamics at MAM microdomains, while PS1 can modulate RYR-mediated calcium signaling through its function at these contact sites.

RYR (ryanodine receptor) is a calcium release channel on the endoplasmic reticulum that, when activated, releases Ca2+ into the cytoplasm. Excessive cytoplasmic Ca2+ overload can trigger opening of the mitochondrial permeability transition pore (mPTP) through calcium-dependent mechanisms involving inner membrane depolarization and activation of proline isomerase cyclophilin D.

RYR (ryanodine receptor) activation increases intracellular Ca2+ levels, which can drive VDAC (voltage-dependent anion channel) oligomerization and pore formation in the outer mitochondrial membrane. This Ca2+-dependent VDAC oligomerization is part of the mitochondrial permeability transition pathway implicated in neuronal death in Alzheimer's disease.

RYR (ryanodine receptor) mediates calcium release from the sarcoplasmic/endoplasmic reticulum into the cytoplasm and mitochondria. Excessive calcium uptake into mitochondria through RYR-mediated signaling directly drives mitochondrial respiration and electron transport chain activity, increasing mitochondrial ROS production as a byproduct of oxidative phosphorylation.

RYR (ryanodine receptor) dysfunction leads to aberrant intracellular calcium signaling, which activates calcium-dependent kinases (particularly GSK-3β and CDK5) that phosphorylate tau at multiple sites, resulting in tau hyperphosphorylation.

RYR (ryanodine receptor) activation increases intracellular calcium levels, which activates CaMKII and other kinases that phosphorylate and activate GSK3beta. Elevated calcium flux through RYR disrupts calcium homeostasis, leading to GSK3beta activation and downstream tau hyperphosphorylation.

RYR (ryanodine receptor) activation leads to calcium release from intracellular stores, which can activate p38 MAPK through calcium-dependent signaling cascades. Elevated intracellular Ca2+ directly or indirectly phosphorylates and activates p38 MAPK through upstream kinases like MKK3/6.

RYR (ryanodine receptor) dysfunction leads to dysregulated intracellular calcium release, which promotes amyloid-beta oligomerization and accumulation through impaired proteostasis and increased oxidative stress. Excessive calcium signaling activates proteases and kinases that enhance APP processing and oligomer formation.

PS1 regulates calcium dynamics at mitochondria-associated endoplasmic reticulum membranes (MAM), and altered calcium signaling at MAMs directly activates calpain proteases through calcium-dependent mechanisms. PS1 mutations that enhance MAM function and ER-mitochondrial calcium transfer increase cytoplasmic calcium levels, which directly activates calpain.

PS1 at mitochondria-associated membranes (MAMs) regulates intracellular calcium homeostasis through IP3R-VDAC interactions; disrupted calcium signaling from MAMs impairs CaMKII activation, which requires calcium/calmodulin binding for its catalytic activity.

PS1 localizes to mitochondrial-associated membranes (MAMs) where it can interact with and modulate MCU (mitochondrial calcium uniporter) function through direct protein-protein interactions, affecting mitochondrial calcium uptake and ER-mitochondria calcium coupling.

PS1 functions at mitochondrial-associated membranes (MAMs) where it regulates calcium transfer between the ER and mitochondria; Mfn2 is a key structural component of MAM tethering complexes that PS1 directly interacts with and functionally modulates to control mitochondrial calcium uptake and bioenergetics.

PS1 localizes to mitochondrial-associated membranes (MAMs) where it forms part of the IP3R-VDAC tethering complex, facilitating calcium transfer from the ER to mitochondria. Mutant PS1 at MAMs increases the efficiency of this calcium coupling, leading to excessive mitochondrial calcium uptake and overload.

PS1 at mitochondrial-associated membranes (MAM) regulates calcium homeostasis and ER-mitochondrial signaling, which controls calcineurin-NFAT pathway activation. Dysregulated calcium flux through PS1 at MAM can increase calcineurin activity, leading to NFAT dephosphorylation and nuclear translocation.

PS1 localizes to mitochondria-associated membranes (MAMs) where it directly interacts with SERCA2b, modulating calcium transfer between the endoplasmic reticulum and mitochondria. PS1 at MAMs regulates SERCA activity through protein-protein interactions that affect ER calcium handling and mitochondrial calcium uptake.

PS1 is a component of the γ-secretase complex localized to mitochondria-associated membranes (MAMs), where it regulates calcium signaling and ER-mitochondrial dynamics. Sigma-1R is an MAM-resident protein that modulates ER-mitochondrial coupling and calcium homeostasis, suggesting PS1 MAM complexes directly regulate Sigma-1R function through their shared MAM localization and calcium signaling roles.

PS1 localizes to mitochondrial-associated membranes (MAMs) where it interacts with VDAC1, a voltage-dependent anion channel in the outer mitochondrial membrane, to regulate calcium transfer between ER and mitochondria. PS1-containing MAM complexes directly influence VDAC1 function and activity in calcium signaling.

PS1 localizes to mitochondrial-associated membranes (MAMs) where it facilitates Ca2+ transfer from the ER to mitochondria through interaction with IP3R and VAPB, directly increasing mitochondrial Ca2+ uptake and causing mitochondrial calcium overload.

PS1 localizes to mitochondria-associated membranes (MAMs) where it facilitates Ca2+ transfer from the ER to mitochondria, leading to mitochondrial calcium overload and increased ROS production through oxidative phosphorylation.

PS1 localizes to mitochondrial-associated membranes (MAM) where it regulates calcium transfer and interacts with IP3R and VDAC, and aberrant PS1 at MAM promotes mitochondrial calcium overload and mPTP opening through altered ER-mitochondrial calcium dynamics.

PS1 localizes to mitochondria-associated membranes (MAMs) where it facilitates calcium transfer from the ER to mitochondria, and this PS1 function at MAMs directly promotes BACE1 activity and γ-secretase cleavage of APP, thereby increasing Aβ production.

PS1 localizes to mitochondria-associated membranes (MAMs) where it facilitates calcium transfer between the ER and mitochondria; dysregulated MAM function and calcium signaling can activate cellular stress responses that upregulate BACE1 expression through ER stress pathways (ATF4, CHOP) and calcium-dependent signaling cascades.

PS1 localizes to mitochondria-associated membranes (MAMs) where it facilitates calcium transfer and interacts with APP, promoting amyloidogenic cleavage of APP by enhancing β-secretase (BACE1) activity at these membrane contact sites.

PS2 (presenilin-2) is an integral component of mitochondrial-associated ER membranes (MAMs) where it facilitates calcium transfer between the ER and mitochondria. MCU (mitochondrial calcium uniporter) is the primary calcium influx channel in the mitochondrial inner membrane, and PS2 at MAMs directly influences calcium availability and uptake through MCU.

PS2 localizes to mitochondria-associated membranes (MAMs) where it regulates calcium transfer and mitochondrial dynamics. Mfn2 is a key MAM tethering protein that directly interacts with ER-mitochondrial contact sites, and PS2-dependent alterations in MAM composition and function can regulate Mfn2 expression or activity.

PS2 (presenilin-2) localizes to mitochondria-associated membranes (MAMs) where it facilitates Ca²⁺ transfer from the endoplasmic reticulum to mitochondria through direct protein-protein interactions. Pathogenic PS2 mutations enhance this Ca²⁺ transfer function, leading to mitochondrial Ca²⁺ overload.

PS2 (Presenilin-2) localizes to mitochondrial-associated membranes (MAMs) where it interacts with RYR (Ryanodine Receptor) calcium channels, enhancing calcium transfer from the endoplasmic reticulum to mitochondria. Pathogenic PS2 mutations at MAMs increase abnormal calcium signaling through RYR, contributing to mitochondrial dysfunction and neurodegeneration in Alzheimer's disease.

PS2 (presenilin-2) localizes to mitochondria-associated membranes (MAMs) where it directly interacts with and regulates SERCA (sarcoplasmic/endoplasmic reticulum calcium ATPase) function, modulating calcium homeostasis at ER-mitochondrial contact sites.

PS2 (presenilin-2) localizes to mitochondrial-associated membranes (MAMs) where it physically interacts with and modulates VDAC1, a key component of the mitochondrial permeability transition pore, thereby increasing VDAC1 activity and promoting calcium transfer between ER and mitochondria.

PS2 (presenilin-2) is a catalytic component of the γ-secretase complex localized to mitochondrial-associated membranes (MAMs). PS2 at MAMs directly facilitates APP processing through γ-secretase activity, which generates amyloidogenic C-terminal fragments (CTFs) that are further processed to produce Aβ peptides.

PS2 (Presenilin-2) is a catalytic component of the γ-secretase complex localized at mitochondria-associated membranes (MAMs), where it directly cleaves APP to generate Aβ peptides. MAM dysfunction or PS2 alterations at these sites can modulate the efficiency of APP processing and subsequent Aβ production.

PS2 (presenilin-2) localizes to mitochondria-associated membranes (MAMs) where it facilitates IP3R-mediated Ca2+ transfer from the ER to mitochondria. Dysregulation of PS2 at MAMs increases mitochondrial Ca2+ uptake capacity, leading to Ca2+ overload.

PS2 (presenilin-2) localizes to mitochondrial-associated membranes (MAMs) where it functions in calcium signaling between the ER and mitochondria. Dysregulation of PS2 at MAMs impairs calcium homeostasis, leading to mitochondrial calcium overload and increased ROS production through enhanced oxidative phosphorylation and activation of the permeability transition pore.

PS2 (presenilin-2) localizes to mitochondrial-associated membranes (MAMs) where it modulates calcium transfer between ER and mitochondria, and dysregulated calcium homeostasis leads to opening of the mitochondrial permeability transition pore (mPTP). PS2 mutations and aberrant PS2 function at MAMs increase mitochondrial calcium overload, which directly triggers mPTP opening.

PS2 (presenilin-2) localizes to mitochondria-associated membranes (MAMs) where it modulates calcium transfer between the endoplasmic reticulum and mitochondria, thereby regulating intracellular calcium levels that directly activate CaMKII. Dysregulation of MAM function by PS2 mutations impairs calcium signaling and CaMKII activation.

PS2 localizes to mitochondria-associated membranes (MAMs) where it forms part of the calcium release machinery. PS2 at MAMs facilitates IP3R-mediated calcium transfer from the ER to mitochondria, leading to increased cytosolic Ca²⁺ levels during the process of calcium mobilization and refilling of intracellular stores.

PS2 (presenilin-2) localizes to mitochondria-associated membranes (MAMs) where it facilitates calcium transfer from ER to mitochondria; excessive calcium accumulation in mitochondria activates calpains, calcium-dependent proteases that contribute to neurodegeneration.

PS2 FAD mutations alter presenilin-2 function, which is a critical component of the γ-secretase complex and interacts with calcium-handling proteins at mitochondrial-associated ER membranes (MAMs). Mutant PS2 directly disrupts proper MAM formation and function through altered protein-protein interactions and calcium signaling at these contact sites.

PS2 FAD mutations alter presenilin-2 function, leading to dysregulated calcium signaling and increased mitochondria-associated membrane (MAM) contacts between the endoplasmic reticulum and mitochondria. This enhanced MAM connectivity facilitates pathological calcium transfer and contributes to neuronal dysfunction and neurodegeneration.

PS2 FAD mutations alter the function of presenilin-2, a component of the γ-secretase complex and an important regulator of intracellular calcium homeostasis. These mutations impair PS2's role in controlling calcium release from the endoplasmic reticulum and calcium uptake into mitochondria, leading to dysregulated mitochondrial calcium accumulation and overload.

PS2 FAD mutations impair presenilin-2 function within the presenilin-containing γ-secretase complex, leading to altered calcium homeostasis through disruption of IP3 receptor signaling and reduced SERCA2b pump activity in the endoplasmic reticulum.

PS2 FAD mutations alter presenilin-2 function in the endoplasmic reticulum, disrupting IP3 receptor and ryanodine receptor-mediated calcium release, leading to abnormal ER Ca²⁺ store depletion and dysregulated intracellular calcium homeostasis.

PS2 FAD mutations impair the function of presenilin-2 as part of the γ-secretase complex and disrupt calcium homeostasis, leading to increased cytosolic Ca²⁺ through enhanced IP3-receptor mediated calcium release from the endoplasmic reticulum and reduced ER-to-cytosol calcium buffering capacity.

PS2 FAD mutations alter presenilin-2 function, leading to enhanced IP3R-mediated calcium signaling through disrupted intramembrane proteolysis and altered ER calcium handling. Mutant PS2 directly interacts with IP3R as part of the IP3R-STIM1-SOCE complex, increasing IP3R activity and ER calcium release.

PS2 FAD mutations alter the catalytic activity and substrate specificity of the γ-secretase complex, which directly cleaves the amyloid precursor protein (APP) to generate Aβ peptides. These mutations shift the cleavage pattern toward longer, more amyloidogenic Aβ42 species.

PS2 FAD mutations alter presenilin-2 function in the endoplasmic reticulum, disrupting calcium homeostasis and leading to increased calcium release into mitochondria, causing mitochondrial Ca2+ overload. This occurs through impaired IP3 receptor signaling and abnormal ER-mitochondrial calcium transfer.

PS2 FAD mutations impair mitochondrial calcium handling and respiratory chain function through altered presenilin-2 protein conformation, leading to increased electron transport chain dysfunction and subsequently elevated mitochondrial ROS production.

PS2 FAD mutations alter presenilin-2 function, leading to dysregulation of intracellular calcium homeostasis, which directly increases CaMKII activation through increased Ca2+ influx and sustained elevation of intracellular calcium levels.

PS2 FAD mutations enhance presenilin-2 function in the γ-secretase complex, leading to increased intracellular calcium dysregulation and enhanced IP3R-mediated calcium signaling, which activates calcineurin-dependent NFAT dephosphorylation and nuclear translocation.

PS1 FAD mutations alter presenilin-1 protein function in the ER membrane, leading to dysregulation of IP3 receptors and ryanodine receptors that control ER Ca²⁺ release, resulting in abnormal cytoplasmic Ca²⁺ accumulation and ER Ca²⁺ store depletion/overload cycles.

PS1 FAD mutations alter the function of presenilin-1, a key component of the γ-secretase complex and an important regulator of ER calcium homeostasis through its role in IP3 receptor signaling and SERCA pump regulation, leading to dysregulation of ER Ca²⁺ store release and refilling capacity.

PS1 FAD mutations enhance IP3R-mediated calcium release from the endoplasmic reticulum by increasing the sensitivity and activity of IP3R channels, leading to dysregulated intracellular calcium signaling.

PS1 FAD mutations alter calcium handling and ER-mitochondrial coupling by disrupting presenilin-1's role in regulating IP3 receptor function and ER calcium release, which directly compromises the structural and functional integrity of mitochondria-associated ER membranes (MAMs). This disruption impairs calcium transfer at MAM contact sites, a critical function for cellular bioenergetics and autophagy.

PS1 FAD mutations alter PS1 function within mitochondrial-associated membranes (MAMs), leading to enhanced physical contacts between the endoplasmic reticulum and mitochondria through dysregulated calcium handling and altered protein-protein interactions at MAM contact sites.

PS1 FAD mutations alter the function of presenilin-1, a component of the γ-secretase complex and a critical regulator of IP3 receptor-mediated calcium release from the endoplasmic reticulum. This leads to increased ER-mitochondrial calcium transfer and elevated mitochondrial calcium uptake through the VDAC-MCU pathway.

PS1 FAD mutations alter presenilin-1 function within the endoplasmic reticulum (ER), leading to dysregulation of ER-mitochondrial calcium handling and increased mitochondrial calcium uptake through IP3R and RyR-mediated calcium release from ER stores.

PS1 FAD mutations alter presenilin-1 function, leading to dysregulation of intracellular calcium homeostasis through aberrant RYR (ryanodine receptor) calcium release. The mutant PS1 protein enhances calcium mobilization from intracellular stores via RYR-mediated pathways, causing excessive calcium signaling implicated in neurodegeneration.

PS1 FAD mutations impair calcium homeostasis by disrupting SERCA (Sarcoplasmic/Endoplasmic Reticulum Calcium ATPase) function, leading to reduced calcium reuptake into the ER and increased cytoplasmic calcium levels. This calcium dysregulation is a well-established consequence of PS1 mutations and contributes to neurodegeneration through excitotoxicity and mitochondrial dysfunction.

PS1 FAD mutations alter calcium homeostasis and mitochondrial function by disrupting presenilin-1's role in regulating ER-mitochondria calcium transfer, leading to increased VDAC1 opening and enhanced mitochondrial calcium uptake as a compensatory response.

PS1 FAD mutations impair the function of PS1 as a component of the γ-secretase complex and disrupt its role in regulating calcium homeostasis, leading to increased IP3R-mediated calcium release from the endoplasmic reticulum and subsequent mitochondrial calcium overload through MICU1/EMRE calcium uptake channels.

PS1 FAD mutations impair the function of mitochondrial calcium handling and ATP synthase, leading to increased mitochondrial ROS generation. This occurs through altered SERCA2 regulation and compromised oxidative phosphorylation efficiency.

PS1 FAD mutations alter calcium handling and mitochondrial function by disrupting presenilin-1's role in regulating IP3 receptors and SERCA pumps, leading to increased cytoplasmic calcium that drives mitochondrial permeability transition pore (mPTP) opening. This increased mPTP opening causes mitochondrial dysfunction, calcium overload in mitochondria, and release of pro-apoptotic factors.

PS1 FAD mutations alter calcium homeostasis and mitochondrial function, leading to increased formation of voltage-dependent anion channel (VDAC) oligomers in the outer mitochondrial membrane. Dysregulated calcium signaling through PS1 mutants promotes VDAC oligomerization and pore opening, facilitating cytochrome c release and apoptosis.

PS1 FAD mutations alter the catalytic activity and substrate specificity of the γ-secretase complex, which directly cleaves amyloid precursor protein (APP) to generate Aβ peptides. Mutant PS1 shifts γ-secretase cleavage patterns to increase production of longer, more amyloidogenic Aβ42 species.

PS1 FAD mutations alter γ-secretase activity and calcium homeostasis, which dysregulates GSK-3β and other kinases responsible for tau phosphorylation at multiple epitopes. This leads to increased tau hyperphosphorylation through both direct kinase activation and indirect effects on phosphatase activity.

PS1 FAD mutations alter presenilin-1 function, leading to increased intracellular calcium dysregulation and ER stress, which activates calpain proteases. Activated calpains then contribute to neurodegeneration through excessive proteolysis of cytoskeletal and synaptic proteins.

PS1 FAD mutations alter calcium homeostasis by increasing IP3R and ryanodine receptor-mediated calcium release from the ER, leading to elevated intracellular calcium levels that hyperactivate CaMKII. This CaMKII hyperactivation contributes to synaptic dysfunction and neurodegeneration in AD pathology.

PS1 FAD mutations alter calcium homeostasis by increasing ER calcium leak and affecting SERCA function, which leads to sustained elevation of cytoplasmic calcium that activates calcineurin, a calcium/calmodulin-dependent phosphatase. This calcium dysregulation is a well-established consequence of presenilin-1 mutations that directly engages calcineurin as a downstream effector.

PS1 FAD mutations enhance calcium release from intracellular stores through altered presenilin-1 function, leading to sustained elevation of cytoplasmic calcium that activates calcineurin. Calcineurin dephosphorylates NFAT, promoting its nuclear translocation and transcriptional activity.

SERCA (Sarcoplasmic/Endoplasmic Reticulum Ca2+-ATPase) and RYR (Ryanodine Receptor) are functionally coupled proteins in ER calcium handling. SERCA pumps Ca2+ into the ER lumen, while RYR releases Ca2+ from ER stores; their activities are interdependent in maintaining calcium homeostasis. In Alzheimer's disease, both proteins are dysregulated, contributing to abnormal calcium signaling and neurodegeneration.

SERCA (Sarcoplasmic/Endoplasmic Reticulum Ca2+-ATPase) and RYR (Ryanodine Receptor) are functionally coupled proteins in ER calcium handling. SERCA pumps Ca2+ into the ER lumen, while RYR releases Ca2+ from ER stores; their activities are interdependent in maintaining calcium homeostasis. In Alzheimer's disease, both proteins are dysregulated, contributing to abnormal calcium signaling and neurodegeneration.

VDAC1 is the primary mitochondrial outer membrane channel for Ca2+ transport; dysregulation or increased VDAC1 activity/opening allows excessive Ca2+ influx into mitochondria, directly causing Ca2+ overload.

VDAC1 (voltage-dependent anion channel 1) in the outer mitochondrial membrane regulates iron homeostasis by controlling mitochondrial calcium and metabolic signaling, which influences ferritin iron storage and ferroptosis-related iron dynamics. VDAC1 dysfunction impairs mitochondrial iron management, affecting ferritin's role in iron detoxification and storage.

VDAC1 (Voltage-Dependent Anion Channel 1) regulates mitochondrial iron homeostasis by controlling the transport of iron and iron-containing metabolites across the outer mitochondrial membrane, thereby influencing the labile iron pool within mitochondria.

VDAC1 (voltage-dependent anion channel 1) regulates mitochondrial calcium influx and ROS production; excessive calcium and ROS accumulation promotes lipid peroxidation of mitochondrial membranes. In Alzheimer's disease pathology, VDAC1 dysfunction and overactivation exacerbates mitochondrial oxidative stress, leading to increased lipid peroxide formation.

VDAC1 (voltage-dependent anion channel 1) mediates mitochondrial calcium overload and cytochrome c release when dysregulated, leading to increased ROS production and oxidative stress. VDAC1 dysfunction impairs mitochondrial ATP production and electron transport chain efficiency, directly amplifying oxidative phosphorylation byproducts.

VDAC1 and GRP75 form a direct physical complex at the mitochondrial outer membrane, with GRP75 acting as a chaperone that regulates VDAC1 channel properties and prevents its oligomerization. This interaction is essential for proper mitochondrial calcium handling and ATP transport.

VDAC1 and GRP75 form a direct physical complex at the mitochondrial outer membrane, with GRP75 acting as a chaperone that regulates VDAC1 channel properties and prevents its oligomerization. This interaction is essential for proper mitochondrial calcium handling and ATP transport.

VDAC1 and IP3R are known to physically interact at mitochondria-ER contact sites (MAMs), forming a complex that regulates calcium transfer between the endoplasmic reticulum and mitochondria. This direct protein-protein binding modulates calcium signaling dynamics critical for cellular bioenergetics and neuronal function.

VDAC1 and IP3R are known to physically interact at mitochondria-ER contact sites (MAMs), forming a complex that regulates calcium transfer between the endoplasmic reticulum and mitochondria. This direct protein-protein binding modulates calcium signaling dynamics critical for cellular bioenergetics and neuronal function.

VDAC1 and Mfn2 are both critical components of mitochondrial dynamics and function. VDAC1 forms the outer membrane channel regulating metabolite/ion transport, while Mfn2 mediates mitochondrial fusion and also localizes to ER-mitochondrial contact sites. They functionally interact in controlling mitochondrial morphology, calcium dynamics, and bioenergetics - processes implicated in Alzheimer's pathology.

VDAC1 and Mfn2 are both critical components of mitochondrial dynamics and function. VDAC1 forms the outer membrane channel regulating metabolite/ion transport, while Mfn2 mediates mitochondrial fusion and also localizes to ER-mitochondrial contact sites. They functionally interact in controlling mitochondrial morphology, calcium dynamics, and bioenergetics - processes implicated in Alzheimer's pathology.

VDAC1 (voltage-dependent anion channel 1) is the primary mitochondrial outer membrane channel through which Ca²⁺ ions enter the mitochondrial intermembrane space. Dysregulation or excessive opening of VDAC1 directly facilitates pathological Ca²⁺ influx into mitochondria, leading to mitochondrial Ca²⁺ overload, which is a key pathogenic mechanism in Alzheimer's disease neurodegeneration.

Amyloid-beta plaques are the pathological substrate that accumulates in the brain and is detected by amyloid PET imaging. The presence and burden of Abeta plaques directly produces the PET signal measured as SUVR (standardized uptake value ratio).

Amyloid-beta accumulation (measured by PET SUVR) activates microglia through pattern recognition receptors (TLRs, CD36), which upregulates phagocytic machinery and enhances microglial phagocytosis of amyloid-beta plaques and cellular debris.

Amyloid-beta pathology triggers tau hyperphosphorylation and aggregation through activation of kinases and disruption of tau phosphatase activity, leading to increased tau PET signal. This represents a key pathogenic cascade in Alzheimer's disease where amyloid burden drives downstream tau pathology.

Sigma-1R (S1R) is a ligand-operated chaperone protein localized at mitochondrial-associated endoplasmic reticulum membranes (MAMs) that regulates calcium transfer from the ER to mitochondria. S1R activation decreases mitochondrial Ca²⁺ overload by modulating IP3 receptor function and stabilizing MAM integrity, thereby reducing excessive calcium influx into mitochondria.

Sigma-1 receptor (Sigma1R) is a ligand-regulated chaperone protein located at mitochondrial-associated endoplasmic reticulum membranes that regulates mitochondrial calcium homeostasis and oxidative stress. Sigma1R activation promotes mitochondrial function and reduces ROS production through stabilization of mitochondrial calcium handling and electron transport chain efficiency.

Sigma-1 receptor (Sigma1R) acts as a mitochondrial chaperone protein that stabilizes mitochondrial function and prevents opening of the mitochondrial permeability transition pore (mPTP). Activation or upregulation of Sigma1R inhibits mPTP opening by maintaining mitochondrial membrane potential and reducing calcium overload.

Sigma-1R (S1R) is a ligand-operated chaperone protein localized at mitochondrial-associated endoplasmic reticulum membranes (MAMs) that directly interacts with and stabilizes VDAC1, preventing pathological VDAC oligomerization and the formation of the permeability transition pore complex.

Sigma-1 receptor (Sigma1R) activation can enhance neuroprotective signaling pathways that promote NFAT nuclear translocation and transcriptional activity through calcium signaling and protein kinase C pathways. NFAT nuclear localization is calcium-dependent, and Sigma1R modulation of intracellular calcium dynamics and ER-mitochondrial interactions can facilitate NFAT dephosphorylation and nuclear import.

Sigma-1 receptor (Sigma1R) functions as a ligand-operated chaperone at mitochondria-associated endoplasmic reticulum membranes (MAMs) and directly modulates autophagy initiation and autophagosome formation through IP3R signaling and calcium homeostasis regulation.

Sigma-1R (σ1R) functions as a ligand-operated chaperone at the endoplasmic reticulum-mitochondrial interface and directly regulates autophagy flux through stabilization of IP3 receptors and modulation of calcium homeostasis, which are critical for autophagosome formation and maturation.

Sigma-1 receptor (Sigma1R) is a chaperone protein that localizes to mitochondria-associated ER membranes and promotes mitochondrial function and quality control. Loss or reduced function of Sigma1R impairs mitochondrial homeostasis and reduces autophagic clearance of damaged mitochondria (mitophagy), leading to accumulation of dysfunctional mitochondria.

Sigma-1R (S1R) functions as a molecular chaperone at the ER-mitochondrial interface and regulates autophagy/mitophagy. Activation or proper function of Sigma-1R promotes clearance of misfolded proteins and damaged organelles, thereby reducing cargo accumulation; conversely, Sigma-1R dysfunction or downregulation impairs these clearance mechanisms, leading to increased cargo accumulation.

Sigma1R acts as a neuroprotective receptor that inhibits NLRP3 inflammasome activation through modulation of mitochondrial function and reduction of ROS production, thereby suppressing neuroinflammation in Alzheimer's disease pathology.

Sigma-1 receptor (Sigma1R) activation suppresses microglial activation and reduces pro-inflammatory cytokine production through modulation of intracellular calcium signaling and NF-κB pathway inhibition. Sigma1R ligands attenuate neuroinflammatory responses by stabilizing mitochondrial function and reducing oxidative stress-induced microglial activation.

Sigma-1 receptor (Sig1R) acts as a ligand-operated chaperone that stabilizes and enhances the activity of Nrf2 through direct protein-protein interactions, facilitating Nrf2's translocation to the nucleus and activation of antioxidant response elements.

Sigma-1R activation enhances cellular antioxidant defenses by promoting glutathione synthesis and reducing oxidative stress through mitochondrial protection and ER-mitochondrial signaling. Sigma-1R ligands have been shown to upregulate expression of gamma-glutamylcysteine synthetase (GCLC), a rate-limiting enzyme in glutathione biosynthesis.

Sigma-1 receptor (Sigma1R) activation enhances cellular antioxidant defenses by promoting glutathione (GSH) synthesis and reducing its oxidative consumption, thereby increasing intracellular GSH levels. This occurs through Sigma1R's cytoprotective signaling that stabilizes mitochondrial function and reduces oxidative stress, which would otherwise deplete GSH.

Sigma-1 receptor (Sigma1R) is a neuroprotective chaperone protein that regulates mitochondrial function and oxidative stress responses. Sigma1R activation decreases lipid peroxidation by stabilizing mitochondrial membrane integrity and enhancing antioxidant defenses, thereby reducing the production of lipid peroxides.

APOE4 genotype is a major genetic risk factor for Alzheimer's disease and increases amyloid-beta accumulation and neuroinflammation, which directly elevates the risk of amyloid-related imaging abnormalities (ARIA-E, amyloid-related imaging abnormality with edema). APOE4 carriers show higher cerebral amyloid burden, making them more susceptible to ARIA-E when exposed to anti-amyloid monoclonal antibodies or in the disease course.

APOE4 genotype increases risk of amyloid-related imaging abnormalities (ARIA), including ARIA-H microhemorrhages, through impaired cerebral amyloid angiopathy (CAA) clearance and compromised blood-brain barrier integrity. APOE4 carriers show elevated susceptibility to microhemorrhagic events, particularly in the context of anti-amyloid immunotherapy, due to reduced vascular stability and increased amyloid burden in vessel walls.

APOE4 genotype increases risk of amyloid-related imaging abnormalities (ARIA), including ARIA-H (microhemorrhages and superficial siderosis), through enhanced amyloid-beta accumulation and cerebral amyloid angiopathy that compromises blood-brain barrier integrity. Superficial siderosis specifically results from chronic microhemorrhages depositing hemosiderin in the cortical and leptomeningeal spaces, a downstream consequence of APOE4-driven vascular amyloid pathology.

APOE4 carriers demonstrate increased susceptibility to amyloid-related imaging abnormalities (ARIA), and this genetic risk factor directly increases the likelihood of developing symptomatic ARIA manifestations including amyloid-beta-related microhemorrhages (ARIA-H) and amyloid-related transient swelling (ARIA-E).

APOE4 carriers have increased risk of amyloid-related imaging abnormalities (ARIA) during anti-amyloid immunotherapy, which can trigger neuroinflammation and neuronal damage that elevates cerebrospinal fluid neurofilament light chain (NfL) as a marker of axonal injury. APOE4-associated neurodegeneration and blood-brain barrier dysfunction also independently increase CSF NfL levels.

ARIA (Amyloid-Related Imaging Abnormalities) Symptomatic represents clinical manifestations of amyloid-β and tau-related vascular pathology, which can directly cause ARIA-H (Amyloid-Related Imaging Abnormalities-Hemorrhage) including superficial siderosis through rupture of affected cerebral vessels and microhemorrhages.

ARIA (amyloid-related imaging abnormalities) symptomatic events represent acute cerebral microhemorrhages and microinfarcts that cause focal neuronal injury and axonal damage, which directly elevates cerebrospinal fluid neurofilament light chain (NfL) as a marker of axonal degeneration.

ARIA (Amyloid-Related Imaging Abnormalities) represent pathological brain changes induced by anti-amyloid monoclonal antibodies (aducanumab, lecanemab, donanemab). ARIA symptomatic manifestations occur when ARIA lesions become clinically evident, and infusion reactions are known adverse events associated with monoclonal antibody infusions, with potential mechanistic overlap through immune activation and endothelial inflammation triggered by the same biological process.

ARIA (Amyloid-Related Imaging Abnormalities) represent pathological brain changes from anti-amyloid immunotherapies that trigger neuroinflammatory responses, including microglial activation and cytokine production. These imaging-detectable lesions (microhemorrhages and microinfarcts) are direct consequences of treatment-induced vascular and parenchymal inflammation.

ARIA-H microhemorrhages disrupt the blood-brain barrier and create a local inflammatory microenvironment that recruits and facilitates infiltration of peripheral immune cells (monocytes, macrophages, neutrophils) into the brain parenchyma. The damaged vasculature and exposed extracellular matrix provide chemotactic signals and reduced barrier resistance that promote immune cell extravasation.

Microhemorrhages cause mechanical disruption and create a local inflammatory microenvironment with reactive oxygen species and immune cell infiltration that directly damages blood-brain barrier tight junction proteins (claudins, occludin, ZO-1). The hemorrhagic disruption of endothelial integrity and subsequent neuroinflammation compromises tight junction protein expression and localization.

ARIA-H microhemorrhages release iron from hemoglobin and red blood cells into brain parenchyma, where ferrous iron catalyzes the Fenton reaction, generating highly reactive hydroxyl radicals that cause oxidative damage.

Microhemorrhages cause local tissue damage and blood extravasation, exposing lipids to iron and reactive oxygen species from hemoglobin breakdown, which catalyzes lipid peroxidation through Fenton chemistry. The resulting lipid peroxides contribute to neuroinflammation and neuronal injury in the context of cerebral amyloid angiopathy-related hemorrhages in Alzheimer's disease.

ARIA-H microhemorrhages involve blood-brain barrier disruption and extravasation of blood products, including iron-containing hemoglobin and red blood cells, which directly contribute to total brain iron accumulation. Conversely, elevated brain iron can promote oxidative stress and vascular dysfunction, increasing microhemorrhage risk.

ARIA-H microhemorrhages involve blood-brain barrier disruption and extravasation of blood products, including iron-containing hemoglobin and red blood cells, which directly contribute to total brain iron accumulation. Conversely, elevated brain iron can promote oxidative stress and vascular dysfunction, increasing microhemorrhage risk.

ARIA-H microhemorrhages disrupt the blood-brain barrier and trigger local tissue damage, which activates microglia and astrocytes, leading to increased production of pro-inflammatory cytokines and chemokines. The hemorrhagic lesion site becomes a focal point of neuroinflammatory cascade activation.

ARIA-H microhemorrhages cause blood-brain barrier disruption and expose brain parenchyma to peripheral immune signals, triggering Type I interferon responses through pattern recognition receptor activation by damage-associated molecular patterns (DAMPs) and infiltrating immune cells.
